description: A small, lively barb from Sumatra with a grid-like pattern of dark scale edges on a silver-gold body — the 'checker' of its common name. Males develop richer coloration with age. Peaceful, undemanding and easy to breed, it is a great starter barb that suits most small community setups.
behavior: A constantly active schooler that explores the full water column; males spar and display to each other in brief fin-spreading chases but rarely cause harm. Gets more active and natural-looking in larger groups.
dimorphism: Males develop richer golden-red coloration and are slimmer; females are plainer and rounder, especially when full of eggs.

description: A striking blackwater barb with five bold black vertical bands on a coppery-gold body. Originates from peat-swamp streams in Borneo and requires soft, acidic water — it does not thrive in the hard alkaline conditions many community tanks provide. In correct water, a lively and peaceful schooler.
behavior: A tight schooler that moves in formation through the mid-water; settles best in a densely planted tank with gentle flow and dim lighting that mimics its peat-swamp habitat. Peaceful and harmless to all but the smallest invertebrates.
dimorphism: Females are rounder and fuller-bodied; males are slimmer and may show brighter banding.

description: The largest of the commonly kept danios — a streamlined, fast-swimming schooler with horizontal blue and yellow-gold stripes along the flanks. Lively and hardy, it makes an impressive display fish in a large school but can bother smaller, slower companions with its boisterous swimming and competitive feeding.
behavior: An energetic open-water schooler that moves fast and covers the full length of the tank; needs plenty of swimming space. Can outcompete slower fish at feeding time. Groups of six or more are calmer and more cohesive than smaller numbers.
dimorphism: Females are deeper-bodied and fuller; males are slimmer with brighter colour during spawning.

description: A delicate, iridescent danio with a faint pinkish or blue sheen across the body and fine horizontal streaks along the flanks — the 'pearl' of its common name becomes most vivid in a good light. Peaceful, active, and extremely hardy. Tolerates a wider temperature range than most tropical danios, suiting a slightly cooler setup.
behavior: An active mid-water to surface schooler that darts constantly across the tank; females tend to be rounder and slightly calmer than the faster-moving males. Strong jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential.
dimorphism: Females are rounder and deeper-bodied; males are slimmer with a brighter iridescent sheen, particularly during spawning.

description: One of the smallest aquarium fish in regular trade — a micro-rasbora barely 2 cm long, with a bold black spot at the base of the tail and a dash of brilliant red on the body that together resemble an exclamation mark. Completely peaceful but extremely vulnerable to any tankmate large enough to swallow it; best kept as a species-only tank or with other nano fish of similar size.
behavior: Schools loosely in the mid-water; most vivid and active in a large group of 10 or more. Needs very small food items — standard flake is too large; use micro-pellets, infusoria, or baby brine shrimp. Sensitive to water quality and parameter swings.
dimorphism: Females are rounder and slightly larger; males are more intensely coloured.

description: A petite, charming schooling fish closely related to the harlequin rasbora but smaller and with a more elongated, lambchop-shaped dark patch on the hind body — the source of both common names. Thrives in a planted, slightly acidic, warm setup with gentle filtration. One of the most rewarding micro-community fish when kept in a large group.
behavior: A tight, graceful schooler that drifts through the mid-water in a synchronized group; most active and colourful in a well-planted tank with gentle flow. Peaceful with all suitably-sized tank mates. Breed in small caves or under broad leaves.
dimorphism: Females are rounder and more uniformly coloured; males are slimmer with a more vibrant body patch.

description: A large, streamlined rasbora named for its deeply forked tail with bold black-and-white tips that open and close as it swims — the scissor motion is most visible in a school. Peaceful, active and hardy; needs a long, open tank to accommodate its restless schooling and surface-feeding habits.
behavior: A fast, surface-hugging schooler that moves in tight formation; can be skittish and bolt into the glass if suddenly startled, so needs a calm environment. Strong jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential. Keep six or more for natural schooling behavior.
dimorphism: Females are fuller-bodied and rounder; males are slightly slimmer.

description: A striking aquarium classic — jet-black body with a vivid red tail, resembling a miniature shark in silhouette. Critically endangered in the wild; almost all trade fish are captive-bred. Peaceful as a juvenile but becomes increasingly territorial and aggressive as it matures, especially toward fish of similar shape or colour. Best kept as the only bottom-dwelling shark-type fish in the tank.
behavior: Patrols a defined territory across the bottom, chasing intruders vigorously; nocturnal but active during the day once settled. Becomes increasingly intolerant of similar-looking species with age. Good jumper — a lid is recommended.
dimorphism: Not easily sexable externally; females may be slightly fuller-bodied.

description: A sleek dark-grey cyprinid with red-orange fins — the 'rainbow' of its name. Almost identical in behaviour to the redtail black shark; peaceful as a juvenile but steadily more territorial as it matures. Not suitable for grouping with other shark-type fish or similar-looking species. Best kept singly with robust, unrelated tankmates.
behavior: Grazes the substrate and rocks for algae and biofilm; establishes a territory that it defends aggressively from similar-looking species. Juveniles are more peaceful. Requires excellent filtration — sensitive to organic waste.
dimorphism: Females may be slightly rounder and fuller-bodied; males develop small black lines on the caudal fin.

description: A streamlined, torpedo-shaped cyprinid with a broad dark stripe bordered by gold, contrasting black-edged fins, and a pointed snout. Often sold as an algae eater but juveniles eat algae while adults abandon this and become territorial. Frequently confused with the true Siamese algae eater (Crossocheilus oblongus), which is the better algae-control choice.
behavior: Grazes biofilm from rocks and plants as a juvenile; adults shift to a more omnivorous diet and become increasingly territorial toward fish with similar body shapes, chasing them relentlessly. Best kept singly with unrelated, robust tankmates.
dimorphism: Females are rounder-bodied; males are slimmer. Difficult to sex externally.

description: The most effective algae-eating fish in the hobby and the only species that reliably consumes black beard algae. A sleek silver fish with a clean black stripe from snout to tail tip. Peaceful and undemanding, though it grows to 15 cm — a medium-sized fish for a species often added to nano tanks. Frequently confused with the flying fox, which is less effective and more aggressive.
behavior: Actively grazes algae from all surfaces throughout its life; can be kept alone or in small groups, though males may skirmish in very small tanks. Becomes more omnivorous with age but continues algae-grazing. Active swimmer — not a sedentary fish.
dimorphism: Not reliably sexable externally; females may be slightly fuller-bodied.

description: A popular algae eater as a juvenile — active, gold-striped and effective at grazing — but its reputation falls apart with age. Adults abandon algae-eating, grow to 28 cm, become aggressive, and develop a destructive habit of sucking the protective slime coat from other fish. A better long-term choice for algae control is the Siamese algae eater (Crossocheilus oblongus).
behavior: Active sucker-mouthed fish that grazes rocks and glass as a juvenile; adults become increasingly territorial and will chase, ram, and attach themselves to the flanks of other fish to rasp their slime coat. Best kept singly with robust, unrelated tankmates.
dimorphism: Not reliably sexable externally; females may be slightly rounder.

description: A widespread and robust labyrinth fish sold in a range of colour forms — the wild blue-grey, gold, opaline/marbled and platinum morphs are all the same species. Relatively easy to keep and hardy, but males become territorial as they mature and should not be housed with other male gouramis or bettas. Available in virtually every fish store.
behavior: A surface-breathing labyrinth fish that gulps air at the surface regularly; keeps this habit even in well-oxygenated tanks. Males are territorial and chase other males and slow-finned fish. Feeler-like pelvic fins probe tank mates and the environment constantly.
dimorphism: Males have a longer, more pointed dorsal fin; females are rounder with a shorter, rounded dorsal.

description: A large, elegant gourami with an ethereal, reflective silver body that shimmers like moonlight — one of the most beautiful in the family. Less common in the hobby than the blue gourami and somewhat more sensitive; it prefers warmer, softer water and is more easily stressed by boisterous tankmates. Males can be territorial.
behavior: A surface-breathing labyrinth fish; calmer and less nippy than the blue gourami. Males will spar and chase rival males; pairs do best with a ratio of one male to two or more females. Skittish in a busy tank — prefers quiet, planted setups.
dimorphism: Males have a longer, more pointed dorsal and anal fin; females are rounder, especially when carrying eggs.

description: A large, distinctive labyrinth fish named for the 'kissing' behaviour in which males lock lips and push against each other — a display of strength, not affection. Grows to 30 cm and needs a spacious tank; sold as small juveniles in shops but quickly outgrows starter setups. Generally peaceful but can intimidate smaller fish.
behavior: A surface-breathing labyrinth fish that regularly gulps air; constantly grazes surfaces for algae and biofilm. The kissing behaviour is most common between rival males. Can be territorial toward similarly-shaped fish but generally leaves unlike species alone.
dimorphism: Males and females are nearly identical externally; females may be slightly fuller-bodied when gravid.

description: The largest gourami species — a powerful, hump-headed labyrinth fish that grows to 70 cm and lives over 20 years. Juveniles are attractive and 10 cm long when sold; adults are enormous, long-lived, and will eat any fish small enough to swallow. Requires a professional-scale aquarium and a genuine long-term commitment.
behavior: A surface-breathing labyrinth fish that needs unobstructed access to the surface. Intelligent and slow-moving; interacts with its keeper and can become hand-fed. Generally peaceful with similarly-large, unrelated fish but will consume anything it can fit in its mouth. Produces large amounts of waste — requires very powerful filtration.
dimorphism: Males develop a more prominent head hump and longer, more pointed dorsal and anal fins; females are rounder.

description: A jewel of the nano tank — the smallest commonly kept gourami, barely 4 cm long, with iridescent blue-green spangles that sparkle in light against a warm beige body. Produces audible clicking and purring sounds during courtship and territorial disputes, detectable without amplification. Males compete but rarely cause serious harm.
behavior: A surface-breathing labyrinth fish that drifts slowly through heavily-planted water, occasionally snapping at the surface. Males spar with fin displays and clicking sounds rather than physical combat. Shy with boisterous tankmates; best kept as a species tank or with other small, peaceful nano fish.
dimorphism: Males are more intensely coloured with longer, more pointed dorsal and caudal fins; females are plainer and rounder.

description: Larger than the sparkling gourami but with the same remarkable ability to produce audible croaking sounds — earned in the name. A peaceful, undemanding labyrinth fish suited to a planted tank with quiet, similarly-sized companions. Builds bubble nests at the surface under broad leaves.
behavior: A surface and upper-column fish that lurks among reeds and under large leaves; occasionally gulps air at the surface. Males croak and display to each other and during spawning. Peaceful outside breeding season; best in a densely-planted, low-flow setup.
dimorphism: Males are more colourful with longer, more pointed fins; females are plainer and rounder, especially when gravid.

description: One of the oldest aquarium fish — stunning red-and-blue banded flanks on a labyrinth fish that is almost indestructible in the right conditions. Thrives in cool water (15–22 °C) where bettas and other gouramis struggle, making it ideal for an unheated tank in a temperate climate. Males are violently aggressive toward each other and will shred bettas.
behavior: An active surface-dweller that builds bubble nests and chases anything it perceives as a rival male. Can live in stagnant, low-oxygen water thanks to its labyrinth organ. Extremely hardy — tolerates temperature swings, low oxygen, and a range of water chemistries that would kill more delicate species.
dimorphism: Males are larger, more brilliantly coloured with longer, more elaborate fins; females are plainer with a rounded belly.

description: A large, laterally compressed cichlid from the Amazon with a warm olive-to-golden body and faint dark banding — one of the calmer large cichlids in the hobby. Comes in several wild-type and captive colour morphs (gold, red, turquoise). Generally peaceful for its size but becomes territorial during spawning and will rearrange plants and decor.
behavior: A relatively calm cichlid that patrols the mid-water; pairs bond strongly and become fiercely protective during spawning. Best kept singly or as a bonded pair. Eats soft-leafed plants and will uproot rooted species — use hardy plants or plastic. Intelligent and recognises its keeper.
dimorphism: Males develop longer, more pointed dorsal and anal fins; females are rounder when gravid and tend to be slightly smaller.

description: A stunning cichlid from coastal Pacific South America — iridescent turquoise-blue and green scales on a powerful, muscular body with orange-red edging on the tail. Males develop a pronounced hump on the forehead with age. Beautiful but aggressive; attacks similar-sized fish and is particularly dangerous during spawning.
behavior: Patrols a territory in the lower water column and will attack any fish that enters it; most aggressive toward other cichlids and similarly-patterned species. Pairs bond strongly but females can be aggressive toward males outside spawning. Digs substrate heavily when establishing territory.
dimorphism: Males develop a prominent nuchal hump and grow larger; females are smaller but may be more aggressive during spawning. Males have more extensive iridescent patterning.

description: One of the most beginner-friendly cichlids — a compact, iridescent turquoise-and-blue fish from Trinidad and Venezuela with a restrained temperament. Pairs bond and dig to spawn but are otherwise peaceful toward unrelated species. An excellent gateway cichlid that shares a tank with most robust community fish.
behavior: Patrols the lower water column and digs in the substrate; pairs form strong bonds and become moderately territorial during breeding but return to peaceful behaviour shortly after. Generally ignores fish that are not competing for its territory.
dimorphism: Males are larger with more pointed dorsal and anal fins; females are smaller, rounder when gravid, and sometimes more intensely coloured when breeding.

description: A large, deep-bodied Amazonian cichlid with a rich reddish-brown to chocolate colouration and a black lateral spot — one of the calmer large cichlids in the hobby. Grows impressively large (30 cm) and requires soft, warm, acidic water. Peaceful for its size when not spawning, and can be kept with other large, peaceful fish.
behavior: A slow-moving, deliberate fish that shares territory more readily than most large cichlids; bonded pairs are devoted parents. Needs warm, soft, acidic water consistently — does not tolerate hard alkaline conditions. Eats plants.
dimorphism: Males grow larger and develop a small nuchal hump with age; females are slightly smaller and rounder.

description: A strikingly coloured Central American cichlid — vivid yellow-green body with a black midlateral stripe and vivid red belly that intensifies during breeding. Aggressive and territorial, particularly during spawning when it becomes markedly more dangerous. From harder, more alkaline water than most New World cichlids.
behavior: A bottom-oriented cichlid that establishes and defends a territory vigorously; females take an unusually active breeding role. Best kept as a single specimen or bonded pair — becomes particularly aggressive toward other cichlids during spawning.
dimorphism: Females are typically smaller but develop more intense red colouration on the belly during breeding; males are larger with more pointed fins.

description: A laterally compressed South American cichlid with a distinctive diagonal black stripe running from the snout through the eye to the base of the dorsal fin — the 'flag' of its common name. Unusually peaceful for a cichlid; suitable for a large community tank with other calm species. Needs a well-planted setup with hiding spots.
behavior: A mid-water cichlid that shelters among tall plants and driftwood; peaceful and shy compared to most cichlids. Pairs become territorial during spawning but return to a relatively calm demeanour afterward. Compatible with angelfish and similar-sized peaceful cichlids.
dimorphism: Males develop longer, more pointed dorsal and anal fins; females are rounder when gravid.

origin: Lakes Nicaragua and Managua, Central America
description: A large, powerfully built Central American cichlid renowned for its aggressive personality and striking coloration — ranging from vivid orange-red to white or yellow. Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump and can exceed 30 cm; their territorial and predatory behaviour makes them one of the most demanding cichlids to keep. Best housed alone or as a bonded pair in a heavily filtered, spacious tank.
behavior: Extremely aggressive and territorial; will attack and kill most tankmates including other large cichlids. Often becomes 'tame' toward its keeper but treats all other fish as rivals or prey. Pairs bond monogamously and both parents guard eggs and fry actively.
reproduction: Substrate spawner; female lays 600–700 eggs on flat rock or cleaned substrate. Both parents defend the clutch vigorously. Fry are free-swimming within a week.
dimorphism: Males grow significantly larger and develop a pronounced nuchal hump; females are smaller (~25–28 cm) and lack the hump. Males also show longer, more pointed dorsal and anal fins.

description: A large, elegant South American cichlid named for its habit of scooping mouthfuls of substrate to sift out food. Males develop striking red lateral stripes and iridescent scaling; both sexes remain relatively peaceful for a cichlid of their size, though males become territorial during spawning. Requires a spacious tank with a fine sand substrate, kept in a group of five or more.
behavior: Constantly sifts the substrate by scooping mouthfuls of sand and expelling it through the gills to filter out invertebrates and organic matter. Peaceful toward similarly sized tankmates outside of breeding; males spar and establish hierarchies within the group but rarely cause serious injury.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder; the female picks up eggs after spawning and incubates them in her buccal cavity. Fry are released once free-swimming and may continue to shelter in the parent's mouth when threatened.
dimorphism: Males grow larger and develop more vivid red striping and elongated dorsal and anal fin rays; females are smaller and less intensely coloured.

description: A compact loricariid from fast-flowing Colombian mountain streams, reaching around 17 cm total length. Its large fleshy mouth is adapted for rasping biofilm and algae from rocks, making it an effective algae-eater. It needs good water flow and oxygenation to thrive, and is territorial toward its own kind — best kept singly in a well-oxygenated tank with smooth rocks and driftwood.
behavior: A benthic grazer that clings to rocks, driftwood, and glass; spends most of its time rasping surfaces for algae and biofilm. Active at dusk and night; prefers areas of strong current. Territorial toward conspecifics but ignores most other species.

description: A large, powerful Central American cichlid from the lakes of Nicaragua and Costa Rica, ranging from grey-brown juveniles to brilliant gold, orange, or white adults. Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump and can reach 35 cm; they are among the most aggressive cichlids available. Often confused with the Red Devil cichlid (A. labiatus) — both are sold under the same common name and hybridise extensively in the trade. Best kept alone or as a bonded pair in a heavily filtered, spacious tank.
behavior: Extremely territorial and aggressive toward all tankmates; will attack and injure most fish. Best kept alone or as a single bonded pair acclimated carefully over time. Pairs form monogamous bonds and both parents guard eggs and fry vigorously.
dimorphism: Males grow substantially larger and develop a pronounced cranial nuchal hump; females are smaller and lack the hump. The hump size increases with age and condition.

description: A man-made hybrid cichlid developed in Southeast Asia in the 1990s, primarily from Red Devil, Three Spot, and Midas cichlids. Best known for its vivid coloration and pronounced nuchal hump (kok), which grows with age. Highly aggressive and territorial — must be kept alone or as a carefully matched breeding pair in a very large tank. Its hardiness and bold, interactive personality make it manageable for experienced fishkeepers despite demanding space requirements.
behavior: Extremely territorial and aggressive toward all tankmates; best kept as a solitary specimen. Known for bold, interactive behaviour with its keeper — often tracks movement outside the tank and responds to feeding routines.

description: A mbuna from the southern rocky shores of Lake Malawi, famous for its dramatic reversed sexual dichromatism: females and juveniles are brilliant golden-yellow with bold black stripes, while dominant males transform to near-black with pale blue stripes. One of the most aggressive and territorial mbuna; must be kept as a harem (one male, three or more females) in a heavily rock-scaped tank. Should never be kept with other Melanochromis species due to aggression and hybridization risk.
behavior: Highly aggressive and territorial; males suppress rivals visually through color dominance (dominant males are dark; subordinates revert to the golden female pattern). Best managed by overcrowding to diffuse aggression. A maternal mouthbrooder; females incubate fry for several weeks.
dimorphism: Reversed colour dimorphism: females and juveniles are golden-yellow with black and white stripes on the upper body; dominant males are near-black with pale blue or yellow stripes — a near-inversion of the female pattern.

description: A robust mbuna from Mbenji Island, Lake Malawi, famous for its reversed sexual dimorphism: females and juvenile males are pale blue with bold black vertical bars, while adult males transform to bright yellow-orange. Highly aggressive and territorial — keep as a harem of one male with three or more females in a heavily rock-scaped tank. Not suitable for peaceful community setups.
behavior: Males establish and defend territories among rocks; females and subordinate males form loose groups. A maternal mouthbrooder — females hold eggs and fry in the mouth for several weeks.
dimorphism: Reversed colour dimorphism: females and juvenile males are pale blue with bold black vertical bars; adult males transform to bright yellow-orange with faint brown barring and plain yellow fins.

description: A large giraffe-patterned haplochromine from Lake Malawi renowned for an extraordinary ambush strategy: it lies motionless on the sandy substrate, mimicking a dead fish, then lunges at curious prey. An exclusive piscivore that will eat any fish small enough to swallow. Males develop vivid blue-and-yellow coloration when in breeding condition; females retain the mottled giraffe pattern year-round. Keep only one male per tank.
behavior: Uses thanatosis — lies motionless on the sand, sometimes partially buried, using its mottled coloration to lure smaller fish investigating what appears to be a dead fish, then strikes. Males are highly aggressive toward each other; best kept as a harem (1 male, 3–6 females).
dimorphism: Males grow larger (up to 30 cm) and develop vivid blue-and-yellow coloration on the head and flanks in breeding condition. Females are smaller (~22 cm) and retain the mottled brown giraffe pattern year-round.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. Female incubates eggs and fry in her mouth for approximately 3 weeks.

description: One of the most vividly colored Lake Malawi haplochromines. Adult males display a striking red-orange body with an iridescent blue head; females remain a plain silver-grey with faint barring. Best kept as a harem of one male with three or more females in a spacious rocky Malawi biotope alongside other peaceful Malawi cichlids. Requires hard, alkaline water and a plant-based diet supplemented with small invertebrates.
behavior: An active mid-water swimmer that patrols open rocky habitat. Males are territorial toward other males and defend a spawning site but are generally peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder. Female incubates fertilized eggs in her mouth for 3–4 weeks and releases free-swimming fry.
dimorphism: Males are substantially larger and brilliantly colored — body red to orange with a blue head and extended dorsal, anal, and pelvic fins. Females and juveniles are plain silvery-grey with faint vertical barring.

description: A small but boldly patterned black-and-white cichlid endemic to the rocky northwestern shore of Lake Tanganyika. It forms a strong monogamous pair bond and spawns in rock caves or crevices, with both parents guarding fry tenaciously. Requires hard, alkaline Tanganyika-type water (pH 8.0–9.0) and a densely rock-scaped aquarium. Aggressive toward intruders of all kinds — not suitable for community tanks.
behavior: A monogamous, cave-dwelling pair-bonder that claims and fiercely defends a rock territory. Aggressive toward any fish entering its space; will consume fry of other species. Stays close to the rockwork at all times.

description: One of the world's smallest cichlids and a Lake Tanganyika endemic. Multifasciatus form self-sustaining colonies on sandy substrates carpeted with empty snail shells, each fish claiming and defending one or more shells as its territory. Their tiny size, fascinating colonial social life, and ease of breeding make them a highlight of any Tanganyikan nano setup.
behavior: A colonial shell-dweller that forms complex hierarchical societies. Each individual claims one or more shells and defends them vigorously against neighbours; the group continuously shuffles shells, digs sand, and monitors intruders.
dimorphism: Males are noticeably larger than females and show slightly more intense colouration with a reddish tint to the upper dorsal fin.
reproduction: Shell brooder; the female spawns inside her chosen shell and guards the eggs alone. Eggs hatch in roughly 24 hours and fry become free-swimming after 6–7 days.

description: A strikingly compressed Lake Tanganyika cichlid covered in pearlescent spots. Slow-growing and long-lived, calvus reward patient keepers with fascinating ambush hunting behaviour — using their laterally compressed body to wedge into rock crevices and ambush prey. Best kept with other calm Tanganyikans; boisterous tankmates will cause chronic stress.
behavior: A slow, methodical hunter that wedges its extremely compressed body into rock crevices to ambush small fish and crustaceans. Shy and easily stressed by boisterous tankmates; best kept with calm species in a heavily rock-scaped Tanganyika biotope.
dimorphism: Males grow substantially larger than females and develop longer, more elaborate fins; females remain noticeably smaller and stockier.
reproduction: Substrate spawner; deposits eggs in a rock crevice or cave. Eggs hatch in approximately 2 days and fry become free-swimming after a further 8–10 days. The male should be removed post-spawning.

description: One of the most visually striking cichlids in the hobby, the electric blue hap dazzles with its intense metallic blue colouration. A piscivore by nature, it will eat any fish small enough to swallow; keep with same-sized or larger Malawi haplochromines and Aulonocara in a spacious tank with hard, alkaline water.
behavior: Males patrol open water and rocky margins, displaying intensely to females and rival males. A piscivore that will consume any fish small enough to swallow; not compatible with small fish or fry.
dimorphism: Strong sexual dichromatism: males are brilliant iridescent electric blue with a red-orange anal fin; females are plain silver-grey.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder; the female incubates eggs in her mouth for roughly 3 weeks and releases free-swimming fry.

description: A jewel of the West African dwarf cichlid world, the Nigerian krib is celebrated for the female's stunning violet belly flush during breeding condition. Distinct from the common kribensis (P. pulcher) with softer water requirements and different colour morphs across populations. Rewards keepers with elaborate pair-bonding rituals and attentive cave-spawning parental care.
behavior: A shy, cave-seeking dwarf cichlid that pairs for life and becomes boldly territorial during spawning. Largely ignores unrelated tankmates outside breeding; the pair together can defend fry aggressively.
dimorphism: Females develop a vivid pink-to-purple belly flush when spawning-ready and are more compact; males are larger with pointed dorsal and anal fins and bold scale patterning along the flanks.
reproduction: Cave spawner; the pair seals themselves inside a chosen cave to spawn and guards eggs and fry together. Eggs hatch in 2–3 days and fry are free-swimming within a week.

description: A hardy Florida killifish with a striking patriotic colour pattern and a fondness for hair algae. The flagfish is one of the best natural algae controllers for cooler-water planted tanks, tolerates a wide pH range, and is easy to breed. Males can be fin-nippers toward slow or long-finned tankmates; best kept with robust, similarly sized fish.
behavior: Males are territorial and may fin-nip long-finned or slow tankmates. An avid algae grazer — one of the few fish that makes a meaningful dent in hair algae. Active swimmer that uses all tank levels.
dimorphism: Males are more colourful with green iridescent scales forming a flag-like pattern and extended dorsal and anal fins; females are plainer with a dark blotch in the dorsal fin and a rounder belly.
reproduction: Fractional spawner; scatters adhesive eggs among algae and plants over several days. The male may guard the spawn; eggs hatch in 7–14 days.

description: One of the largest and most spectacular killifish available, the blue gularis is a non-annual West African species whose males sport vivid blue, red, and yellow patterning with dramatic fin extensions. Males are aggressive toward one another and will eat fish small enough to swallow; tankmates must be robust and comparably sized. Best kept as a single male with two females, or in a species setup with plenty of cover.
behavior: Males are highly territorial toward conspecific males and will harass them relentlessly. Compatible with robust, similarly sized peaceful species. An accomplished jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential.
dimorphism: Males are notably larger, intensely coloured in blue and red, and carry extended rays on the caudal, dorsal, and anal fins; females are smaller, plainer, and rounder-bellied.
reproduction: A substrate spawner that deposits eggs into peat moss or fine-grain substrate; eggs hatch in approximately 21 days in water — no dry desiccation phase required.

description: A brilliantly coloured annual killifish from the seasonal floodplains of coastal Mozambique, where pools dry completely each year. Males display intense turquoise and orange banding that rivals any reef fish; females are drab olive-brown. The entire lifecycle — from hatch to death — unfolds within a single year. Eggs require a carefully managed dry storage phase of five to seven months before re-flooding triggers hatching.
behavior: Males are aggressive toward one another; keep only one per small tank or provide multiple females to diffuse attention. Generally tolerant of similarly sized peaceful species not prone to fin-nipping.
dimorphism: Males are larger and spectacularly coloured in vivid turquoise-blue with orange-red banding; females are considerably smaller and uniformly olive-brown.
reproduction: Annual species; pairs drive eggs into peat or fine sand substrate. Eggs undergo obligate diapause and must be stored dry at 21–25°C for five to seven months before re-wetting triggers hatching.

description: One of the largest rainbowfish, reaching over 20 cm in well-maintained aquaria, with colours that intensify markedly as males mature and rival one another. Despite its size it is entirely peaceful, though its energetic swimming can unsettle smaller, slower tankmates. A school of six or more in a spacious, well-planted tank brings out the best in this species.
behavior: An active, shoaling open-water swimmer. Males display and spar constantly but rarely cause injury; this behaviour intensifies their colouration significantly.
dimorphism: Males grow larger, develop a deeper body profile, and show far brighter colouration with extended dorsal and anal fin rays; females are smaller, slimmer, and subdued in colour.
reproduction: Egg scatterer; spawns over fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over multiple days. Eggs hatch in seven to twelve days and fry require infusoria or liquid fry food initially.

description: A hardy, colourful Australian native introduced to the hobby as far back as 1927. Males display a silvery-blue to green body overlaid with yellow striping, red-spotted fins, and vivid black fin margins that intensify during spawning. One of the few tropical-looking rainbowfish that tolerates genuinely cool water, making it suitable for unheated tanks in temperate climates.
behavior: A peaceable, active schooling fish that congregates around submerged wood and vegetation. Males spar and display but cause no real harm; competition produces their most intense colouration.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more intensely coloured, with red spots on the fins and jet-black fin margins that become prominent during breeding; females have clear fins and a more subdued overall colour.
reproduction: Egg-adhesive scatterer; eggs stick to fine-leaved plants or floating roots and hatch within approximately one week.

description: A large, muscular New Guinea endemic whose males develop a striking golden-orange blaze across the rear half of the body as they mature, set against a dark lavender-grey front. Females and juveniles are considerably plainer. A peaceful but energetic shoaling species that thrives in hard, slightly alkaline water reflecting its clear PNG river origins. Listed as Near Threatened in the wild; captive-bred stock should be preferred.
behavior: A fast, open-water shoaler that needs significant horizontal swimming space. Males display vigorously with flared fins and broadside posturing, intensifying colouration without causing serious injury.
dimorphism: Males develop a dramatically deeper body profile, extended dorsal and anal fins, and characteristic golden-orange posterior colouration; females and young males are silvery with only subtle fin colour.
reproduction: Egg scatterer; spawns among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Eggs hatch in seven to ten days and fry require infusoria or prepared fry foods.

description: Dominant males develop a stunning deep crimson-red body against an olive-gold female. An active, fast-moving Lake Sentani endemic that needs groups of 6+ and open swimming lanes with dense planting along the edges. Speed and size make it a good community fish for similarly robust species; it can stress delicate or slow-moving tankmates.
behavior: Active open-water schooler; males display intensely to each other and to females, requiring a clear length of tank.
dimorphism: Males develop deep red body colour and a pronounced hump with age; females remain smaller and olive-brown.

description: An ancient, surface-patrolling predator reaching 90 cm with a dragon-like appearance and the habit of leaping clear of the water to catch prey. Suitable only for the largest private aquaria; juveniles outgrow a standard tank within a year. Will eat any fish or invertebrate it can swallow, and must be kept under a heavy, secured lid.
behavior: Solitary surface cruiser; jumps powerfully — a heavy, weighted lid is mandatory. Aggressive toward conspecifics in confined spaces.
dimorphism: Females develop a broader, rounder belly; males are typically slimmer. Difficult to sex outside of breeding condition.

description: An elegant, scaleless black fish with a white tail band that navigates by weak electric fields. Nocturnal and shy, it needs caves and dim areas to feel secure. Peaceful toward most tankmates but territorial with its own kind; the lack of scales makes it highly susceptible to parasites and medication overdose.
behavior: Uses electrolocation to navigate and hunt in darkness. Best provided with caves, PVC tubes, or dense plants to shelter in during the day.
dimorphism: Not reliably distinguishable externally; females may appear slightly rounder in the abdomen when gravid.

description: A large nocturnal Southeast Asian predator with a knife-like body, ornate ocellated spots, and a rippling anal fin used for propulsion. Juveniles are commonly sold but grow to 1 m, making them suitable only for very large custom installations. Will consume any fish that fits in its mouth and demands pristine, highly oxygenated water.
behavior: Nocturnal ambush predator; propels itself using anal fin rippling and can swim backward. Territorial toward its own kind; needs large hiding structures and dim lighting.
dimorphism: No reliable external sexual dimorphism; females may develop a fuller abdomen when gravid.

description: The smallest aquarium knifefish and the only African species, reaching 20–30 cm. Hardy and adaptable, it uses a modified swim bladder to breathe air and can survive in low-oxygen water. Becomes increasingly territorial with age, especially toward its own kind; small fish that fit in its mouth are at risk.
behavior: Nocturnal; swims in a sinusoidal pattern and can move backward. Needs caves or dense vegetation for daytime shelter.
dimorphism: No reliable external markers to distinguish sexes.

description: A slender, translucent knifefish that navigates by generating a weak electric field — the same field it uses to detect prey and communicate with others of its kind. Naturally gregarious and best kept in groups of 3+; large groups actually reduce inter-individual aggression. Strictly nocturnal, it needs hiding spots and dim lighting during the day.
behavior: Produces a continuous electric field for navigation and communication; does not swim with tail-powered strokes but ripples its anal fin. Groups establish dominance hierarchies.
dimorphism: Males are typically larger and develop a longer body. Females tend to be shorter and may appear rounder when gravid.

description: One of the world's tiniest percoid fishes, with males displaying stunning iridescent blue vertical bars and red flanks. Males are intensely territorial and must not be kept together without enough space and visual breaks. A strict micropredator — it will usually refuse dried food, requiring live or frozen Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, and small worms.
behavior: Males defend small territories vigorously, displaying and sparring constantly. A 1:2 or 1:3 male-to-female ratio with visual barriers reduces stress.
dimorphism: Males are brightly coloured with blue bars and red pigment; females are drab olive-brown and significantly smaller.

description: A powerful, fast-growing South American cichlid that reaches 74 cm and requires tank sizes achievable only in large custom installations. Will eat any fish it can fit in its large mouth; juveniles are commonly sold but rapidly outgrow standard aquaria. Mature males develop a prominent nuchal hump and can become highly aggressive.
behavior: Open-water ambush predator; hunts by speed rather than stealth. Territorial toward its own kind and toward other large cichlids in confined spaces.
dimorphism: Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump with age; females remain slimmer. Males tend to be larger overall.

description: A large, beautifully patterned South American freshwater ray reaching 60 cm disc width that demands huge, low-profile tanks with sandy substrate. The venomous tail spine poses a serious injury risk during maintenance — purpose-built wide tanks without décor are essential. A captivating specialist fish that can live over 20 years.
behavior: Buries in substrate during rest; hunts invertebrates and small fish by ambush. Requires twice-daily feeding. The barbed tail spine is a venom delivery mechanism — never reach under the ray.
dimorphism: Males have two claspers (modified pelvic fins) clearly visible near the tail base; females lack these structures.

description: An extraordinarily camouflaged catfish shaped exactly like a dead twig, using algae and biofilm as its primary diet. Peaceful and slow-moving, it can be kept with most community fish but is sensitive to deteriorating water quality and will starve if algae is insufficient. Established tanks with well-developed biofilm suit it far better than sterile setups.
behavior: Sedentary algae-scraper; clings to driftwood, plant stems, and glass. Rarely moves quickly. May compete with other algae-eating plecos for grazing territory.
dimorphism: Males develop small odontodes (bristle-like projections) on the rostrum (snout) during breeding; females lack these.

description: A slender, leaf-litter Loricariid that scrapes aufwuchs and accepts most sinking foods. One of the most peaceful catfish available and a good beginner Loricariid. Pairs readily, and males guard eggs in caves; juveniles can be delicate to raise but adults are hardy.
behavior: Sifts through leaf litter and rests against the substrate or on driftwood. Males defend a spawning cave and fan the eggs; remove them to a separate rearing tank for best fry survival.
dimorphism: Males develop odontodes (bristles) on the cheeks and flanks during breeding condition; females lack these and are generally shorter and rounder.

description: A leaf-mimicking anabantoid from the Congo that hunts by drifting motionless until prey is within range. Hardy and long-lived (15+ years), it is one of the most rewarding oddballs for aquarists willing to feed live or frozen prey. Any fish small enough to fit in its mouth will be eaten; otherwise it ignores tankmates.
behavior: Cryptic ambush hunter; floats with head angled downward imitating a drifting leaf. Possesses a labyrinth organ for atmospheric air-breathing.
dimorphism: Males may show more intense spotting and develop odontodes on the body scales; females tend to be plainer and rounder.

description: The most widely distributed and commonly kept snakehead, reaching 15–20 cm with extraordinary geographic colour variation. An obligate air-breather requiring a gap between water surface and a tight lid; juvenile pairs can bond but adults become territorial and aggression usually excludes all but the bonded partner. Not for community tanks.
behavior: Obligate air-breather; must access the surface regularly. Pairs can form lasting bonds and often spawn in floating vegetation. Capable jumper — lid must be secure.
dimorphism: Males tend to be more vividly coloured; females appear rounder in the belly. Difficult to sex definitively outside of spawning condition.

description: A jewel among snakeheads from the cool Brahmaputra highlands, displaying iridescent blue, red, and orange colours. Requires a seasonal cool-down (15°C winter period) to thrive and breed; constant tropical temperatures shorten its lifespan. Adults pair-bond and become highly aggressive to all intruders; keep singly or as a dedicated bonded pair.
behavior: Obligate air-breather needing surface access. Notably lacks pelvic fins. Both parents guard the fry aggressively after hatching.
dimorphism: Males are more intensely coloured, especially on the fins; females are larger-bodied and duller. Easier to distinguish during spawning season.

description: The largest snakehead species, reaching over 1 m and widely sold as a brightly-coloured juvenile for as little as a few dollars — a deceptive sale that ends with a fish suitable only for public aquaria. Juveniles display vivid orange-and-black stripes that fade with age; adults are powerful predators that will eat anything moving. Should not be purchased for home aquariums.
behavior: Fast, powerful open-water predator; actively hunts by speed rather than ambush. Capable of injuring handlers. An obligate air-breather requiring surface access and a very heavy lid.
dimorphism: No reliable external sexual dimorphism at juvenile or sub-adult stage. Adults: females may be broader-bodied.

description: One of the world's largest freshwater fish; wild adults commonly exceed 2 m and 100 kg. An obligate air-breather that produces an audible gulping sound at the surface. Exclusively a public aquarium species — even at 120 cm it requires a swimming-pool-scale enclosure. Apex predator consuming fish, crustaceans, and occasionally terrestrial animals near the water's edge.
behavior: Obligate air-breather; surfaces regularly with an audible gulp. Powerful and potentially dangerous — lifting the fish clear of the water can rupture spinal blood vessels. Strong jumper requiring secure barriers. Builds 15 cm nests in sandy substrates for spawning.

origin: Southeast Asia (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Kapuas, Musi basins)
description: A striking ambush predator with bold yellow-and-black tiger barring; also known as the Indonesian tiger perch or finescale tigerfish. Inhabits flooded forests and areas with submerged branches. Adults are solitary and aggressive to conspecifics. Often refuses food initially after import — patience and varied live/frozen foods are needed. SF lists adults as public-aquarium territory.
behavior: Lurking ambush predator. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly shaped fish. Adults must be kept alone. Can be difficult to transition to dead food after import. Juveniles may be raised in groups of 5+.

description: The iconic piranha species with a vivid red-orange belly. Shoaling predator forming strict dominance hierarchies; best kept as a single specimen or in a group of 5+. Pairs fight persistently. Primarily crepuscular; cautious and often shy in captivity. Significant bioload demands robust filtration. Check local regulations before acquiring — banned in many US states and countries.
behavior: Forms dominance hierarchies in groups; 5+ individuals distribute aggression more evenly than pairs. Primarily active at dawn and dusk. Messy feeder requiring efficient filtration. Groups may mob an intruder but solitary individuals are often timid.

origin: South America (Amazon, Orinoco, Guiana Shield rivers)
description: The largest solitary piranha; a true specimen fish that must be kept alone and will kill any tankmate including conspecifics. Recognised by its dark body and striking red iris. Cryptic and timid in captivity — spends most time hiding and striking suddenly at prey. More dangerous to handle than social piranhas. Place all heaters externally — fish will bite equipment.
behavior: Strictly solitary. Cryptic ambush predator — hides in cover and strikes suddenly. Primarily active at low light. Will bite through heaters, sensors, and silicone; position equipment externally. Feed 2–3 times per week.

description: The 'vegetarian piranha'; a massive characin with flat, human-like crushing teeth adapted to crack seeds and fruit. Frequently sold cheaply as juveniles but grows to 90 cm+ requiring a 2,550-litre tank. Do not keep with live plants — they will be eaten. Banned from sale in several US states due to release incidents. Commonly surrendered to public aquaria as adults.
behavior: Primarily herbivorous; actively seeks and crushes seeds, fruit, and vegetation. Adults become solitary and may fin-nip in cramped conditions. Significant bioload — requires large efficient filtration and regular 30–50% water changes.

description: Florida's endemic gar species, found in the state's lakes, canals, and lowland streams. Very similar to the spotted gar in care. Facultative air-breather using a modified swim bladder. Voracious ambush predator; max wild size 132 cm, typical captive adult 55–90 cm. Note: roe is highly toxic.
behavior: Facultative air-breather; surfaces regularly to gulp air. Slow-moving ambush predator. Needs a secure lid. Roe is highly toxic to mammals.

description: A living fossil capable of aestivating for over 3 years sealed in a mucus cocoon during drought. Obligate air-breather that must surface every ~30 minutes. Carnivorous on fish, frogs, mollusks, and worms. Should be kept alone — capable of delivering powerful, damaging bites. Powerful substrate digger.
behavior: Obligate air-breather; must access the surface at least every 30 minutes. Aestivates by sealing itself in a mucus cocoon within hardened mud. Aggressive and capable of strong bites — handle with extreme caution. Will uproot all substrate and decor.
reproduction: Spawns at the onset of the rainy season in burrows. Male guards the nest and fans the eggs to maintain oxygen levels.

origin: South America (Amazon, Paraguay, lower Paraná basins)
description: The only lungfish species in the Americas and the sole member of its order. Obligate air-breather with highly reduced gills — would drown if prevented from surfacing. Generally tolerant of large tankmates but may bite bottom-dwellers. Requires a very large, low-flow tank; a 20+ year commitment.
behavior: Obligate air-breather; must access surface regularly. Aestivates by burrowing in mud when water recedes, sealed in a mucus cocoon. Adults need feeding only once weekly. Minimum water movement required.
reproduction: Males develop highly vascularised fin filaments that release oxygen directly beside the eggs during brooding, aerating the clutch in the burrow nest.

origin: Australia and New Guinea (Gulf of Carpentaria drainage)
description: The Australian/New Guinean arowana; closely resembles Asian arowanas and is sometimes mislabelled in trade. Considered the most aggressive arowana species — will attack most tankmates regardless of size. Surface-feeding predator taking insects, fish, crustaceans, and small vertebrates. Mouthbrooder; uniquely, the female carries the young (unlike Asian arowanas where the male broods).
behavior: Highly aggressive surface predator. Leaps from the water to take prey; requires a very secure, heavy lid. Territorial toward conspecifics and attacks most tankmates. Prone to drop-eye syndrome if kept under bright overhead lighting.

description: A blackwater arowana from the highly acidic Rio Negro; closely related to the silver arowana. Juveniles are striking black with yellow-white bands; adults become uniformly dark. Surface predator with a specialised upturned jaw adapted to catching prey from below the water surface. Sensitive to water quality and prefers soft, acidic conditions.
behavior: Surface predator. Leaps to catch prey; secure lid essential. Aggressive toward conspecifics; best kept alone. Mouthbrooder — male carries the eggs and fry. Sensitive to water hardness; performs best in soft, slightly acidic water.

description: Striking snakehead with dark brown body bearing 7–8 large irregular orange blotches. Endemic to the Brahmaputra Basin in Assam. Requires seasonal temperature cycling with a cool winter period (10–15 °C) for long-term health. Obligate air-breather; needs a tight-fitting lid and humid air layer above the water.
behavior: Obligate air-breather. Highly territorial — best kept alone or as a proven pair. Capable jumper; lid must be tight-fitting with a humid air gap.

origin: South Asia (India, Pakistan, China, Thailand, Cambodia)
description: The largest snakehead species; wild individuals reportedly reach 183 cm TL, with captive adults typically 50–65 cm. Pelagic and active, requiring extensive open swimming space. Obligate air-breather. Juveniles can be relatively tolerant but become highly aggressive at sexual maturity — realistically a public aquarium species.
behavior: Obligate air-breather. Pelagic; needs large open water. Builds floating weed nests and provides brood care. Extremely aggressive at maturity; best kept alone.

description: A small hill-stream snakehead from the eastern Himalayas (Assam, Arunachal Pradesh, Nepal). Common in the aquarium trade. Care is similar to Channa bleheri; seasonal temperature variation is recommended. Obligate air-breather requiring a secure lid.
behavior: Obligate air-breather; must access the surface. Aggressive toward conspecifics. Inhabits ponds and hill streams.

origin: North America (Mississippi Basin, Great Lakes, Gulf Coast)
description: A primitive North American predator from the gar family. Facultative air-breather using a modified swim bladder to supplement gill respiration. Voracious ambush predator; will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow. Wild max 150 cm; typical captive adult 60–90 cm. Requires a very large cool-water aquarium.
behavior: Facultative air-breather; surfaces periodically to gulp air. Slow-moving ambush predator. Can tolerate brief brackish exposure. Needs a secure lid.

description: The most widespread gar species in North America; reaches 1–1.2 m in captivity and up to 2 m in the wild. Recognised by its extremely elongated, tooth-studded snout. Facultative air-breather and apex predator; realistically a public aquarium species. Mostly active at dusk and night.
behavior: Facultative air-breather; regularly surfaces to breathe. Largely inactive by day; active at dusk. Ambush predator — will engulf any suitably-sized tankmate. Tolerates brief brackish exposure.

description: A classic schooling fish with a distinctly round, silver body that resembles a coin. Peaceful with similarly sized fish but will consume soft-leaved aquarium plants. Best kept in groups of five or more in a spacious tank furnished with hardy or artificial décor.
behavior: Active midwater schooler. Groups of five or more are calmer and bolder than isolated specimens. Will systematically consume or uproot most soft-leaved plants.
dimorphism: Males develop a concave anal fin edge; females are rounder and fuller-bodied when gravid.

description: A large disc-shaped silver dollar identified by vivid red anal and caudal fins. Primarily herbivorous and peaceful with similarly sized fish, but will consume very small companions and will destroy most soft-leaved plants. Typically reaches 20–25 cm in captivity; wild individuals can approach 40 cm.
behavior: Active schooler spending most of its time in the middle water column. Groups of five or more reduce stress and skittish behaviour.

description: The largest freshwater predatory fish in Africa, capable of exceeding 150 cm and 50 kg in the wild. Inhabits the Congo River and Lake Tanganyika, where it uses massive interlocking teeth to take prey up to half its own size. Captive specimens typically reach 1–1.2 m; their enormous space requirements and aggressive nature make them suitable only for large public aquaria.
behavior: Highly aggressive apex predator. Solitary in captivity — cannot be housed with smaller fish. Ambushes prey using acute eyesight and lateral line detection. Juveniles may shoal briefly.

description: A master of camouflage that mimics a dead, drifting leaf almost perfectly — complete with a chin barbel resembling a leaf stalk. Ambushes small fish and invertebrates with lightning-fast lunges. Requires very acidic soft blackwater and a constant supply of live prey; does not readily adapt to frozen or prepared foods.
behavior: Extremely sedentary ambush predator. Drifts motionlessly imitating a leaf, then strikes when prey comes within range. Sensitive to disturbance; needs a quiet, dimly lit tank.
dimorphism: Females are rounder and fuller-bodied than males; otherwise difficult to sex.

description: Not a true eel but a knifefish capable of generating discharges up to 860 volts — enough to stun large prey or seriously injure a human. Growing to 2.5 m, it requires a dedicated 2000-litre or larger tank with no tankmates and strict safety protocols for all maintenance. A specimen for specialist and public aquaria only.
behavior: Largely nocturnal, slow-moving bottom dweller. Nearly blind; navigates and hunts using weak electric pulses (electroreception). Surfaces regularly to breathe air. Can deliver a powerful electric shock when stressed or attacking prey.
reproduction: Males build a foam nest from saliva and guard eggs. Rarely bred in captivity.

description: A large, ribbon-like knifefish with bold dark bands on a tan or olive body. Uses weak electric pulses for navigation and prey detection. Strictly solitary — extremely territorial with conspecifics and will consume any fish small enough to swallow. Best kept alone or with large, boisterous species in a 680-litre or larger aquarium with ample cover.
behavior: Nocturnal and reclusive; hides in PVC tubes, caves, or dense cover by day. Generates weak electric fields for electroreception. Extremely territorial with its own kind — no two adults can share the same tank.

description: Africa's largest freshwater puffer, reaching 43 cm. Highly aggressive — will attack conspecifics and relentlessly bite the fins of any tank mate — and must be housed alone as a species specimen. Requires a diet of hard-shelled invertebrates (snails, shellfish, crustaceans) to grind down its continuously growing fused beak. A bold, interactive fish for experienced keepers with large aquaria.
behavior: Curious and intelligent; often recognises its keeper. Extremely territorial toward any tank mate — will bite and tear fins. Beak requires regular wearing down through hard-shelled prey.
dimorphism: Difficult to sex externally; females may be rounder in the abdomen when gravid.

description: One of the most aggressive freshwater puffers in the hobby, the pignose or arrowhead puffer must be housed strictly alone. An ambush predator, it launches itself at prey from a motionless resting position and can overpower fish considerably larger than itself. Requires live and meaty foods plus hard-shelled invertebrates for beak maintenance.
behavior: Ambush predator that rests motionless on the substrate for extended periods, then strikes with explosive speed. Extremely aggressive and must be kept alone. Will attempt to bite during tank maintenance.

description: A medium-large hump-backed puffer from the sluggish rivers of Southeast Asia. Primarily nocturnal, it ambushes sleeping fish and preys on hard-shelled invertebrates. Like all puffers, it needs snails and shelled shellfish to maintain its beak. Unsuitable for community tanks; best housed alone in a species setup.
behavior: Nocturnal hunter; most active after dark. Rests motionless by day. Puffs up defensively when threatened. Territorial toward conspecifics.

description: A small but feisty puffer with striking red irises. Despite measuring only 5–7 cm, it is territorial and will nip the fins of slower tank mates. Best kept alone or in a species-only setup. Like all puffers, regular access to hard-shelled prey (snails, small crustaceans) is necessary to prevent beak overgrowth.
behavior: Active and curious; patrols its territory and investigates everything in its environment. Will nip fins of slower or less aggressive tank mates. Needs snails or shelled invertebrates to prevent beak overgrowth.
dimorphism: Males are slimmer with a reddish belly streak; females are rounder and more subdued in colour.

description: One of the most iconic large Central American cichlids, boldly patterned with irregular black blotches on a gold background. Males can reach 55 cm and are highly predatory, consuming any fish that fits in their mouth. Extremely territorial; best kept as a single specimen or verified breeding pair in a 475-litre or larger aquarium.
behavior: Highly aggressive and territorial; males especially attack anything that enters their space. Will consume smaller fish. Monogamous pair-bonders that display intense parental care.
dimorphism: Males are significantly larger and develop a more pronounced jaw; females are smaller and develop intense orange-red belly colouration during breeding.

description: The only cichlid native to the United States, the Texas cichlid is a robust, blue-green spotted fish reaching 30 cm. Highly territorial and aggressive; unsuitable for a community tank. Unusually tolerant of cooler water and harder conditions than most cichlids, reflecting its subtropical North American origins.
behavior: Aggressive and territorial; digs extensively and rearranges substrate. Confrontational with other fish — best kept as a single specimen or a verified breeding pair.
dimorphism: Males develop a distinct nuchal hump with age and are slightly larger; females are less intensely coloured.

description: One of the most spectacularly colored cichlids in the hobby. Males reach 45 cm and display intense orange-red coloration with bold black bars during breeding; females (~33 cm) are often even more brilliantly colored. Extremely aggressive — must be kept alone or as a mated pair; it will kill virtually any tankmate in a standard aquarium.
behavior: Extremely territorial toward conspecifics and other fish. Monogamous pair-bonder with intense parental care. Aggression escalates sharply during breeding.
dimorphism: Males are much larger (up to 45 cm) and orange-red with black bars. Females (~33 cm) are typically deeper red-orange and often more vividly patterned.

description: A compact dwarf cichlid from coastal rivers of Guyana and Suriname. Males display striking iridescent green-gold flanks; females are smaller and develop intense black-and-gold breeding colouration when guarding eggs. Generally peaceful with community fish but females become fiercely aggressive during brood care. A good introduction to keeping dwarf cichlids.
behavior: Shy and peaceful when not breeding. Females are devoted parents and will aggressively defend eggs and fry against fish many times their size.
dimorphism: Males significantly larger (up to 8 cm) with iridescent flanks; females (~4 cm) develop bold black-and-gold breeding pattern.

description: One of the most beautiful small cichlids in the hobby, the lyretail checkerboard displays an intricate chequerboard pattern on a silver body; breeding males develop spectacular lyre-shaped caudal extensions. Demands very soft, acidic blackwater (pH 5–6 optimal) and live or frozen foods. Best kept in a species or blackwater biotope tank.
behavior: Relatively peaceful but sensitive. Males may spar briefly; females guard eggs and fry alone after spawning. Very sensitive to water quality — requires pristine acidic conditions and zero ammonia.
dimorphism: Males develop long filamentous caudal extensions and vivid colouration; females are rounder and more subdued.

description: A hardy dwarf cichlid from the subtropical rivers of Paraguay and Argentina — one of the few Apistogramma species able to tolerate cooler water. Males develop a striking blue, yellow and orange colouration with extended finnage; females are smaller and yellow when guarding fry. Suitable for a peaceful community tank when kept with captive-bred specimens.
behavior: Peaceful and curious; explores the substrate and lower water column. Males hold small territories and may squabble mildly. Females guard eggs and fry in caves with intensity.
dimorphism: Males larger (up to 7 cm) with vivid blue-yellow-orange colouration and extended fins; females smaller (~5 cm) and yellowish.

description: One of the most popular Apistogramma species, offering vivid yellow-orange colouration in males and a compact size suitable for smaller aquaria. Captive-bred specimens are adaptable to a wide range of soft, slightly acidic water. Males hold territories and display intensely; females guard eggs and fry in caves with fierce dedication.
behavior: Males are territorial; will posture and display to rivals but rarely cause serious injury in adequate space. Females become very aggressive toward intruders during brood care. Compatible with small peaceful tetras and other upper-water community fish.
dimorphism: Males significantly larger (up to 8 cm) with vivid yellow-orange colouration and extended fins; females smaller (~5 cm) and yellowish-brown.

description: A striking Apistogramma from the Meta River system of Colombia, popular in the hobby for its intense red-and-yellow male colouration. Captive-bred individuals are adaptable to a range of soft, slightly acidic to neutral water. Males hold small territories; females guard eggs and fry in caves. Compatible with small, peaceful dither fish.
behavior: Territorial males display and posture; rarely seriously aggressive in adequate space. Females devoted cave-brooders, becoming hyper-aggressive during fry care.
dimorphism: Males larger (up to 7 cm) with vivid red-yellow-orange colouration and extended fins; females smaller (~4 cm) with more muted yellow colouration.

description: A vivid lemon-yellow to orange cichlid endemic to the rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika. The leleupi shelters in crevices and hunts invertebrates in the biocover. Pairs are highly territorial, particularly during breeding, and will attack both conspecifics and similar-looking species; only a single pair can be kept in a standard aquarium.
behavior: Crevice-dweller; explores rocky substrates and hides in caves. Highly territorial toward conspecifics and other elongated Tanganyika cichlids. Pair bonds are stable but can turn violent if the pair is incompatible.
dimorphism: Males slightly larger; otherwise difficult to sex outside of breeding behaviour.

description: A medium-sized Tanganyika cichlid distinguished by five bold vertical bars on a pale body. Highly territorial; only a single pair should be kept in a standard aquarium. The five-bar cichlid is more aggressive than some other Lamprologines and should be housed with robust, similarly sized Tanganyika species that can hold their own.
behavior: Highly territorial pair that defends a fixed area aggressively against all intruders. Patrols the open bottom around rocks. Cave-brooders; pairs show intense parental care.
dimorphism: Males slightly larger; sex difficult to determine externally until breeding behaviour is observed.

description: A highly laterally compressed Tanganyika cichlid with a steeply angled forehead that allows it to peer into crevices to hunt invertebrates and small fish. Relatively peaceful toward larger tank mates but will consume any fish small enough to fit in its surprisingly large mouth. Suitable for a well-chosen Tanganyika community.
behavior: Slow-moving crevice hunter. Not particularly territorial except during spawning. Peers into rock crevices and shell beds to extract invertebrates. Can be kept in pairs or small groups in larger aquaria.
dimorphism: Males significantly larger (up to 13 cm); females smaller (~10 cm). Males develop a more pronounced forehead slope.

description: A boldly marked Julie cichlid from the rocky northwestern shores of Lake Tanganyika, with a distinctive checkerboard pattern of white squares on a dark body. Forms a monogamous pair bond; only one pair should occupy a standard aquarium. Compatible with other similarly-sized Tanganyika cichlids in larger setups but highly territorial toward conspecifics.
behavior: Pairs explore rock crevices and the substrate together. Highly territorial toward other Julidochromis and similarly-patterned species. Biparental cave-brooders; both parents guard fry. Females are typically larger than males.
dimorphism: Females are larger than males — unusual among cichlids. Otherwise difficult to sex externally.

description: A slender, torpedo-shaped Julie cichlid from Lake Tanganyika with a cream-and-brown horizontal stripe pattern. Forms tight pair bonds and is highly territorial toward conspecifics; only one pair per standard aquarium. Generally compatible with other Tanganyika cichlids of different body shape. Requires hard, alkaline water.
behavior: Pair explores rocks and open bottom together. Aggressive toward other Julidochromis and similar elongate species. Biparental cave-brooders. Requires rocky decor with multiple crevices.
dimorphism: Males and females look very similar; sexing is difficult without observing spawning behaviour.

description: A mid-water schooling cichlid from Lake Tanganyika that forms large, shimmering shoals in open water. Unlike most cichlids, it is genuinely peaceful and must be kept in schools of eight or more to feel secure. Males display vivid blue and yellow colouration; females are more subdued. Requires a spacious tank with plenty of open swimming space above the rockwork.
behavior: Schooling open-water fish. Males display to each other and females within the school. Mouthbrooder; females incubate eggs in the water column. Non-territorial; will not harass other Tanganyika species.
dimorphism: Males develop vivid blue and/or yellow colouration; females are plain silver or pale yellow.

description: A peaceful and adaptable West African dwarf cichlid with attractive iridescent scaling. One of the most beginner-friendly cichlids available, tolerating a wide pH range and mixing well with many community species. Territorial only during spawning, when pairs guard eggs and fry aggressively. Prefers a planted tank with soft, tannin-stained water.
behavior: Shy and peaceful outside of breeding. Pairs form and substrate-spawn; both parents guard eggs and fry. Prefers shaded areas and dense planting.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more intensely coloured; females develop a more rounded belly and vivid colouration during spawning.

description: A vivid orange-red mbuna from the rocky shores of Lake Malawi. Despite the name, males can be orange, red, or blue depending on population; females are typically orange. An active algae-grazer that should be fed a primarily plant-based diet — high-protein live foods can cause digestive problems. Aggressive and territorial; best kept in harem groups (one male, multiple females).
behavior: Active and territorial rock-dweller. Males defend territories and display to females. Keep in harem groups (1 male : 2–3 females) to spread aggression. Maternal mouthbrooder.
dimorphism: Males are vivid orange-red, blue, or yellow; females are uniformly orange. Males are larger.

description: A slender, torpedo-shaped mbuna from the rocky shores of Lake Malawi. Males are highly aggressive and territorial; they must be kept in a harem (one male with at least four females) to dilute male aggression. Diet should be primarily plant-based to avoid the protein-related disease Malawi bloat. Requires hard, alkaline water.
behavior: Extremely aggressive and territorial. Males compete intensely and will pursue and harass females relentlessly without adequate numbers of females. Maternal mouthbrooder.
dimorphism: Males are larger (up to 14 cm) and more intensely coloured; females (~10 cm) are plainer. Males often show stronger vertical barring.

description: One of South America's most impressive large catfish, patterned with bold black-and-white stripes and spots on a silver body. The tiger shovelnose can exceed a metre in length and requires an institutional-scale aquarium of at least 10,000 litres. A voracious piscivore that will consume any fish small enough to engulf; suitable only for public aquaria or extremely large private facilities.
behavior: Active open-water swimmer that cruises the mid and lower water column. Territorial toward conspecifics and other large catfish. Highly piscivorous; consumes smaller fish with ease. Can become accustomed to dead foods in captivity.

description: A spectacularly fin-adorned pimelodid catfish reaching 60 cm, with a dramatically elongated dorsal fin and bold spotted pattern. Will consume any fish small enough to swallow and is territorial toward its own kind. The sailfin pimelodus requires a 2,500-litre or larger aquarium — a genuine large-facility fish that is unsuitable for the average home aquarium.
behavior: Solitary and territorial; attacks conspecifics and other large catfish. Piscivorous but can be weaned onto dead meaty foods. Most active at night.

description: A large, bold catfish from the rivers of Kerala and the Western Ghats of India. Distinguished by a vivid yellow body with a central black lateral spot and black dorsal stripe. The sun catfish is a predatory species that will consume any fish small enough to engulf, but can be kept with robust, similarly-sized tank mates. Accepts a wide range of meaty prepared and frozen foods.
behavior: Moderately active; patrols the lower water column. Peaceful toward similarly-sized companions but ambushes and swallows smaller fish. Most active at dusk and night.

description: An intricately camouflaged catfish that resembles a dead leaf, with a flat disc-like body and an extremely elongated thin tail. The banjo catfish buries itself in the substrate by day and forages at night. Completely unaggressive; can be kept with any community fish that won't eat it. Accepts most sinking foods and live invertebrates.
behavior: Nocturnal burrower; spends daylight hours buried in sand or leaf litter. Peaceful and largely invisible by day. Will scavenge after lights-out.
dimorphism: Females become noticeably rounder when carrying eggs.

description: A boldly yellow-and-black banded catfish from the rivers of Thailand and the Mekong basin. Shy and nocturnal; hides in caves and driftwood crevices by day. Despite a striking resemblance to the South American bumblebee catfish (Microglanis iheringi), it is a distinct and larger species. Will consume any fish small enough to swallow and should not be housed with small tankmates.
behavior: Nocturnal; hides by day in caves or dense cover. Territorial toward bottom-dwelling conspecifics and similar species. Piscivorous at night; keeps to itself during daylight.

description: A bizarre ambush predator that mimics a piece of debris or dead leaf on the riverbed. The toadhead catfish angles its lure-like barbels to attract small fish, then engulfs them with a lightning-fast strike. Strictly piscivorous and will refuse non-living foods; requires a steady supply of feeder fish or live shrimp. Best kept as a specialist specimen tank.
behavior: Completely motionless ambush predator. Lies on the substrate and uses lure-like movements of its barbels to attract prey. Very rarely moves except to strike. Highly piscivorous.

description: The gulper catfish earns its name from an enormous gape that allows it to swallow prey nearly twice its own body length. A sit-and-wait predator from the blackwater rivers of the Amazon and Orinoco, it must be kept alone or with fish too large to swallow. Requires very soft, acidic water and prefers meaty foods fed just once or twice a week.
behavior: Solitary ambush predator. Hides among submerged wood and roots until prey passes. Extremely distensible stomach allows it to swallow fish almost as large as itself. Feeds infrequently — 1–2 meals per week for adults.

description: One of the most peaceful and sociable Synodontis species, the petricola catfish is endemic to the rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika. Best kept in groups of four or more where it swims confidently and behaves more naturally. An ideal addition to a Tanganyika cichlid community, sharing its preference for hard, alkaline water.
behavior: Social and active compared to most Synodontis. Groups explore the rockwork together. Not particularly territorial; multiple individuals can share hiding spaces.

description: A large, spotted upside-down catfish from the rivers of West Africa capable of reaching 49 cm in the wild, though typical aquarium specimens are around 20 cm. Moderately territorial toward other Synodontis but generally compatible with similarly-sized robust community fish. An unfussy omnivore that accepts most meaty, plant-based, and prepared foods.
behavior: Nocturnal and somewhat secretive; hides in caves or under wood by day. Becomes slightly territorial toward other bottom-dwellers as it matures. Will swim upside-down near the surface, typical of the genus.

description: A large, spotted Synodontis from West and Central Africa, capable of developing a very long upper caudal fin lobe that gives an impressive total length. Typical aquarium specimens stay around 17–18 cm body length. Moderately territorial toward other bottom-dwellers but generally compatible with larger community fish. An adaptable omnivore that accepts most foods.
behavior: Nocturnal; hides during the day in caves or dense cover. Mildly territorial toward other Synodontis and bottom-dwelling catfish. Exhibits the characteristic upside-down swimming posture near the surface.

description: A relatively peaceful Synodontis from the Congo River basin, distinguished by an intricate spotted pattern on a pale background. More tolerant of conspecifics than many of its relatives provided each specimen has its own hiding spot. An adaptable omnivore that accepts most sinking and frozen foods. Best kept with robust mid- to upper-water species that won't compete for bottom territory.
behavior: Nocturnal; hides in caves and among wood by day, emerging at dusk to forage. Territorial but not aggressive — coexists peacefully with others of its species if each has its own refuge.

description: One of the most striking and sought-after plecos, with bold black-and-white zebra stripes. The zebra pleco is Critically Endangered (CITES Appendix II) due to habitat loss from dam construction on the Rio Xingu; all trade specimens should be captive-bred. Requires warm, highly oxygenated, fast-flowing water. Primarily carnivorous and non-algae-eating.
behavior: Nocturnal cave-dweller; stays hidden in crevices by day. Requires strong water movement and excellent oxygenation. Males guard eggs inside caves. Not an algae eater — feed meaty foods.
dimorphism: Males develop odontodes (bristle-like growths) along the pectoral fins and snout; females are rounder when gravid.

description: A striking pleco with a dark body covered in large white polka dots, resembling a ball of snow. Found in the blackwater rivers of Venezuela and Brazil, it requires soft, acidic to neutral water with good water movement. Peaceful with most tankmates but territorial toward other Hypancistrus.
