A cave-spawner where males guard and fan the eggs.
behavior: Nocturnal and reclusive; hides in caves during the day. Territorial with other Hypancistrus. Requires caves or PVC tubes for shelter and spawning. Appreciates stronger flow than most plecos.
dimorphism: Males develop fine odontodes (bristles) around the head and pectoral fins; females are rounder when gravid.

description: A scaleless African catfish capable of generating electric discharges exceeding 350 volts to stun prey and deter predators. Adults can approach 60–90 cm or more in captivity and must be kept alone; the species is aggressive toward any tankmate, including its own kind. All electrical equipment inside the tank must be guarded or replaced with external units to prevent the fish from injuring itself.
behavior: Strictly solitary and aggressive; cannot be housed with any other fish. Nocturnal ambush predator that stuns prey with electric discharges before consuming it. Burrows into soft substrate when available.

description: A slender South American knifefish that generates weak electric fields for navigation and communication, closely related to the popular black ghost knifefish. Reaches about 27 cm, making it more manageable than many knifefish species. Requires a large, dimly lit tank with plenty of hiding spots. Best kept singly or with peaceful, larger tankmates; territorial toward its own kind.
behavior: Nocturnal and cryptic; generates a weak electric organ discharge to navigate in low-light conditions. Territorial toward its own kind but generally peaceful toward fish too large to eat.

description: A compact burrowing spiny eel from South Asia named for its distinctive zigzag stripe along the lateral line and yellow-tipped tail. One of the smaller Macrognathus species at under 20 cm, suitable for a 190-litre aquarium. Semi-aggressive toward very small fish it can swallow. Requires a sand substrate for burrowing and a tightly sealed lid.
behavior: Nocturnal burrower that spends much of its time buried in substrate with only its head exposed. An accomplished escape artist capable of squeezing through very small gaps in the lid.

description: A slender spiny eel from Southeast Asia with distinctive half-bands along its flank. Wild specimens can reach 29 cm but aquarium-kept individuals rarely exceed 20 cm. Generally peaceful except toward very small fish or shrimp that it will consume. Requires sand substrate to burrow into and a tightly sealed aquarium lid.
behavior: Shy, nocturnal burrower that hides during the day. Despite a generally peaceful disposition, adults will prey on neon-tetra-sized fish or shrimp. A skilled escape artist.

description: Widely regarded as the most aggressive cichlid in the hobby, the wolf cichlid from Central America regularly reaches 55–72 cm and will consume any fish it can fit in its mouth. Males are substantially larger than females and intensely territorial; adults must be maintained as solitary specimens or as a bonded pair in tanks of 570 litres or more. Not suitable for beginners.
behavior: Extremely aggressive; males will attack and kill any tankmate unless in a bonded pair. Despite their aggression, wolf cichlids are intelligent and often become interactive with their keeper. Will destroy plants and rearrange décor.
dimorphism: Males grow significantly larger (up to 72 cm) with a more pronounced jaw; females are smaller (typically under 45 cm) and display more restrained colouration.

description: A massive, highly aggressive cichlid from Colombia and Panama that regularly reaches 45–54 cm and will consume any fish it can fit in its mouth. Closely related to the jaguar cichlid but considered by many hobbyists to be even more aggressive. Must be kept alone or as a verified bonded pair in tanks of 570 litres or more; not suitable for beginners.
behavior: Extremely aggressive and piscivorous; will attack and kill most tankmates. Males are particularly intolerant of conspecifics. Despite their aggression, umbee cichlids are intelligent and develop strong bonds with their keeper.
dimorphism: Males grow larger and develop a more pronounced hump on the forehead with age; females are typically smaller and less intensely coloured.

description: A robust Central American cichlid named for the bold black vertical bar on its flank, contrasted by a red or orange belly. Primarily herbivorous in the wild, grazing on algae, plant matter, and fallen fruits. Fairly aggressive and best kept singly or as a mated pair; will bully smaller tankmates and dig up the substrate.
behavior: Aggressive and territorial; digs in the substrate and may uproot plants. Primarily herbivorous but will eat meaty foods in captivity. Best kept singly or as a known bonded pair.

description: A slender, elongated piscivore from the blackwater rivers of Brazil's Negro basin, resembling a freshwater barracuda with spotted flanks and a pointed snout. Formerly classified as Crenicichla lenticulata, reclassified to Lugubria lenticulata in 2023. Extremely territorial and aggressive; will consume any fish small enough to swallow. Requires large tanks with plenty of cover and slightly acidic water.
behavior: Ambush predator that lurks among vegetation and driftwood before striking at prey fish. Highly territorial; males will attack conspecifics on sight. Best maintained as a single specimen or as a carefully managed mated pair.

description: A charming dwarf cichlid from the Amazon basin named for the distinctive curved line below its eye that resembles a smile. One of the most beginner-friendly cichlids, peaceful enough for planted community tanks, and a devoted parent that guards eggs and fry attentively. Typically kept as pairs or small groups in well-planted aquariums.
behavior: Peaceful and pair-bonding; forms monogamous pairs that guard eggs and fry vigorously but rarely bother other tankmates. Will not uproot plants. Males may occasionally spar with other males.
dimorphism: Males are larger and develop more intense colouration; females show a rounded body and a rosy belly when ready to spawn.

description: A massive Southeast Asian bagrid catfish that rivals the South American redtail catfish in size, reaching 130 cm and reportedly claimed to be among the most aggressive freshwater fish in the world. Inhabits large upland rivers in the Mekong and Chao Phraya systems. Only suitable for public aquaria or very large private facilities; home tanks are entirely inadequate for adult specimens.
behavior: Highly aggressive and territorial toward other fish; voracious predator of crustaceans and fish. Active and powerful swimmer that will rearrange all décor. Juveniles are sold in the trade but rapidly outgrow home aquariums.

description: Europe's largest freshwater predator, the wels catfish can exceed 260 cm and live for decades in large temperate rivers from the Rhine to the Caspian Sea. A cool-water species (4–20 °C) that is entirely unsuitable for tropical aquariums; adults require public-aquarium facilities. Feeds on fish, crustaceans, frogs, and occasionally water birds.
behavior: Solitary, nocturnal ambush predator. Uses barbels to detect prey in low-visibility conditions. Highly territorial toward conspecifics; adults must be kept alone.

description: A slender, active pimelodid catfish from South American rivers including the Amazon and Orinoco, closely related to the pictus catfish. Reaches about 35 cm and should be kept in groups of three or more, as solitary individuals become stressed. Will consume any fish small enough to fit in its mouth; best paired with medium-to-large tankmates.
behavior: Active schooling catfish that patrols the mid-water and bottom. Gregarious — groups of 3+ are noticeably calmer and less prone to erratic dashes. Will eat very small fish and invertebrates at night.

description: A striking West African catfish named for the elongated, feather-like first dorsal ray that waves when the fish swims. Reaches up to 30 cm but typically stays 15–20 cm in captivity. Hardy and adaptable, it is one of the most popular Synodontis species, though it may eat very small fish. Best kept with similarly sized tankmates.
behavior: Nocturnal and crepuscular; hides in caves and under driftwood by day. May swim upside-down, typical of Synodontis. Peaceful toward most fish but will consume very small species or invertebrates.
dimorphism: Females typically grow larger and are noticeably rounder when gravid; males are more slender.

description: A spotted Lake Tanganyika catfish famous for its remarkable brood parasitism — it sneaks its eggs into mouthbrooding cichlid clutches, where the host parents inadvertently raise the catfish fry. Reaches about 28 cm and should be kept in groups of three or more. Requires the hard, alkaline water of Lake Tanganyika.
behavior: Gregarious and active; groups are noticeably bolder than solitary individuals. Best kept with Tanganyikan mouthbrooding cichlids whose nests it will parasitise. Uses sharp pectoral spines defensively.

description: A small, striking pleco from the Orinoco basin of Venezuela and Colombia, known in the hobby as L239 or 'Blue Panaque.' Its slate-blue body with pale spots makes it one of the more desirable L-number plecos. Primarily an aufwuchs grazer that requires surfaces covered in biofilm; needs warm, well-oxygenated water and slightly acidic conditions. Territorial toward other bottom-dwellers.
behavior: Grazes aufwuchs from rock and wood surfaces; needs extensive grazing area. Territorial toward other plecos and bottom-dwelling fish in smaller tanks. Requires high water oxygenation and moderate current.
dimorphism: Males develop odontodes (bristle-like growths) along the snout and pectoral fins; females are plainer.

description: A small, strikingly patterned pleco from the Rio Tapajós system, popular in the hobby as L134. Males develop bold black-and-yellow reticulated markings reminiscent of a poison dart frog, while females are slightly rounder and less vibrant. Males are territorial toward each other and will compete aggressively for caves, but are otherwise peaceful with fish occupying other parts of the tank. Prefers warm, well-oxygenated water with driftwood and caves for shelter.
behavior: Cave-dwelling and territorial between males; a single male can be kept with females or other non-competing fish. Nocturnal but becomes active at dusk. Requires hiding spots.
dimorphism: Males have a broader, flatter head and more vivid markings; females are rounder in the abdomen.

description: A vivid lime-green pleco from the fast-flowing, rocky rivers of the Orinoco basin, sold under the L-number L200. Its bright green body covered in pale spots fades somewhat with age but remains striking in juveniles and sub-adults. Peaceful with fish in other parts of the tank but territorial toward other plecos and benthic species. Requires well-oxygenated water with moderate flow, plenty of flat rocks, and supplemental feeding with algae wafers and occasional protein.
behavior: Territorial toward other plecos but ignores mid-water fish. Prefers high-flow environments with rocky substrate. Nocturnal; more active after lights-out.
dimorphism: Males develop inter-opercular odontodes (spiny bristles on the cheeks); females are generally plainer.

description: One of the most sought-after L-number plecos (L014), the Sunshine is a large catfish with vivid yellow-orange spots on a dark base that are most intense in juveniles. Adults reach 30 cm and require a very large tank with high flow and warm water mimicking the Xingu's specific conditions. Males become territorial with conspecifics and similar plecos. A premium fish commanding high prices and demanding advanced keeper commitment due to tank size, flow requirements, and restricted wild collection.
behavior: Territorial among males; best kept singly or as a single male with females in a very large tank. Requires strong water flow and high oxygen saturation. Grazes algae from rocks and accepts sinking carnivore pellets.
dimorphism: Males are larger, develop odontodes on the cheeks and pectoral spines; females are less ornamented and broader in the abdomen.

origin: Amazon, Madeira, and Tocantins basins, Brazil
description: A striking dark pleco covered in small white-to-cream spots resembling a galaxy, sold as L029 and sometimes called Galaxy plec. Named for its fang-like teeth, which are adapted for prying snails and crustaceans from rocky substrates. Grows to around 25 cm and is peaceful toward mid-water tankmates but territorial with other bottom-dwelling plecos and similar benthic species. Nocturnal and strongly cave-oriented; requires ample hiding places and moderate water flow.
behavior: Strongly territorial toward benthic species with similar coloration; ignores fish in higher water column. Primarily nocturnal; needs caves or PVC tubes to shelter in during the day.
dimorphism: Males develop pronounced inter-opercular odontodes and are generally slimmer; females are broader in the abdomen when in breeding condition.

origin: Amazon and Orinoco basins, South America
description: One of the most commonly sold plecos, the Sailfin is distinguished by its enormous fan-like dorsal fin and leopard-spot patterning that can shift between light and dark with mood. Juveniles are cheap and widely available, but adults reach 50 cm and require a very large tank — a fact rarely communicated at the point of sale. Hardy and peaceful toward most tankmates but territorial with other large plecos. A prolific grazer that produces significant waste; robust filtration and a large footprint are essential.
behavior: Mostly nocturnal but will graze during the day in established tanks. Peaceful with other species; territorial toward similarly-sized plecos. Gulps surface air if oxygen levels drop.
dimorphism: Difficult to sex visually; males tend to be larger and develop a small papilla near the anal opening.

description: A small, attractively marked cory from the Madeira River drainage, named for the smudge-like spots that dot its pale body. Also called the Violet Cory for the subtle iridescent sheen some individuals display. Peaceful and undemanding, it works well in a community tank and is happiest in a tight-knit shoal foraging over fine sand. Prefers slightly soft, gently acidic to neutral water.
behavior: Gregarious bottom-forager; shy when kept in small numbers, bold and active in groups of six or more. Peaceful with all other fish.
dimorphism: Females are broader and rounder in the abdomen; males are slimmer.

description: A robust, adaptable cory from the Amazon borderlands of Peru and Brazil, historically placed in Corydoras but reclassified to Brochis. Reaches around 7 cm — slightly larger than most beginner cories — and is highly tolerant of a wide pH range, making it a forgiving choice for community aquariums. Gregarious and peaceful, it benefits from a shoal of six or more and a fine sandy substrate for natural foraging behaviour.
behavior: Active bottom-forager that works sand with its sensitive barbels. Gregarious; displays bolder, more natural behaviour in groups. Peaceful with all tankmates.
dimorphism: Females are broader across the belly, particularly when carrying eggs; males are slimmer and slightly smaller.

description: An attractive, slim-bodied cory from the upper Amazon with a distinctive green-to-brown iridescent sheen. Unlike most cories it frequently swims through the mid and upper water column rather than staying at the substrate, making it an interesting addition to a community tank. Peaceful and sociable, it should be kept in groups of six or more over fine sand. Tolerant of a broad temperature range, which makes it suitable for cooler community tanks.
behavior: Atypical for cories — regularly swims up into mid-water and upper levels. Still forages at the substrate but spends more time off the bottom than most species. Peaceful and gregarious.
dimorphism: Females are noticeably rounder in the abdomen; males are more streamlined.

origin: Upper Amazon, Solimões, and Rio Negro basins, Brazil/Peru
description: A commonly available cory recognised by its warm rusty-orange to coppery colouration along the back and sides, contrasting with a pale belly. Found across a large range spanning the upper Amazon and Negro systems, it is one of the more adaptable species and tolerates a reasonably wide temperature and pH range. Best kept in groups of six or more over a fine sand substrate; peaceful and compatible with most community fish.
behavior: Active bottom-forager; gregarious and noticeably bolder in larger shoals. Occasionally makes short dashes to the surface to gulp air — a normal behaviour for armoured catfish. Handle with care as pectoral spines can pierce skin.
dimorphism: Females are broader and heavier-bodied, especially when gravid; males are slimmer.

description: A small blackwater cory from the upper Rio Negro, frequently confused with Corydoras adolfoi (the Adolfo's cory) due to nearly identical markings — both share a bold black eye-stripe and orange shoulder patch. The duplicareus has a slightly smaller orange area. Like its look-alike, it demands the soft, acidic conditions of the Negro River and is less tolerant of hard or alkaline water than most cories. Best kept in a dedicated blackwater setup with dimmed lighting and tannin-rich water.
behavior: Gregarious and peaceful; confidence increases markedly in groups of six or more. Sensitive to poor water quality; requires very clean, well-filtered water. Pectoral spines can lock extended as a defence — handle with care.
dimorphism: Females are noticeably rounder in the belly; males are slimmer and slightly smaller.

description: A small, subtly attractive cory from the Mamoré River drainage in Bolivia and western Brazil, identified by a dark blotch at the base of the dorsal fin and mottled body patterning. Peaceful and sociable like all corys, it benefits from a group of six or more and a fine sand substrate. Moderately tolerant of water conditions, making it approachable for hobbyists comfortable with standard cory care.
behavior: Gregarious bottom-forager; shyer in small numbers, more active and confident in groups. Peaceful with all tankmates.
dimorphism: Females are broader across the abdomen, especially when gravid; males are slimmer.

origin: Coastal drainages, Rio de Janeiro to Santa Catarina, Brazil
description: The largest of the commonly kept cories, the Bearded Cory stands out for its subtropical temperature requirements (16–24°C) and striking sexual dimorphism. Males develop elaborate facial bristles and mane-like odontodes along the snout and pectoral girdle; females are much plainer. Males are strongly territorial with each other and should not be housed together in small tanks. Incompatible with most tropical fish due to its preference for cooler water.
behavior: Males stage dramatic displays and will fight if two are placed in a small tank without ample territory. Females and juveniles are peaceful and gregarious. All individuals forage the substrate for small invertebrates.
dimorphism: Pronounced: males grow significantly larger and develop dense facial odontodes (bristles) along snout, cheeks, and pectoral spines; females are smaller, rounder in the body, and lack bristles.

origin: Mekong, Chao Phraya, and Mae Klong basins, Southeast Asia
description: A torpedo-shaped loach from Southeast Asia, named for the bold dark dorsal stripe running from nose to tail reminiscent of a skunk. Despite its attractive markings, the Skunk Loach is notably combative: it forms complex social hierarchies within its group and regularly nips the fins of tankmates, particularly long-finned species. Must be kept in groups of six or more to dilute in-group aggression, and should only be combined with robust, similarly-sized fish.
behavior: Forms dominant hierarchies within groups; combative and may nip fins of long-finned or slow-moving tankmates. Primarily nocturnal. More stable in larger groups (10+) than in minimum groups.
dimorphism: Females tend to be slightly larger and rounder-bodied when mature; otherwise difficult to sex visually.

description: A sociable, active loach from the Salween River system in Myanmar and Thailand, covered in a vivid pattern of dark spots and bars on a pale body that shifts with age and mood. Reaches 15 cm and requires a spacious tank with a large group of conspecifics to maintain a stable social hierarchy; isolated individuals or very small groups become stressed and aggressive. Compatible with peaceful to semi-aggressive fish of similar size; will eat snails and small invertebrates readily.
behavior: Active and playful; forages continuously along substrate and enjoys exploring caves and crevices. Social hierarchy forms within the group; instability arises in groups fewer than 5-6. Will eat snails as a side benefit.
dimorphism: Females are broader-bodied; otherwise difficult to distinguish visually.

description: A prized, historically hard-to-find Peruvian cory marked with two dark saddle-like blotches over a speckled body. Peaceful and gregarious, it must be kept in a group over soft sand that protects its delicate barbels. Prefers slightly cooler, clean water and is sensitive to elevated nitrate, so frequent water changes matter.
behavior: A calm, social bottom forager that sifts sand for food and is most confident in a shoal of six or more. Dashes to the surface for an occasional gulp of air.
reproduction: Egg layer. Pairs deposit sticky eggs on glass and plants in the classic Corydoras 'T-position'; cooler water changes can trigger spawning.
dimorphism: Mature females are noticeably rounder and broader-bodied when viewed from above.

description: A hardy, fast-swimming Australian rainbowfish with a deep, laterally compressed body and a bold dark midline stripe; mature males develop vivid red, blue and orange depending on the population. Very peaceful but active, so it wants length to swim and a group to settle skittish individuals. Males display and spar with raised fins but rarely cause harm.
behavior: An active mid-water schooler that colours up best when males compete in a group; jumpy, so keep the tank well covered.
reproduction: Continuous egg scatterer that deposits sticky eggs on fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over many days.
dimorphism: Males are deeper-bodied and far more intensely coloured; females stay slimmer and more silver.

description: A small, pearly Sri Lankan micropredator that flushes orange-red to pinkish when settled and well-fed. Retiring and easily intimidated, it does poorly with boisterous tankmates and needs a quiet, well-planted, soft and slightly acidic tank in a decent-sized group. A jewel of a fish in a species or quiet blackwater setup.
behavior: A shy, loosely shoaling micropredator; males display to one another and colour up most strongly in a calm, shaded tank.
reproduction: Egg scatterer that spawns among fine-leaved plants; adults eat the eggs, which are not guarded.
dimorphism: Males are slimmer with more intense red-orange colour and extended finnage; females are rounder and plainer.

description: A large, deep-bodied rasbora of brick-red to coppery orange marked with two dark blotches on the flank, native to peat-swamp and blackwater habitats. Peaceful but a comparatively loose shoaler that can be shy; it needs soft, acidic water, plenty of swimming length and a sizeable group to look its best. Bigger and more demanding than the typical community rasbora.
behavior: A roaming, loosely shoaling open-water swimmer that stays bolder and more colourful in a group of eight or more.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among vegetation; not commonly bred in the home aquarium and most stock is wild-caught.
dimorphism: Difficult to sex; mature females are deeper-bodied and fuller, males slightly more intensely coloured.

description: A slender, silvery rasbora crossed by a brilliant red-orange horizontal stripe that glows most vividly over dark substrate in soft, acidic water. Peaceful and strictly schooling, it can be timid and its colour washes out in hard or bright conditions, so a shaded, well-planted blackwater-style tank with a good-sized group suits it best.
behavior: A tight, active schooler that holds the upper-middle water column; nervous when kept in small numbers and shows its red line best in a large group.
reproduction: Egg scatterer that deposits eggs among fine-leaved plants; adults do not guard the spawn.
dimorphism: Subtle; mature females are rounder-bodied while males tend to show a slightly brighter, cleaner stripe.

description: A long, slender, downward-faced loach with an elongated 'horse-like' snout that spends its day buried to the eyes in sand. Peaceful and shy, it needs a deep bed of fine sand to sift and burrow in; sharp gravel will damage it. Reaches around 25 cm, so despite its calm nature it needs a long tank with plenty of floor space.
behavior: A sand-sifting burrower that vanishes into the substrate when startled and forages by passing mouthfuls of sand through its gills. Forms loose shoals and is calmer kept in a small group, though it is also commonly kept singly.
reproduction: Not bred in the home aquarium; effectively all stock is wild-caught and its spawning behaviour is undocumented.
dimorphism: No reliable external differences; sexing is essentially impossible outside of spawning condition.

description: A tiny, brilliant lime-to-neon-green nano fish from forest streams in Thailand, often sold as the neon green rasbora. Very peaceful and strictly schooling, it shows best in a large group in a mature, well-planted tank. Its small mouth needs correspondingly small live, frozen and crushed dried foods.
behavior: An active, tightly schooling micro-fish that occurs naturally in groups of dozens; nervous and washed-out unless kept in good numbers.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants and mosses; adults do not guard the eggs.
dimorphism: Subtle; mature females are slightly larger and rounder-bodied than the slimmer males.

description: One of the smallest fish in the hobby, an emerald-green micro-rasbora from the Irrawaddy region of Myanmar barely over a centimetre long. Peaceful and shoaling, it suits a quiet, planted nano tank with no boisterous tankmates and only the tiniest of foods. Easily out-competed and intimidated, so best in a species or dedicated nano community.
behavior: A delicate, tightly shoaling nano fish that holds together in open water and fades in colour when kept alone or stressed.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among mosses and fine plants; not commonly bred and adults will eat the eggs.
dimorphism: Very hard to sex; gravid females show a slightly fuller belly.

description: A slim, surface-hugging livebearer named for its long, fixed lower jaw and the way rival males lock beaks and wrestle. Strictly a top-dweller, it needs surface space, a tight lid (it is a determined jumper) and floating plants. Best kept with more females than males to spread out male sparring, and fed small surface foods like fruit flies and insect larvae.
behavior: A surface insectivore that cruises just under the film waiting for prey; males spar by locking beaks but rarely injure one another given space.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to small broods of fully formed fry after a long gestation, and may use superfetation.
dimorphism: Males are smaller and slimmer with a modified anal fin (andropodium); females are larger and fuller-bodied.

description: A hardy, peaceful livebearer from Haiti whose mature males develop an arched, humpbacked profile and a tall, banner-like dorsal fin. It thrives in hard, alkaline water and grazes readily, so it needs vegetable matter or algae alongside regular foods. An easy, characterful community fish for harder-water setups.
behavior: A calm, constantly grazing livebearer; males display their sailfin dorsal and pursue females but cause no real harm in a group with enough females.
reproduction: Livebearer; females drop broods of free-swimming fry every few weeks and may eat their own young in bare tanks.
dimorphism: Males develop the humped back and enlarged dorsal fin and stay smaller; females are larger, deeper-bellied and plainer.

description: A small, secretive 'chameleon fish' that shifts between blues, greens and near-black with its mood, lurking among caves and plants. Males are territorial around a chosen cave but it is otherwise shy and slow-moving, so it does poorly with fast or boisterous tankmates. It largely ignores dry food and needs small live or frozen foods to thrive, and appreciates cooler, subtropical temperatures.
behavior: A retiring, cave-loving ambusher; males stake out and defend a territory around a cave or crevice and display intense colour when courting or sparring.
reproduction: Cave spawner; the male lures a female into a cave, embraces her to release eggs, then guards the eggs and fry alone.
dimorphism: Males are larger, more intensely coloured with a slightly concave belly; females are smaller, rounder and much drabber.

description: A hardy, elegant tetra with an iridescent body, a bold dark lateral stripe and striking blue-green eyes. Males extend the central rays of the tail into a trident and form a loose hierarchy, sparring with display rather than real aggression, so it makes a peaceful, characterful community fish. Best kept in a group with a few females per male.
behavior: An active midwater shoaler; dominant males stake out small territories and display to rivals but rarely cause damage.
reproduction: Egg scatterer that deposits eggs among fine-leaved plants; a continuous spawner that will eat its own eggs.
dimorphism: Males are larger and more colourful with blue-green eyes and an extended trident caudal; females are rounder with bluish eyes and shorter fins.

description: A close cousin of the emperor tetra, distinguished by glowing red eyes and a shifting purple-to-gold sheen over a dark lateral stripe. Males grow long trident caudal filaments and extended fins and defend small territories from each other, but it stays peaceful toward other community fish. Less common and a touch more delicate than the emperor tetra, it does best in soft, gently acidic water.
behavior: A midwater shoaler; rival males display and spar over small territories while remaining peaceful toward tankmates.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops; eggs and fry are not guarded.
dimorphism: Males have red eyes, brighter colour and long trident caudal and fin extensions; females are smaller, rounder and plainer.

description: A small, peaceful tetra with a violet-blue sheen and a dark lateral stripe ending in a tail spot, resembling a compact emperor tetra. It shoals readily and only nips fins when kept in too-small a group, so numbers matter. An easy, attractive fish for a soft, gently acidic community tank.
behavior: A peaceful midwater shoaler; males display to one another, and the species becomes nippy only in groups that are too small.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants; eggs are not guarded and may be eaten by the adults.
dimorphism: Males are more intensely blue-purple with a blue adipose fin; females are rounder with a reddish adipose fin.

description: A hardy, very peaceful schooling tetra marked by a black band sitting beneath a bright iridescent green-white stripe, topped with a red glint in the upper eye. It is undemanding, adaptable and active, making it one of the easiest community shoalers. Looks its best in a planted tank in a group of six or more.
behavior: A peaceful, active midwater shoaler that tightens into a school when kept in good numbers.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants; readily bred and will eat its own eggs.
dimorphism: Females are larger and noticeably rounder-bodied; males are slimmer. Otherwise the sexes look alike.

description: The smallest of the neon-type tetras, with a near full-length iridescent blue-green stripe and only a trace of red. A peaceful nano shoaler that craves warm, soft, acidic blackwater conditions, so it is a little more demanding than the common neon. It looks spectacular as a dense school of eight or more in a planted tank.
behavior: A tight-schooling, active midwater nano fish that shows best colour and security in large groups.
reproduction: Egg scatterer in very soft, acidic water; considered challenging to breed.
dimorphism: Females are slightly larger and rounder-bodied; sexual differences are otherwise subtle.

description: A translucent reddish tetra with a dark shoulder spot and red fins, the male carrying a tall, banner-like dorsal. It is very peaceful and forms loose dominance hierarchies in which males display rather than fight. It prefers slightly cooler water than most tropical tetras and looks superb in a planted group.
behavior: A peaceful midwater shoaler; males display their tall dorsal fins and form temporary, harmless pecking orders.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants; eggs are not guarded and may be eaten.
dimorphism: Males are redder with a tall, pointed dorsal fin; females are rounder with a black-and-white marked dorsal.

description: A rosy-pink, deep-bodied tetra with red fins and a black blotch in the dorsal, the male developing a tall sail-like dorsal fin. Very peaceful, it suits any calm community but can be outcompeted by boisterous or much larger tankmates. A handsome, easy shoaler for soft, gently acidic water.
behavior: A peaceful midwater shoaler; rival males flare their extended dorsal fins at one another without real aggression.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants; eggs and fry are not guarded.
dimorphism: Males have a tall, sickle-shaped dorsal fin and brighter colour; females are rounder with a shorter dorsal.

description: A member of the rosy tetra complex with a pale pinkish body, a black dorsal blotch and bright white tips to the dorsal, anal and pelvic fins. It is very peaceful and undemanding, making an easy community shoaler. Males show off taller, white-edged fins, so a small group looks its best.
behavior: A peaceful midwater shoaler; males display their white-tipped fins to each other harmlessly.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants; eggs are not guarded.
dimorphism: Males have longer, white-tipped dorsal and anal fins; females are rounder-bodied with shorter fins.

description: A blackwater rosy-complex tetra, silvery-pink with a small red 'blood' spot on the flank and a black-and-white dorsal fin, milder in pattern than the true bleeding heart. It is generally peaceful, though males spar mildly over display space, so keep it in a group. Best in soft, acidic, gently stained water.
behavior: A peaceful midwater shoaler; males display their extended dorsal fins and occasionally squabble without harm.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants in soft, acidic water; eggs are not guarded.
dimorphism: Males have a longer, sickle-shaped dorsal fin; females are rounder and fuller-bodied.

description: A slender, understated tetra named for the tricolour stripe running along its body: a gold line above red above black, like a flag. Very peaceful and active, it does best in a shoal in soft, gently acidic water and a planted tank that shows off its subtle colours. An easy community fish that is sometimes overlooked.
behavior: A peaceful, active midwater shoaler that schools more tightly in good numbers.
reproduction: Egg scatterer among fine-leaved plants; spawns best in soft, acidic water and will eat its own eggs.
dimorphism: Females are rounder and fuller-bodied; males are slimmer. The sexes are otherwise very similar.

description: One of the smallest members of its genus, this delicate tetra from the upper Peruvian Amazon reaches barely 3 cm. Sensitive to water-quality fluctuations, it is best kept in a mature, well-planted tank with stable, slightly acidic conditions. Shoals of 8 or more bring out its best colours and reduce shyness.

A small, hardy tetra from the Atlantic coastal rivers of southeastern Brazil, sometimes called the Goldspotted Tetra for the bright gold-and-black shoulder marking. Its tolerance of a wide pH range makes it more forgiving than many tetras. Best kept in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted community tank.

A petite tetra from the Araguari and Oyapock river basins of northeastern Brazil and French Guiana, named for the prominent dark lateral blotch resembling a coffee bean. Peaceful and well-suited to planted community tanks; prefers soft, slightly acidic water and should always be kept in groups of 6 or more.

A medium-sized, active tetra from the Amazon, Negro, and Orinoco river basins and Guiana coastal drainages, named for the vivid red patch at the base of the caudal fin. Hardy and peaceful, it thrives in groups of 6 or more in a planted community tank with soft, slightly acidic water.

A compact, diamond-shaped tetra from the Peruvian Amazon near Iquitos, prized for the rich burgundy-red and gold wash on its flanks. Hardy and beginner-friendly, it does best in soft to moderately soft, slightly acidic water and should be kept in a shoal of at least 6. Also sold under the synonym Hemigrammus pulcher (Pretty tetra).

A striking tetra from the rivers of the Guianas and the Amazon basin. Wild-caught specimens display a shimmering gold metallic sheen caused by a superficial microsporidian coating; captive-bred fish lack this but remain attractive. Peaceful and easy to keep in a planted community tank. Best in shoals of 8 or more.

The Brilliant Rummynose Tetra (formerly Hemigrammus bleheri) has the most vivid red headband of the three rummynose species, extending well past the gill plate onto the body. Native to the Negro and Meta river systems, it is more demanding than typical community tetras — requiring stable, soft, slightly acidic water. Shows its best colours and schooling behaviour in large groups of 10 or more.

A compact iridescent tetra from the Amazon basin, also called the January Tetra. When kept in soft, slightly acidic water and a proper shoal, the silvery-green lateral stripe glows brilliantly. Peaceful and well-suited to mature, planted community tanks; groups of 6 are the minimum and 10 or more bring out the best schooling behaviour.

A lively, fast-swimming tetra from the Paraná river system of Argentina and Paraguay, instantly recognizable by the blood-red fins on an otherwise silvery body. One of the most cold-tolerant tetras, handling temperatures as low as 18°C, and adaptable to a wide pH range — an excellent beginner fish. May nip the fins of slow or veil-finned tankmates, so choose companions accordingly.

A vibrant green-and-red tetra from the Paraguay, Paraná, and Uruguay river drainages, also known as the Green Fire Tetra. Hardy and adaptable, but an active fin-nipper that should be kept with robust, fast-moving species rather than slow or veil-finned fish. Best in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted tank.

A tiny, rosy tetra from the Amazon and Paraguay river basins related to the bloodfin tetras. Its pinkish body tones are reminiscent of dawn light. Peaceful, though it may nip the fins of slow or veil-finned tankmates. Prefers the upper water column and should be kept in groups of 6 or more.

A slender, near-transparent tetra from the Amazon basin with vivid red fins — essentially a glass-bodied version of the classic bloodfin tetra. Peaceful and undemanding, it prefers the upper water column and is an active jumper, so a tight-fitting lid is essential. Best kept in groups of 6 or more.

A hardy, adaptable tetra from the river basins of central and southern South America (also listed as Moenkhausia sanctaefilomenae), easily identified by its large red eye and black-and-yellow tail marking. Tolerant of a wide pH range and an excellent beginner fish. May nip fins of slower tankmates; groups of 6 or more reduce this tendency.

A transparent-bodied tetra from the Amazon basin and Guiana region (also listed as Moenkhausia oligolepis), prized for its near-glassy appearance with a vivid red eye. Aquarium specimens typically reach 5 cm; wild fish can grow considerably larger. Peaceful and easy to care for; best kept in groups of 6 or more with a tight-fitting lid as it is an active jumper.

A large, deep-bodied silver tetra from the Orinoco river system and the coastal rivers of the Guianas. More active and boisterous than most tetras; best kept with other robust, fast-moving species. Needs adequate space, a school of 6 or more, and a well-covered tank — it is an active jumper.

A highly aggressive, scale-eating tetra from the Amazon and Tocantins river basins. Named for its protruding teeth, it attacks and strips the scales of virtually any silver-bodied fish — making it incompatible with nearly all community tankmates. Must be kept as a large, species-only school of at least 12; in smaller groups individuals are singled out and harassed to death. A specialist fish best suited to experienced keepers.

A vivid electric-blue tetra from the Amazon basin, popular for its intense metallic iridescence. Generally peaceful in a suitable shoal but can turn nippy toward long-finned or slow-moving tankmates, especially when underschooled. Undemanding in care; best paired with other active, similarly sized fish rather than docile long-finned species.

A tiny, jewel-red nano tetra from the blackwater headwaters of the upper Meta River in Colombia. Requires very soft, acidic water (pH 4–6.5) and benefits from daily small live or frozen foods (Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, Moina) rather than dried food alone. Not suitable for a typical community tank due to its small size and specialist water requirements; best kept in groups of 8 or more in a dedicated blackwater nano setup.

One of the smallest aquarium fish in the hobby at just 1.5–2 cm, from tannin-stained blackwater rivers of the upper Rio Negro basin in Brazil. Requires very soft, highly acidic water (pH 4–6.5) that is demanding to replicate, plus small live or frozen microprey alongside dried foods. Strictly a blackwater species or nano-tank specialist — unsuitable for mixed community aquariums.

A small, round-bodied characin from the forest streams of Sierra Leone, West Africa — one of the very few African characins kept in the hobby. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water and benefits from daily portions of small live or frozen foods (brine shrimp nauplii, Daphnia, Moina) alongside dry food. Keep in groups of 8 or more in a planted nano tank with similarly small, peaceful tankmates.

A fast-moving, active tetra from the Lower Niger and Ogun river systems of Nigeria, recognised by its vivid red eye and iridescent flanks. Peaceful but too boisterous for shy or slow-moving tankmates; ideally housed with other active West African species. Requires a long tank with ample open water and should be kept in schools of 10 or more.

A lively, shoaling tetra from the Congo River basin, named for the striking yellow flush on its tail. Males develop extended fin filaments as they mature. Requires a long tank with open swimming space and should be kept in a mixed-sex group of 8 or more to display natural schooling behaviour. Undemanding in diet; a varied mix of live, frozen, and dry foods brings out the best coloration.

A large, fast-swimming schooler from the Atlantic-draining rivers of West and Central Africa (Gambia to the Congo basin). Males develop spectacular elongated fin filaments. Boisterous and an active feeder, it will predate fish small enough to fit in its mouth — best kept with other large robust African species. Keep in groups of 6 or more in a spacious, well-covered tank. Also sold as Brycinus longipinnis.

Famous for its remarkable spawning behaviour: pairs leap clear of the water to deposit eggs on overhanging leaves or the tank lid, and the male periodically splashes the clutch with his tail to keep it moist until hatching. The tank must have a tight-fitting lid and space above the waterline. Peaceful and easy to feed; keep in a group of 8 or more with floating plants and subdued lighting.

A subtly spotted copella from the Amazon and Orinoco river systems, closely related to the splash tetra. Peaceful and undemanding, it inhabits the upper water column and appreciates dense floating vegetation with subdued lighting. Keep in groups of 8 or more; a tight-fitting lid is essential as it leaps readily. Best offered small live or frozen foods alongside good-quality dry food.

A small, fan-finned characin from the subtropical rivers of Argentina and southern Brazil, requiring notably cool water (15–22°C) that makes it incompatible with most tropical fish. Its spotted body and upturned mouth reveal a surface-oriented micropredator. Best kept in a dedicated cool-water setup with other temperature-tolerant species; feeds readily on small live and frozen foods.

An unusual characin from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, named for the male's large, sail-like dorsal fin that he erects during courtship and territorial displays. Peaceful toward other species but males challenge each other; best kept as one male with multiple females or in small groups with plenty of cover. Requires soft, acidic water and prefers small live or frozen foods over dried alone.

A bottom-dwelling characin from the fast-flowing rivers of the São Francisco and upper Paraná drainages in Brazil. Unlike typical tetras it rests on the substrate and hops using its pectoral fins as props. Needs cool water (18–24°C) and strong current with high dissolved oxygen, making it incompatible with standard tropical setups. Keep in groups of 8 or more over a sandy substrate with powerful filtration.

One of the most vividly coloured pencilfish, with males displaying an intense coral-red body and delicate finnage. Native to Peru, it requires soft, acidic water and benefits from daily small live or frozen foods. Males spar regularly — keep in groups of 10 or more with a higher female ratio and plenty of cover to disperse aggression. A rewarding species for a planted blackwater biotope.

A deep-purple and crimson pencilfish from Peru, noted as one of the more aggressive pencilfish — males challenge each other more intensely than most Nannostomus. Requires soft, acidic blackwater conditions and small live or frozen foods. Keep in groups of 10 or more with a high female-to-male ratio and dense planting to distribute male aggression. Best suited to a specialist blackwater nano setup.

A distinctive pencilfish from the central and upper Amazon basin that swims at a characteristic oblique downward angle — a behaviour unique among the Nannostomus. Very peaceful and timid; unsuitable for boisterous community tanks. Best kept in a planted nano setup with other small, gentle species, soft acidic water, and small live or frozen foods. Keep in groups of 10 or more.

A slender pencilfish from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, named for the single dark horizontal stripe along its body. Very peaceful and timid; best kept in a planted nano setup away from boisterous species. Requires soft, acidic water and small live or frozen foods alongside quality dry food. Keep in groups of 10 or more.

A compact, attractively patterned pencilfish from the Mazaruni River system in Guyana. Very peaceful with other species but males display and spar regularly; fin nipping within the group is common and usually harmless. Requires soft, acidic water and small live or frozen foods. Keep in groups of 10 or more with visual cover to reduce male conflict.

A relatively large pencilfish from the Demerara River basin in Guyana. Very peaceful and timid; best housed in a calmer planted setup rather than a boisterous community tank. Requires soft, slightly acidic water and small live or frozen foods as part of the daily diet. Keep in groups of 10 or more.

One of the smaller pencilfish, with a fine two-stripe pattern along its slender body. Very peaceful and timid, well-suited to a compact nano or planted setup. Requires soft, slightly acidic water and benefits from small live or frozen foods alongside quality dried food. Keep in groups of 10 or more.

A tiny surface-dwelling hatchetfish from the blackwater rivers of the upper Negro and Orinoco basins. Like all hatchetfish it has a deep, keel-shaped body and large pectoral fins that allow it to glide above the water surface. A tight-fitting lid is essential. Very peaceful and timid; best kept in a planted blackwater setup in groups of 10 or more, away from boisterous tankmates.

One of the smallest hatchetfish at barely 2.5 cm, from the Peruvian Amazon. Very peaceful but extremely shy and delicate; not suitable for tanks with larger or more active fish. A tight-fitting lid is essential as it leaps readily. Prefers soft, acidic water and accepts floating dry foods alongside small live or frozen items. Keep in groups of 6 or more.

A large hatchetfish from the rivers of Panama and northwestern Colombia — the most westerly-distributed hatchetfish regularly available in the hobby. Like all hatchetfish it hugs the surface and can leap powerfully; a tight-fitting lid is essential. Peaceful and best kept in groups of 6 or more in a long, well-covered tank with plenty of surface area.

The largest hatchetfish commonly kept in the hobby, from across the Amazon basin. Its enormous pectoral fins generate the power to leap clear of the water surface — a tight-fitting lid is absolutely essential. Peaceful and best kept in groups of 6 or more in a spacious, well-covered tank; sensitive to poor water quality and elevated nitrates.

A boldly banded, fast-swimming leporinus from the Amazon basin that can exceed 30 cm. Quarrelsome toward its own kind in small groups — best kept singly or in groups of 6 or more, but only in a very large tank (210 cm / 7 ft minimum). A persistent nibbler of aquatic plants; suitable only for experienced keepers with very large, heavily filtered aquariums.

A large, black-banded leporinus from the Amazon basin closely related to L. fasciatus, reaching up to 30 cm. Like other large Leporinus it will decimate aquatic plants and can be quarrelsome toward conspecifics; best kept singly or in groups of 6 or more in a very large, plant-free aquarium. Suited only to experienced keepers with robust filtration and tanks of 400 litres or more.

A large leporinus from the coastal rivers of the Guianas and Venezuela, easily identified by the vivid red-orange coloration around its lips. Fast-moving and active; requires a very large tank (210 cm / 7 ft minimum). Primarily a herbivore and benthic grazer — avoid high-protein diets. Best kept singly or with similarly large, robust tankmates.

A boldly striped anostomus from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, recognized by its characteristic head-down swimming posture. Highly quarrelsome with conspecifics — best kept singly or in groups of 6 or more; groups of 2–5 invariably result in one fish dominating and injuring the others. Pairs well with medium to large peaceful species that occupy different territories.

A smaller, slender anostomus from the Orinoco, Amazon, and Guiana river systems that shares the characteristic head-down posture of its relatives. Very quarrelsome with conspecifics — keep singly or in groups of 6 or more only. Primarily a herbivore; provide plant-based foods and algae-based wafers alongside occasional live or frozen items.

A compact headstander from the Orinoco, Amazon, Paraguay, and lower Paraná basins, recognizable by its strongly arched back, marbled pattern, and distinctive head-down resting posture. May nip the fins of long-finned tankmates; best paired with robust, short-finned species. Accepts most prepared foods and fresh vegetables, tolerating a wide temperature range.

A quirky schooling fish known for its distinctive head-down swimming posture and spotted, silvery body. Peaceful and shy; best kept in groups of at least three with similarly calm tankmates. Sensitive to dissolved organics — introduce only into mature, well-established aquariums with soft, slightly acidic water.

A massive Central/West African characin reaching 75 cm. Juveniles are attractively banded and shoal peacefully, but adults become increasingly unpredictable and aggressive, best kept alone. Only suitable for very large, public-style displays and requires a heavy, secure lid as it is an exceptional jumper.

A large, elongated African characin with a distinctive long snout. Temperament is somewhat unpredictable and it will destroy most aquatic plants; driftwood and smooth rocks make better decor. Best kept singly or with robust, similarly-sized fish in a very large setup.

A relatively peaceful Distichodus that tolerates group housing unlike many of its larger relatives. May nip fins of slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. Not plant-safe; furnish with driftwood and robust species such as Anubias or Bolbitis rather than soft-leaved plants.

The smallest member of the Distichodus genus, reaching just 7.5 cm. Rarely encountered in the hobby, it shares the family's herbivorous tendencies and will graze on soft-leaved plants. Best kept in small groups in a Congo-biotope setup with soft, slightly acidic water.

A slender West African tetra with a bold lateral stripe, resembling a pencilfish in form but unrelated. Peaceful and shoaling; keep in groups of six or more in a well-planted, dimly lit aquarium. Prefers live or frozen foods but can adapt to quality dried foods with patience.
dimorphism: Males are smaller (around 4.5 cm) and develop yellow and red coloration in the fins; females reach up to 6.5 cm and are plainer.

description: A tiny, jewel-like West African characin barely reaching 3.5 cm, prized for the striking metallic blue-violet and red-orange colors of breeding males. Requires very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0) and thrives in a species-only or very small, peaceful community. Very shy; prefers live or frozen foods and may hide entirely when kept with boisterous fish.
dimorphism: Males display blue-violet iridescence and red-orange fin coloration; females are plainer and slightly larger.

description: A Congo basin tetra named for the distinctive crescent marking at the base of its tail. Peaceful and shoaling; best kept in groups of six or more in a well-planted aquarium with soft to neutral water and gentle flow. A natural choice for Congo biotope setups alongside species such as Congo tetras and butterfly cichlids.

A large West African tetra reaching 25 cm, distributed across the Niger, Chad, and Nile basins. Rarely seen in the hobby due to its size demands; needs a very spacious aquarium and benefits from the company of five or more conspecifics alongside robust, similarly sized tankmates.

A heavily built, disc-shaped cousin of the piranhas from the Amazon and Guiana regions. Primarily herbivorous with powerful dentition that makes it destructive to aquarium plants; use robust species like Anubias or Java fern, or opt for artificial plants. Peaceful and sociable; best kept in groups of five or more.

One of the most commonly kept silver dollars, forming large shoals in the Amazon and Paraguay basins. Primarily herbivorous and highly destructive to aquarium plants; decorate with driftwood and robust species like Anubias, or use artificial plants. Peaceful and sociable; keep in groups of five or more.

A large, disc-shaped serrasalmid reaching 40 cm, identifiable by a distinctive black vertical bar on its flank. Despite its piranha family membership, it is omnivorous rather than predatory, though it will consume small fish and invertebrates. Only suitable for very large aquariums; keep in groups of five or more with similarly sized, robust tankmates.

A rounded, deep-bodied silver dollar from the Amazon and Orinoco basins. Peaceful and gregarious; keep in groups of five or more. Omnivorous with strong herbivorous tendencies; supplement with fresh vegetables, and do not expect soft-leaved aquarium plants to survive.

A disc-shaped silver dollar from the Paraguay, Paraná, and Uruguay river systems, somewhat more cold-tolerant than Amazon relatives. Peaceful and gregarious; keep in groups of five or more with robust tankmates. Not plant-safe; feeds primarily on fruits, seeds, and vegetable matter in nature.

A massive South American serrasalmid reaching 88 cm — one of the largest in the piranha family. Commonly sold as cheap juveniles but grows rapidly to a size requiring public-aquarium-scale facilities (2,500+ litres). Primarily herbivorous; will consume small fish when full-grown. Not suitable for home aquariums.

A unique scale-eating specialist (lepidophage) that attacks the flanks of other fish to strip and consume their scales — a feeding strategy unlike any other common aquarium fish. Adults are highly aggressive and must be kept alone. Best suited to experienced keepers who can appreciate its unusual biology.

The Orinoco piranha, closely related to the red-bellied piranha but exclusive to Venezuela's Orinoco basin. Requires a massive dedicated aquarium — at least 1,300 litres — and must be kept either alone or in a shoal of five or more; intermediate group sizes increase aggression. Not compatible with community tanks.

The largest Pygocentrus piranha, endemic to Brazil's isolated São Francisco River basin. Extremely aggressive; suitable only for very large dedicated specimen tanks or public aquariums. Rarely available in the hobby and considered one of the most demanding piranha species to maintain.

An unusually elongated piranha with a distinctly pike-like body plan, adapted for ambush predation on smaller fish. Highly aggressive and strictly solitary; cannot be housed with any tankmates. Relatively accessible among dedicated piranha keepers — one of the few Serrasalmus suitable for a 450-litre home setup.

One of the largest piranha species, regularly exceeding 40 cm. Extremely aggressive; adults will fight to the death with conspecifics and must be kept in strict isolation. Only suited to very large dedicated aquaria of 1,200+ litres and exclusively for experienced piranha keepers.

A medium-sized piranha from Brazil's Tocantins basin with a notably wide temperature tolerance. Like all Serrasalmus, it must be kept strictly alone and is unsuitable for community aquariums. Suitable for the dedicated piranha enthusiast with a well-filtered 450-litre dedicated setup.

A rarely traded Serrasalmus piranha from the Rio Madeira in the Amazon basin. Like all congeners, it must be kept in strict isolation — no tankmates of any kind. Care requirements follow the genus: well-oxygenated, slightly acidic water with efficient filtration and a sandy substrate.

A widely distributed piranha with an unusually broad temperature range, found from the Amazon south to the Uruguay River. Aquarium specimens typically reach 18-21 cm; wild females are reported to grow considerably larger. Strictly solitary; must be kept alone with robust filtration.

A small, brightly colored Peruvian piranha known for its ruby-red coloration. Primarily a fin and scale eater in the wild, it can be acclimated to frozen and dead foods in captivity. Despite its small size it is still strictly a specimen fish, kept alone in a dedicated 110-litre setup.

A sleek, needle-nosed surface predator from northern Colombia and Venezuela with a gar-like body form. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow, but will ambush anything that fits in its mouth. Best kept in groups of four or more; requires a very long tank with a tight lid, as it is an exceptional jumper.

A sleek, torpedo-shaped pike characin from the Amazon, Tocantins, and Orinoco basins. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow but will ambush smaller tankmates. Despite its relatively modest adult length, it demands a very spacious aquarium of nearly 2,000 litres and is best kept in groups of four or more with a secure, tight-fitting lid.

The larger of the two commonly kept Boulengerella species, reaching 42 cm. A surface-oriented ambush predator that requires enormous, public-aquarium-scale facilities. Best kept in groups of four or more; a tight-fitting, heavy lid is essential as this species is an exceptional jumper.

A powerful piscivore reaching 105 cm, distributed across the Congo, Nile, Niger, Zambezi, and other major African river systems. An apex predator that must be housed alone in public-aquarium-scale facilities. Juveniles can be weaned onto dead foods relatively easily, but adults still require enormous, well-filtered setups.

An African pike characin from West and Central African river systems, superficially resembling the South American pike characins in body plan. Highly predatory; best housed alone or only with fish too large to be eaten. Requires large, well-filtered setups and is rarely encountered in the hobby.

A massive predatory characin reaching 89 cm, infamous for its enormous canine teeth that can exceed 10 cm in large specimens — giving rise to the common name 'vampire fish'. An obligate piscivore that requires live fish initially, though some individuals learn to accept dead alternatives. Only suitable for very large public aquariums.

A massive, torpedo-shaped predatory characin reaching 80 cm with a distinctly gar-like silhouette and prominent fang-like teeth. An obligate piscivore that in practice requires public-aquarium-scale facilities. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow; rarely kept by private hobbyists.

A slender, pike-like predatory characin with a bold black spot at the base of the caudal peduncle. Despite its modest adult size, this highly active open-water swimmer requires an enormous aquarium. Peaceful toward fish too large to swallow; best kept in matched groups of four or more to reduce cannibalism.

The larger of the two commonly kept Acestrorhynchus species, distinguished by spotted body patterning. A highly active open-water predator requiring an enormous dedicated setup of at least 2,000 litres. Best maintained in groups of four or more similarly sized individuals.

A popular Sri Lankan barb known for the intense crimson-red and jet-black coloration males develop during breeding. Peaceful and hardy; suitable for community aquariums with other small, peaceful species. Keep in groups of six or more to dilute any mild fin-nipping tendency.
dimorphism: Breeding males develop vivid red-crimson body coloration and jet-black dorsal barring; females remain silver-olive and are less colorful.

description: A colorful South Indian barb with green, red, and black banded patterning, also known as the panda barb. Peaceful but lively; may unsettle very timid companions. Keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted aquarium to encourage natural schooling behavior.

A widely distributed Asian barb with a characteristic spot-and-stripe pattern, found from Pakistan through India to Southeast Asia. Hardy and adaptable; tolerates cooler water than most tropical barbs and can thrive in unheated aquariums in temperate climates. Peaceful; keep in groups of eight or more.

A tiny, subtly beautiful barb from the Indian subcontinent, rarely exceeding 5 cm. Very peaceful and ideally suited to nano or species-specific setups; can be outcompeted by boisterous tankmates due to its small size. Keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted aquarium.

A small, peaceful barb from the Irrawaddy, Salween, and upper Mekong basins. Hardy and adaptable; tolerates cooler water down to 18°C, making it suitable for temperate or subtropical setups. A good choice for peaceful community aquariums or nano tanks with other small, similarly sized species.

A small, colorful Sri Lankan endemic with distinctive two-spot patterning and vibrant orange-yellow highlights. Peaceful and suitable for community aquariums with other small, non-aggressive species. Keep in groups of six or more in a well-planted setup with soft, slightly acidic water.

A striking medium-sized Indian barb named for the long filament extensions on the dorsal fin of adult males. Peaceful in community settings; males may establish hierarchies but aggression is generally mild. Prefers cooler water (20-25°C) than most tropical barbs and does best in groups of eight or more.
dimorphism: Males develop elongated filament extensions on the dorsal fin and display brighter coloration; females are plumper and less ornamented.

description: A medium-sized schooling barb from the Cauvery and Tungabhadra River systems of Karnataka, India. Males develop elongated dorsal fin rays, giving them a striking silhouette. Tolerates cooler water (19-25°C) than most tropical barbs; keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted aquarium.
dimorphism: Males develop extended dorsal fin rays and display more intense coloration; females are deeper-bodied.

description: A rare barb endemic to the hill streams of Kanyakumari District, Tamil Nadu, India — listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN. Closely related to the Arulius Barb and has similar care requirements; prefers cool, well-oxygenated water. Very uncommon in the trade; specimens are typically wild-caught or from specialist breeders.

A boldly patterned barb from the forested hill streams of Sarawak and West Kalimantan on Borneo. Peaceful and well-suited to a large community with similarly sized fish; wild individuals can reach 15 cm but aquarium specimens typically top out at 12 cm. Keep in groups of six or more in a spacious, well-planted setup.

A robust, wide-ranging barb from the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, identified by its distinctive T-shaped or spanner-like markings. Adults can reach 15 cm and become boisterous; best kept with similarly sized, robust species. Provide a large tank of at least 150 cm length and keep in groups of six or more to distribute aggression.

A medium-sized barb from clear streams and acidic swamps of Peninsular Malaysia and formerly Singapore, now listed as Endangered by the IUCN. Wild populations have declined severely; most trade specimens are farm-raised. Care is similar to the Clown Barb; peaceful and best kept in groups of six or more in a well-planted aquarium.

A strikingly patterned barb from river shallows, streams, and ditches across the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Wild individuals reach 12 cm but aquarium specimens typically top out at 10 cm. Keep in groups of eight or more in a large, well-planted setup; prefers soft, slightly acidic to neutral water.

A small, patterned barb endemic to blackwater rivers and peat swamps of southern Kalimantan, Borneo. The intricate scale patterning gives it a snakeskin-like appearance. Requires soft, very acidic water; feed primarily on small live and frozen invertebrates. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A small, vividly banded barb from the blackwater streams and peat swamps of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Often confused in the trade with the five-band barb (*Desmopuntius pentazona*); best distinguished by the absence of a dorsal-fin spot. Needs soft, acidic water and live or frozen invertebrate foods. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A tiny, attractively marked African barb from slow-moving, forested waters of the Congo River basin in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Rarely exceeds 3.5 cm; best suited to a species-specific or West/Central African biotope nano setup. Requires stable, soft, slightly acidic water within a narrow temperature range; feed on small live and frozen invertebrates.

One of the smallest barbs in the hobby, from slow-moving, shaded rainforest streams and swamps of West and Central Africa (Cameroon to Congo). Feeds naturally on insect larvae and small crustaceans; accepts appropriately sized live and frozen foods. Demands very soft, acidic water and benefits from a biotope-style setup with leaf litter and subdued lighting.

A small, scaleless cyprinid endemic to Inle Lake in Myanmar — listed as Endangered by the IUCN. Males are iridescent silvery-blue with a vivid red snout and red tail lobes; females are subdued. The high-altitude origin means it needs cooler, alkaline water unlike most nano cyprinids. Males are aggressive with each other; keep a single male with multiple females.
dimorphism: Males are iridescent silvery-blue with red snout and red tail lobes; females are duller with a dark pigmentation spot near the anus.

description: One of the smallest and most widely available nano fish, native to blackwater swamps and streams of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Singapore. Reaches only 2.5 cm; requires soft, very acidic water. Best kept as a single-species group of eight or more in a well-planted nano tank; easily outcompeted by larger or boisterous tankmates.

A brilliantly colored nano rasbora endemic to the blackwater peat swamps of southern Borneo. The body is a vivid orange-red, making groups highly attractive in a well-planted setup. Extremely delicate; requires very soft, acidic water and small live or frozen foods. Easily overlooked or outcompeted in a community; best in a species tank or with other micro-fish.

A tiny, reddish-pink nano rasbora from the lower Tapi River drainage of southern Thailand. Closely related to other Boraras species; distinguished by a black spot on the flank. Tolerates slightly less acidic water than some relatives (pH 5.0-7.0) but still needs soft conditions and small live or frozen invertebrate foods. Suitable only for nano or species-specific setups.

One of the smallest vertebrates kept in the aquarium hobby, rarely exceeding 1.5 cm. Endemic to northeastern Thailand in the Mekong basin. Very peaceful but easily intimidated; strictly a nano or species-only fish. Requires small live or frozen invertebrates and soft-to-neutral water. Impressive in groups of eight or more against a dark substrate.

A slender, active rasbora with a bold black horizontal stripe running from snout to tail. Native to slow-moving, heavily planted waters of the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins and northern Malay Peninsula. Hardy and peaceful; an ideal schooling fish for a planted community aquarium. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A larger, elegant rasbora from the streams, rivers, and peat swamps of southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Singapore, and Borneo. Named for its iridescent, glittering flanks. Peaceful and suitable for a large, well-planted community; provide a long tank with ample swimming space and keep in groups of six or more.

A small, adaptable rasbora from the peat swamp forests of southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo. The vivid emerald-green eye is the key identifying feature. Peaceful and tolerant of a wide pH range; suitable for planted nano tanks and larger community setups alike. Keep in groups of eight or more.

One of the largest rasboras, reaching 15 cm, with a striking forked tail marked by black-tipped lobes. Native to forest streams and peat swamp rivers of Peninsular Malaysia and Borneo. Peaceful and ideal for a spacious community with robust species; active swimmer that needs a long tank with open water. Keep in groups of six or more.

A medium-sized rasbora with a distinctive yellow tinge to the tail, native to blackwater streams, rivers, and forest ditches of Sumatra, Peninsular Malaysia, and Borneo. Tolerates very acidic water down to pH 4.0. Peaceful and active; provide a long tank with ample swimming space and keep in groups of six or more.

A medium-large rasbora from forested streams and peat swamps of Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo, now extirpated from Singapore. Identified by a series of bold dark spots ('portholes') along the lateral line. Peaceful and suitable for a spacious community with robust, similarly sized fish. Keep in groups of six or more.

One of the largest rasboras in the hobby, reaching 15 cm in a spacious aquarium. Native to peat swamps, forest streams, and slow-moving rivers of Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Sumatra, and Borneo. Despite its size it is very peaceful; can, however, intimidate smaller, shyer companions. Keep in groups of six or more in a large, well-planted setup.

A close relative of the popular Harlequin Rasbora, distinguished by a glowing orange mark along the flank rather than the harlequin's solid black wedge. Native to blackwater streams and peat swamps of Sumatra and Borneo. Peaceful and suitable for a planted nano or community aquarium; keep in groups of eight or more for best behavior and coloration.

A jewel-like nano fish from the blackwater peat swamps of Borneo, Sumatra, and Bangka Island, barely exceeding 2 cm. Males shimmer with electric blue-green iridescence. Requires very soft, acidic water and should be kept in very large groups of 20 or more for natural schooling behavior. Strictly a nano or species-specific setup; easily outcompeted in a standard community.
dimorphism: Males develop intense iridescent blue-green coloration; females are more subdued and rounder-bodied.

description: A tiny, rosy-red nano fish endemic to Inle Lake in Myanmar — listed as Endangered by the IUCN. Unlike most nano fish that prefer acidic water, Inle Lake is alkaline, so this species requires neutral to hard water (pH 6.0-8.0) and cooler temperatures (20-24°C). Very shy; best kept in a species-specific setup or with other small Inle Lake endemics.

A brilliantly colored small danio from the Mogaung Chaung river system of northern Myanmar, displaying a vivid orange-red band along the flank. Tolerates a very wide temperature range (16-26°C), making it suitable for unheated aquariums in temperate climates. Peaceful and active; keep in groups of eight or more in a well-planted setup.

A small danio from fast-flowing, clear streams of northern Myanmar, distinguished by vivid orange fins. Closely related to and sometimes confused with the Glowlight Danio. Tolerates cool water down to 16°C; an excellent choice for subtropical or unheated setups. Peaceful and active; keep in groups of eight or more.

A tiny nano danio from the Mogaung Chaung drainage of northern Myanmar, barely reaching 3 cm. Often sold as 'Gold Ring Danio' in the trade due to a series of golden rings along the body. Peaceful and adaptable; suitable for nano aquariums. Keep in groups of eight or more alongside other small, peaceful species.

A small, attractively spotted danio endemic to central and southern Myanmar, found in the Ayeyarwady, Sittaung, and Bago river drainages. Peaceful and hardy; tolerates a wide range of temperatures and water chemistry. A good choice for a smaller planted community aquarium. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A jewel-like nano fish from Inle Lake in Myanmar, listed as Endangered in the wild but commonly captive-bred. The turquoise body and vivid orange-pink fins make it highly sought-after. Unlike typical nano fish, it needs alkaline water (pH 7.0-8.0) and cooler temperatures (20-24°C), matching its highland lake origin. Keep in very large groups of 20 or more to disperse male aggression.

A softly colored danio with rosy-pink iridescence, native to rivers and streams of the Mekong basin in northern Thailand, Laos, and Myanmar. Hardy and peaceful; one of the more muted members of the Danio genus yet appealing in groups. Keep in groups of eight or more alongside other small, peaceful community fish.

A subtly beautiful danio from the peninsular Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, and adjacent islands, notable for its electric blue lateral stripe. Closely resembles the Blue Danio in appearance. Peaceful and hardy; tolerates a wide temperature range (18-26°C) and neutral to slightly acidic water. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A large, fast-moving danio from the streams and rivers of the Indian peninsula and Sri Lanka, ranging from lowland floodplains to 1,300 m elevation. Active and boisterous; can disturb slow-moving or long-finned tankmates despite not being overtly aggressive. Best kept with robust, similarly sized species in a large, well-aerated aquarium. Keep in groups of eight or more.

A small, rarely seen devario from mountain streams of the Ping River basin in northern Thailand, sometimes called Fire Bar Danio in the trade. Feeds primarily on terrestrial insects in the wild; readily accepts quality dried foods supplemented with small live or frozen invertebrates. Peaceful and active; keep in groups of eight or more.

A large, torpedo-shaped cyprinid with greatly elongated, whisker-like pectoral fins that give it its common name. Native across South and Southeast Asia from Pakistan to Myanmar. An active surface swimmer prone to fin-nipping, especially in small groups or with long-finned species; best kept in large schools with robust, similarly sized tankmates. Requires a tight-fitting lid as it can jump.

A slender, nearly translucent surface-dwelling cyprinid widely distributed across South and Southeast Asia. Feeds primarily on small invertebrates at the surface; keep a tight-fitting lid as it is an active jumper. Peaceful and adaptable; tolerates a wide pH range. Keep in groups of eight or more and avoid slow-moving or long-finned tankmates.

A rare cousin of the White Cloud Mountain Minnow, endemic to the Ben Hai River system of north-central Vietnam. Slightly smaller and more cold-tolerant than the common White Cloud. Peaceful and hardy; suitable for nano tanks and temperate setups. Best kept in groups of ten or more alongside other small, peaceful species.

A large, silver-bodied shark-shaped cyprinid native to Borneo, Sumatra, and Peninsular Malaysia. Widely sold as juveniles at 5-8 cm but grows to 35 cm and requires a massive tank — commonly mis-stocked. Peaceful toward other fish despite its size but is an active, boisterous swimmer that will overwhelm small tankmates. Keep in groups of five or more in a tank of at least 240 cm length.

A massive, jet-black labeo from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins capable of reaching 90 cm in the wild; aquarium specimens typically reach 50-60 cm. Highly territorial and belligerent toward conspecifics and similarly-shaped species. Suitable only for public aquariums or very large specialist private installations. Not recommended for home aquariums.

A streamlined African labeo from rocky, fast-flowing rivers of the Zambezi, Limpopo, and Congo basins. Named for its vivid red eye. Grazes on diatoms and algae scraped from rocks; will also accept plant matter and quality dried foods. Can be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly shaped bottom dwellers; usually kept as a single specimen.

A boldly patterned African labeo from the Congo and Ogowe river basins, recognized by its dark-spotted or marbled body. Territorial and aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly shaped species; must be kept singly. Grazes on algae and aufwuchs; will also eat small invertebrates and quality sinking foods. Only wild-caught specimens are available.

A common SE Asian garra widely available in the aquarium trade, often confused with the true Siamese Algae Eater. Tolerant of a wide range of conditions; feeds on algae, biofilm, and meaty foods. Not particularly tolerant of conspecifics in small tanks — keep in groups of three or more to establish a hierarchy and reduce aggression. Sometimes reclassified as Ceratogarra cambodgiensis.

A striking small garra from the fast-flowing, well-oxygenated hill streams of the Arakan Mountains in western Myanmar. Named for its panda-like black-and-white patterning. Relatively peaceful toward non-garra species; develops a pecking order with conspecifics. Needs excellent water flow and oxygenation; keep in groups of three or four in a rocky biotope setup.

A cold-water garra from the rivers and springs of Turkey, Syria, Jordan, and the Middle East — famous for use in 'fish spa' treatments where it nibbles dead skin. Requires cool, well-oxygenated water (14-20°C) that most tropical setups cannot provide. Tolerates a wide pH range but not tolerant of conspecifics; keep in small groups of three or four with good water flow.
