A large, algae-eating cyprinid from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, valued for its appetite for tough algae types including black beard algae. Tolerates cool water down to 16°C. Strictly a herbivore — avoid high-protein foods. Peaceful when kept in groups of six or more; individual specimens can become territorial. Requires a long tank of at least 180 cm.

A massive SE Asian barb reaching 1 m in length, native to Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula. The common name comes from the intoxicating effect of its flesh when eaten after the fish has ingested fermenting plant matter. Peaceful toward similarly-sized tankmates but will consume smaller fish. Suitable only for public aquariums or very large specialist installations.

A large, powerful predatory barb from the Mekong, Chao Phraya, Malay Peninsula, Borneo, Java, and Sumatra, capable of reaching 70 cm. Primarily a piscivore that feeds on smaller fish, insects, and crustaceans. Becomes increasingly dominant with age; juveniles are more tolerant. Suitable only for public aquariums or very large specialist setups. Keep in groups of five.

A large, peaceful herbivore widely distributed across the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Java, and Borneo. Named for its characteristic thick, bony lips used for scraping algae and plant matter. Occasionally appears in the aquarium trade. Peaceful with most species; needs a large tank with ample herbivore-friendly foods.

A fast-moving, surface-dwelling cyprinid endemic to the fast-flowing hill streams of Kerala in the Western Ghats, India. Feeds primarily on terrestrial and aquatic insects; readily accepts quality dried foods with live or frozen supplements. Vigorous and boisterous; develops a pecking order and is unsuitable for slow-moving or delicate tankmates. Keep in groups of five or more.

A large, silver-bodied barb with vivid red fins from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins and peninsular Thailand. Closely related to the Tinfoil Barb. Active and boisterous; will consume smaller tankmates and disturb timid species. Keep in groups of six or more with robust, similarly-sized fish. Readily accepts most foods including plant matter, flakes, and pellets.

A sleek, torpedo-shaped surface fish native to rivers of the Malay Peninsula, Indochina, and Indonesia. Reaches 30 cm in the wild but typically stays around 22 cm in aquaria. An accomplished jumper that demands a tightly-fitted lid and a large, open swimming space. Feeds primarily on surface insects, invertebrates, and small fish fry; keep only with robust tankmates too large to be eaten. Best kept in groups of three or more.

A tiny corydoras from the Madeira River basin that spends more time hovering in the mid-water column than most of its bottom-dwelling relatives. Keep in groups of 6 or more; a fine-sand substrate is appreciated even though it frequently schools away from the bottom. Accepts most small prepared and frozen foods. FishBase currently recognises this species as Gastrodermus pygmaeus.

One of the smallest corydoras species and one of the most pelagic, spending much of its time hovering and schooling in open water rather than scavenging the bottom. Native to the Amazon and Paraguay basins. Requires small live and frozen foods such as micro-worms, Daphnia, and Cyclops. Keep in groups of 6 or more. FishBase currently recognises this species as Gastrodermus hastatus.

A tiny, speckled corydoras from soft, acidic streams of the Upper Orinoco basin in Venezuela. Slightly more bottom-oriented than its close relatives C. pygmaeus and C. hastatus, though it also ventures into open water. Prefers fine sand, leaf litter, and dim lighting. Keep in groups of 6 or more. FishBase currently recognises this species as Hoplisoma habrosum.

A mid-sized corydoras from the Purus River basin of Brazil, recognisable by its reticulated dark pattern on a pale body. A hardy, peaceful community fish that is well-suited to the beginner. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate; will graze on sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and Daphnia. FishBase currently recognises this species as Hoplisoma schwartzi.

A striking blackwater corydoras from the Rio Negro basin of Brazil, distinguished by its elongated dorsal-fin spine and distinctive dark patterning. Requires soft, acidic water typical of the Negro's blackwater environment. More demanding than common corydoras; keep in groups of 6 or more with pristine water quality. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma davidsandsi.

A soft-water corydoras from the upper Rio Negro and Meta River basins, closely resembling the bandit cory (C. metae) but identifiable by a narrower eye stripe that does not meet the dorsal blotch. Naturally inhabits acidic blackwater streams; prefers soft, acidic to neutral conditions in captivity. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma melini.

Named for the bold black mask across its eyes and a large dorsal blotch connected by a stripe, the bandit cory is native to the Meta River basin of Colombia. A hardy, peaceful community fish suitable for beginners once water parameters are stable. Often confused with the false bandit cory (C. melini); the bandit's mask stripe connects cleanly to the dorsal spot. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma metae.

A small, spotted corydoras from the Napo River drainage of eastern Ecuador and Loreto, Peru. A typical community corydoras with a calm temperament and modest space requirements. Accepts sinking pellets supplemented with small live or frozen foods. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Gastrodermus napoensis.

A small, striped corydoras from the upper Amazon basin, named after the Loreto region of Peru. A peaceful, undemanding community fish with slightly cooler temperature preferences than many Amazon corydoras. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand; accepts sinking pellets and small live or frozen foods. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma loretoense.

A widely distributed corydoras found throughout the upper Amazon basin in Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. Named for its spotted patterning, which superficially resembles the leopard or three-line corydoras. A hardy, adaptable species well suited to community aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma leucomelas.

A small corydoras from the Guaporé River basin of Brazil and Bolivia, easily identified by the distinct dark blotch on the caudal peduncle that gives it its common name. A calm, social fish suitable for community tanks with other peaceful species. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma caudimaculatum.

A distinctive corydoras from the upper Rio Negro basin, named for the bold black-and-white striped tail fin that resembles a flag. One of the larger corydoras species. Inhabits blackwater environments and prefers soft, slightly acidic water in captivity. Keep in groups of 6 or more; avoid strongly alkaline conditions. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis robineae.

A reticulated corydoras from the Amazon River in Peru and Brazil, superficially resembling the network corydoras (C. reticulatus) but distinguished by a different pattern arrangement. A peaceful community fish that accepts most sinking foods. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis sodalis.

A handsome corydoras from the Purus River basin of Brazil with attractive dark reticulation on a pale body. Prefers slightly cooler temperatures than many Amazonian corydoras. A peaceful community fish; keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. Less commonly available than many other corydoras. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis pulcher.

A small corydoras from the Meta River basin of Colombia, named in honour of the renowned fish author and collector Dr. Herbert R. Axelrod. Prefers soft to moderately soft, slightly acidic water. A peaceful community fish that does best in groups of 6 or more on a fine-sand substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma axelrodi.

A tiny and seldom-seen corydoras from the upper Caquetá River basin of Colombia, rarely available in the trade. Reaches only about 4 cm and is one of the smaller corydoras species. Care is typical for small Colombian corydoras: soft, slightly acidic water, a fine-sand substrate, and groups of 6 or more. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma reynoldsi.

A subtropical corydoras from the coastal rivers of Santa Catarina and Paraná, and the Iguaçu River basin in southern Brazil. Unlike most tropical corydoras, it prefers distinctly cooler water temperatures (18–23°C) reflecting its temperate habitat. Unsuitable for warm tropical aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more; a well-oxygenated tank is beneficial. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma ehrhardti.

A larger blackwater corydoras from the upper Rio Negro basin, named for its mimicry of the external appearance of Corydoras adolfoi. Requires soft, acidic water typical of the Rio Negro. One of the larger and more striking corydoras, reaching 6.5 cm. Keep in groups of 6 or more; avoid neutral or alkaline conditions. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis imitator.

A handsome blackwater corydoras from the upper Rio Negro basin, with a distinctive dark stripe along the body. Named after Dr. Warren Burgess. Prefers soft, acidic water conditions reflecting its blackwater origins; avoid hard or alkaline water. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma burgessi.

A small corydoras from the upper Amazon basin with a distinctive dark mask across the face. Prefers slightly cooler and softer water than many tropical corydoras, reflecting its montane Amazonian origins in Ecuador and Peru. A peaceful community fish; keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma atropersonatum.

A plain, uniformly-coloured corydoras from the Orinoco River basin, distinguished by its lack of any strong pattern. Despite its seemingly drab appearance, it has a subtle sheen and is sought by corydoras collectors. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma concolor.

An attractive, spotted corydoras from the lower Tapajós River basin of Brazil, with a reticulated pattern that gives it a decorative appearance. A hardy, peaceful community fish well suited to the beginner. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate; accepts sinking pellets and small live or frozen foods. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis ornatus.

A small, attractively spotted corydoras from the coastal rivers of the Guianas. Distinguished by the dark spots and a blotch on the dorsal fin. A hardy, adaptable species suitable for community aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a soft substrate. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma melanistium.

A medium-large corydoras from the Suriname River basin and coastal rivers of French Guiana, identifiable by its scattered dark spots on a pale body. One of the original Corydoras species described by Bloch in 1794. A peaceful, hardy community fish suitable for most neutral to soft-water community setups. Keep in groups of 6 or more. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma punctatum.

A small, seldom-imported corydoras from the Nanay River basin of Loreto, Peru. Rarely available in the hobby and lightly documented. Care is typical for Amazonian corydoras: moderate temperatures, slightly soft to neutral water, fine sand, and groups of 6 or more. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis sychri.

A small corydoras from the Guaporé River basin of Brazil and Bolivia, at the border between Amazon and Paraná drainages. Prefers soft, slightly acidic to neutral water. The elongated body shape distinguishes it from many of its relatives. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Gastrodermus guapore.

A cool-water corydoras from the coastal rivers of southeastern Brazil (Espírito Santo to Paraná), with attractive blue-green tones and a spotted pattern. Requires cooler temperatures than most tropical corydoras, reflecting its subtropical habitat. Incompatible with warm tropical aquaria. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand in a well-oxygenated tank. FishBase recognises this species as Hoplisoma nattereri.

A well-spotted corydoras from the upper Amazon basin, widely kept in the hobby but frequently mislabelled or confused with similar spotted species such as C. punctatus. A robust, adaptable community fish tolerant of a range of water conditions. Keep in groups of 6 or more on soft sand. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis ambiacus.

A moderately-sized corydoras from the Inírida and upper Orinoco River basin in Colombia, with a distinctive mask through the eye and a dark dorsal blotch. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water from its blackwater and clearwater habitats. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand. FishBase recognises this species as Brochis delphax.

A tiny aspidoras catfish from the upper Araguaia River basin of Brazil, related to but distinct from the Corydoras genus. Slightly smaller than most corydoras and requires very clean, soft water. Behaves similarly to its relatives: groups of 6 or more, fine-sand substrate, and varied diet of small foods. FishBase has reclassified this species to Gastrodermus pauciradiatus.

A small aspidoras catfish endemic to the upper Paraná River basin of Brazil. Rarely available in the hobby. As a subtropical species from southern Brazil, it prefers cooler and neutral to slightly acidic water compared to most tropical corydoras. Keep in groups of 6 or more on fine sand with good water quality.

A small scleromystax from the coastal rivers of southeastern Brazil (Espírito Santo to São Paulo). More robust and armoured than typical corydoras, the Scleromystax genus is sexually dimorphic with males developing odontode bristles. Requires a long tank with good flow. Keep in groups of 6 or more with a mix of males and females when possible.

One of the largest corydoras-type catfish, from the upper Paraguay River basin of Brazil. Despite its size (up to 10 cm), it retains the peaceful, gregarious nature of the family. Requires a spacious tank with a soft substrate and excellent filtration. Keep in groups of 6 or more. The elongated dorsal fin spine is a distinguishing feature.

A large, hog-nosed brochis from the western Amazon basin with a distinctive elongated, downturned snout adapted for rooting through substrate. One of the largest members of the corydoras family. Peaceful and suitable for large community tanks. Keep in groups of 6 or more on a deep, soft-sand substrate that allows natural foraging behavior.

An elegant, elongated loricariid catfish from the Magdalena, San Jorge, and Cesar River basins of Colombia, with a characteristic long rostrum and trailing filaments on the tail and fins. A dedicated algae grazer that also rasps wood and accepts blanched vegetables. Prefers warm, well-oxygenated water. Can be kept singly or in pairs. FishBase recognises this species as Sturisomatichthys aureus.

A large loricariid from the Pacific and Caribbean drainages of Panama, Colombia, and Ecuador, reaching 30 cm. Unusually cool temperature requirements (20–22°C) reflect its coastal rivers influenced by the Humboldt Current. Incompatible with warm tropical aquaria. Requires a long tank, excellent water quality, and a diet rich in vegetable matter. FishBase recognises this species as Sturisomatichthys panamensis.

A slender, stick-like loricariid from the Paraguay River basin, one of the most widely kept whiptail catfish. Camouflages itself flat against wood and substrate. Peaceful and largely undemanding; accepts sinking pellets, blanched vegetables, and small live foods. Males guard eggs laid on flat surfaces. Can be kept in pairs or small groups.

A remarkable twig-mimic catfish from the Orinoco basin with an extremely elongated body and rostrum. A specialist herbivore — the bulk of its diet must be algae and blanched vegetables; prepared foods alone are insufficient. Sensitive to deteriorating water quality and requires consistent pristine conditions. Largely sedentary and will not compete for food in busy tanks. Best kept singly or in species tanks.

An unusual, extremely flattened loricariid from the Paraná, Paraguay, and Uruguay basins that resembles a flounder in profile. Spends the day buried in soft substrate and emerges to feed at night. Requires a very large tank with a deep, fine-sand substrate and cool subtropical temperatures (18–24°C). A specialist species demanding excellent water quality and live or frozen invertebrate foods.

A large, boldly striped wood-eating pleco from the Orinoco and Amazon systems. Requires a constant supply of driftwood to rasp — this is a dietary necessity, not just decoration. Peaceful with most fish but very aggressive toward other plecos and catfish; only one specimen per tank is recommended. Produces copious waste and demands excellent filtration.

A sought-after wood-eating pleco from the Cauca and Magdalena River basins of Colombia, recognised by its striking vivid-blue irises. Like other Panaque, it requires driftwood as a primary dietary component and strong filtration to handle the resulting cellulose waste. Highly aggressive toward other plecos; keep only one per tank. Rarely encountered in the trade.

One of the largest plecos known, reaching at least 60 cm and reported from main-channel habitats of the upper Amazon (Solimões) in Peru and Brazil. Strictly a public aquarium fish — no home aquarium can realistically house an adult. A wood-eating specialist like its Panaque relatives. Rarely available; juveniles occasionally appear in the trade but quickly outgrow any domestic setup.

A small, attractive pleco (L204) from the San Alejandro River in the Ucayali basin of Peru, with a striking pattern of white or cream vermiculations on a dark body. Like other Panaqolus, it requires driftwood to rasp. Territorial toward other plecos but manageable in size. Provide good water flow and hiding places. Rarely imported; commands a premium price.

A heavily spined, leopard-spotted pleco from the Rupununi River basin of Guyana and Brazil. Unlike the wood-eating Panaque group, Pseudacanthicus species are carnivorous and require high-protein foods such as meaty sinking pellets, prawns, and frozen invertebrates. Highly aggressive toward other catfish; one per tank only. The dense armoured spines make handling hazardous.

A heavily spined, dark-bodied Pseudacanthicus from the Amazon basin in Brazil and Ecuador. Unusually cool temperature requirements (20–24°C) distinguish it from most Amazon species. Like all cactus plecos, it is carnivorous, requiring meaty foods, and highly aggressive toward other catfish — keep singly. Dense armour and spines make handling dangerous.

A striking L264 pleco from the Tapajós River basin of Brazil, identifiable by the white spots on a dark body and a distinctive upturned snout. Unlike the vegetarian Panaque group, Leporacanthicus are strongly carnivorous — live and frozen foods such as bloodworm, mussel, and prawn are essential. Males are territorial with conspecifics; provide a cave per fish. Water movement should be moderate to strong.

A distinctive, flat-bodied pleco from the lower Rio Negro basin with an attractive marbled or banded pattern and broad, fan-like pectoral fins. A primarily herbivorous species requiring a vegetable-rich diet supplemented with algae wafers and blanched greens. Softer, acidic water reflecting its blackwater origins is preferred. Peaceful and suitable for community tanks with compatible species.

A massive, heavily spined pleco from the lower Tocantins River that reaches 100 cm and is strictly a public aquarium fish — no domestic tank can house an adult. Juveniles are sold in the hobby but quickly outgrow any private setup. Adults become extremely territorial and must be kept singly. A generalist detritivore accepting a wide range of sinking and fresh foods.

One of the largest Amazonian plecos, reaching 100 cm in the wild (captive specimens typically 50–60 cm). A public aquarium fish entirely unsuitable for the home hobbyist as an adult. Juveniles are occasionally sold; buyers must plan for a species that becomes massive, aggressive, and heavily armoured. Requires extremely strong filtration and large quantities of varied food.

A large, distinctive loricariid from the upper Amazon basin, recognisable by its rough, raised-ridge armour and the fleshy protuberances around the snout that give it its common name. A dedicated algae and biofilm grazer requiring a large tank with substantial algae growth or supplemental vegetable feeding. Keep singly or in species setups with adequate territory.

A mid-sized, striped Peckoltia widely distributed across the Amazon basin, known as the banded peckoltia pleco. An omnivorous loricariid that rasps algae and biofilm but also benefits from small meaty foods. Males are territorial with conspecifics and similarly-shaped plecos; provide caves and line-of-sight breaks. Accepts a range of sinking and fresh vegetable foods.

A larger Peckoltia from the rivers of Guyana and Venezuela, including the Essequibo, Rupununi, and Orinoco systems. Uncommonly imported and infrequently available. An omnivorous loricariid that feeds on algae, biofilm, and small invertebrates. Males are territorial; provide plenty of caves and hiding spots. Prefers warm water with moderate flow.

The L183 starlight bristlenose pleco from the upper and middle Amazon, recognisable by white-spotted fins and the bristle tentacles on the snout of males. Larger and warmer-preferring than the common bristlenose (A. cirrhosus), requiring soft, slightly acidic water and stronger flow. Territorial with conspecifics and similarly-shaped plecos. Feeds on algae, sinking foods, vegetables, and occasional invertebrates.

A strikingly tentacled Ancistrus from the Xingu and Tocantins River basins, named for the elaborate fleshy growths on the snout of mature males. A specialist of fast-flowing, rocky river habitats that requires strong current and high oxygen levels. Needs high-protein foods including frozen bloodworm, mussel, and prawn. Territorial; provide individual caves. Not suitable for still-water community setups.

A large, spotted sailfin pleco from the Amazon basin, one of the most commonly sold but widely mis-sized plecos in the hobby — often sold as a 'tank cleaner' for small aquaria but capable of reaching 58 cm. A prolific algae grazer and biofilm feeder. Requires a very large tank with excellent filtration and produces substantial waste. Territorial with other plecos; keep only one per tank.

One of the largest doradid catfish, reaching 90 cm, with a row of sharp lateral scutes that give it its common name. Despite its size, a true gentle giant — peaceful with nearly all tankmates it cannot swallow. A nocturnal detritivore that sifts the substrate for food. Requires a very large tank with substantial horizontal space and excellent biological filtration.

A massive doradid catfish named for its ability to crush and consume entire snails and other hard-shelled invertebrates with powerful pharyngeal teeth. From the Amazon, Tocantins, and Essequibo basins. Highly gregarious — must be kept in groups of 4 or more and will pine and refuse food when kept singly. Peaceful with large tankmates. A public or specialist aquarium species.

A small, armoured doradid catfish from the Amazon basin and coastal Guiana rivers. Nocturnal and secretive; spends the day hiding in caves or dense vegetation. Produces audible sounds by vibrating the swim bladder. Gregarious — best kept in groups of 4 or more of its own kind or with similar species. Will consume very small fish and invertebrates; not suitable with nano species.

A heavily spined doradid catfish from the Amazon and Essequibo River basins, very similar to A. cataphractus but slightly larger and more densely spined. Nocturnal, secretive, and highly gregarious — keep in groups of 4 or more. Produces audible croaking sounds. Will eat very small fish and invertebrates. The dense spines lock when extended, making the fish difficult to dislodge from crevices and dangerous to net.

A medium-sized armored doradid catfish from the Guianas and Amazon Basin, closely related to the larger striped Raphael. Nocturnal and secretive, spending days hidden in caves and driftwood hollows. Peaceful with fish too large to swallow but may consume very small tankmates at night. Accepts sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and shrimp.

A strikingly patterned auchenipterid catfish from the blackwater Branco and Trombetas rivers of Brazil, with bold dark spots on a pale body recalling jaguar markings. Shy and nocturnal; requires caves and driftwood for daytime shelter. Small fish may be consumed at night — avoid very small tankmates. Gregarious and reportedly less shy in groups of 3+. FishBase records typical adults at 17 cm; hobby sources report up to 25 cm.

A small, nocturnal driftwood catfish from the upper Amazon of Peru, also known as the midnight catfish. Males develop a modified anal fin for internal fertilization — a trait unique to auchenipterids. Spends daylight hours wedged tightly into wood crevices and is rarely visible until lights go off. Peaceful with similarly sized or larger tankmates but may consume very small fish and shrimp after dark.

A large, active pimelodid from the Amazon, Orinoco, Paraná, and Guianan rivers, with a pale silver body boldly spotted in black and a deeply forked tail. Typical aquarium adults reach 28 cm; wild specimens exceed 38 cm SL (~44 cm TL). Nocturnal predator — will consume any fish small enough to fit in its wide mouth. Solitary individuals hide all day; groups of 3+ are more visible and active.

A fast-moving pimelodid from the Paraná and São Francisco basins, commonly sold as a juvenile at 18 cm but reaching 51 cm in the wild; long-term captive adults typically settle around 18–30 cm. Shoaling by nature — groups of 3+ are considerably more active and less skittish. Predatory toward smaller fish; keep only with robust tankmates of similar or larger size.

A shovelnose catfish with a distinctive elongated, duck-bill snout that it uses to probe wood and roots for prey; adults reach 40–54 cm and require a 15,000-litre system. Exclusively suitable for public aquaria — juveniles are widely sold but almost always surrendered as they grow. Any fish that fits in its broad mouth will be eaten. Accepts large frozen and fresh seafood in captivity.

A spectacularly striped pimelodid, also sold as the gold zebra catfish, with bold black-and-white banding that fades in adults; reaches 60 cm. Suitable only for public aquaria or very large private systems — almost entirely piscivorous, and any tankmate small enough to swallow will be eaten. Adapts to large frozen prawns, squid, whitebait, and fish strips in captivity. Solitary and highly predatory.

One of the largest freshwater fish in South America, reaching nearly 2 metres and famous for completing the longest freshwater migration known — some individuals travel 5,500 km from the Amazon estuary to Andean spawning grounds and back. An exclusively public-aquarium species; no private enclosure can meet the requirements of a 192 cm piscivore. Adapts to large frozen seafood in captivity but must be fed sparingly — adults require feeding only once weekly.

The largest catfish in South America, reportedly reaching 3.6 m and over 200 kg in the wild, though aquarium specimens rarely exceed 2–2.5 m. Found across the Amazon, Orinoco, and Guianan river systems. A truly enormous obligate piscivore that is entirely unsuitable for any private aquarium — only the largest public aquaria can house adults. Adapts to large frozen seafood in captivity but requires feeding no more than once weekly.

A bizarre, highly flattened ambush predator from the São Francisco River of Brazil, named for its enormous gaping mouth relative to its body. Sedentary by nature — it buries itself in sandy substrate and waits for prey. Cannot be kept with any fish less than half its own length. Males provide parental care to adhesive eggs. A specialist fish for experienced aquarists with very large systems and knowledge of South American endemics.
behavior: Sedentary ambush predator; spends most of its time motionless, buried in sand or lying on the bottom. Analogous in strategy to the Asian Chaca catfish despite distant relation.

description: A huge, nearly scaleless pimelodid from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, reaching 140 cm and commonly encountered at 130 cm / 50 kg in the wild. Also called the manguruyu or black manguruyu. A nocturnal migratory piscivore that hunts shoaling characins; entirely a public aquarium species. Prefers cooler water than typical Amazonian fish, reflecting its large river habitat.

A large shovelnose catfish with bold black-and-white tiger striping from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, reaching 105 cm in aquariums and up to 130 cm in the wild. Debatably an aquarium fish at all given its eventual size; only very few dedicated private aquarists can accommodate it. Exclusively piscivorous; adapts to large frozen seafood in captivity. Territorial with conspecifics — must be kept alone. Adults require feeding once weekly maximum.

A large, bottom-dwelling pimelodid from the Amazon and Orinoco basins, also known as the sailfin pim or achara catfish, reaching 100 cm. Attractively marbled in brown and cream with elongated dorsal and pectoral fin rays. A large-scale predator that belongs in very large specialist or public aquarium systems only. Piscivorous by nature; adapts to large frozen and fresh seafood in captivity.

One of the most sought-after Synodontis, with a dark body covered in bright white spots that fade with age. Typical aquarium adults reach 25 cm; wild individuals can exceed 55 cm. Nocturnal and increasingly territorial — best kept as a solitary specimen or pair, not with other Synodontis. Omnivorous; accepts most sinking foods and live or frozen invertebrates. Not recommended for general community tanks due to adult aggression.

A medium-large Congo catfish with bold black-and-white barring and a distinctively elongated dorsal fin ray — the 'clown' patterning makes this one of the most recognizable Synodontis. Reaches 32 cm. Nocturnal and generally peaceful toward unrelated species but may become territorial with age. A sturdy community fish for Congo-themed tanks with robust tankmates. Accepts sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and vegetable matter.

A medium-sized Congo synodontis also sold as the pyjama syno or orange-striped squeaker, with bold orange or yellow longitudinal stripes on a dark body. Reaches ~19.5 cm and adapts well to captivity. Nocturnal and generally peaceful but may show mild territorial tendencies as an adult. Accepts sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, algae wafers, and vegetable matter.

A small, peaceful synodontis endemic to the fast-flowing rapids of the lower Congo River — one of the world's most biodiverse aquatic habitats. Feeds on insect larvae, algae, gastropods, crustaceans, sponges, and opportunistically on fish eggs. Prefers high-flow tanks with strong oxygenation. Suitable for Congo-biotope setups; peaceful with similarly sized tankmates but avoid aggressive or territorial species.

A small synodontis endemic to the rocky shores of Lake Tanganyika in Zambia, often sold in the hobby as the 'false petricola' or 'dwarf petricola.' Reaches about 10 cm TL. Omnivorous, feeding naturally on insect larvae, algae, crustaceans, sponges, and bivalves. Requires hard, alkaline Tanganyikan water; does not tolerate soft or acidic conditions. Peaceful toward similarly sized tankmates.

The only synodontis native to Lake Malawi; reaches ~19 cm and is well-suited to Malawi cichlid community tanks. Hardy and adaptable, it will not fight with conspecifics but is aggressive toward other Synodontis species. May eat very small fish. Omnivorous and accepts most foods including cichlid pellets, frozen invertebrates, and blanched vegetables. Prefers the hard, alkaline water of its home lake.

A medium-large synodontis endemic to Lake Tanganyika, reaching 27 cm with a heavily granulated body texture that gives it its common name. Feeds naturally on insect larvae, algae, crustaceans, sponges, bivalves, and fish eggs at depths of 20–40 m. Requires hard, highly alkaline Tanganyikan water — unlike Congo synodontis, it will not tolerate soft or acidic conditions. Suitable for Tanganyika community tanks with robust cichlids.

A handsome Congo catfish instantly recognized by the single large dark spot on its pale flanks, reaching ~26.5 cm. Widespread across the Congo Basin including Pool Malebo, the Kasai, Ubangi, and Luapula drainages. Nocturnal and generally peaceful toward unrelated species but may show territorial tendencies with age. Accepts a varied diet including sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, and vegetable matter.

A small, peaceful synodontis from Lake Tanganyika, reaching ~15 cm. One of the smaller Tanganyika synodontis and well-suited to Tanganyikan community tanks with smaller cichlids and other hard-water species. Omnivorous and accepts most foods. Unlike Congolese synodontis, it requires the hard, alkaline conditions of its home lake.

A slender, semi-transparent catfish from the Congo River system and adjacent Gabon drainages, reaching ~15 cm. Strongly diurnal and shoaling — keep in groups of 10 or more; individuals become withdrawn and stop feeding. Prefers moderate water movement and dimmed lighting. Despite its glass-catfish appearance, it is robust and active, spending the day in constant motion near the midwater.

A small, three-striped African catfish from the Niger and Cross River systems of Nigeria and Benin, sometimes sold as a glass catfish due to its semi-transparent body. Peaceful and schooling; a group of six or more is essential — single specimens withdraw and may refuse food. Typical aquarium size is around 8 cm; wild maximum reaches ~11.5 cm. Accepts most small sinking foods and frozen invertebrates.

A large, long-snouted catfish with giraffe-like brown blotching, found across the Nile, Chad, Congo, Niger, and Tanganyika systems — one of the most widespread African catfish. Reaches 60–80 cm and requires a very large aquarium. Despite its size it is a gentle omnivore, scavenging insects and plant material from muddy substrates. Males guard egg nests. Not a predator of tankmates but must be housed only with robust fish of substantial size.

An obligate air-breather capable of 'walking' across land using pectoral spines, surviving 18+ hours out of water. Typical aquarium adults reach ~50 cm; wild specimens in invasive populations (established in Florida) can exceed 100 cm. Highly predatory and voracious — will consume fish, molluscs, invertebrates, and plant matter. Invasive in many regions; regulations may prohibit keeping or releasing this species. A heavy lid is essential.
behavior: Obligate air-breather that surfaces frequently; can survive out of water for extended periods. Uses pectoral spines to walk on land. Highly invasive — check local regulations before acquiring.

description: A slender, striped bagrid catfish from the Indian subcontinent found in lowland lakes, swamps, and slow-moving rivers with muddy bottoms. Reaches ~21 cm SL (~24 cm TL). Nocturnal and somewhat predatory — will consume small fish and invertebrates at night. Does better in small groups of 3 or more. Tolerates a range of conditions but prefers neutral, slightly soft water. Occurs occasionally in mildly brackish lowland habitats.

A large, slender silurid catfish from the Indian subcontinent, valued as a food fish and kept in the aquarium trade as juveniles. Reaches 45 cm and requires a very large aquarium. Fairly peaceful and shy for its size, but will consume any fish small enough to fit in its mouth. Readily accepts most live and frozen foods; larger individuals take earthworms, prawn pieces, and pellets.

A tiny torrent catfish from fast-flowing mountain streams of India and Bangladesh, reaching only 3–4 cm. Flattened body with adhesive texture lets it cling to rocks in strong current. Peaceful and best kept in a dedicated species or nano biotope setup with slow-to-medium flow and no large or boisterous tankmates. Feed live or frozen bloodworm and daphnia after lights out.
behavior: Clings to smooth rocks and wood using its flattened undersurface. Prefers moderate to strong flow and well-oxygenated water reflecting its torrent-stream habitat. Nocturnal feeder.

description: A hardy callichthyid catfish from the Amazon, Orinoco, and Guianan drainages, closely related to Corydoras. Reaches ~15 cm and adapts to a wide range of conditions including oxygen-poor water, using a modified intestine for aerial respiration. Males build elaborate bubble nests for breeding. A peaceful community fish compatible with most tankmates, though males become territorial around active nests.
reproduction: Males build floating bubble nests; females deposit eggs inside. Males aggressively defend the nest until eggs hatch.

description: A large callichthyid catfish known by many local names — tamatá, atipa, hassa, kwi kwi, cascadura — across its range from Venezuela to Argentina. Reaches ~24–26 cm and is highly adaptable to poor-oxygen conditions using air-intestine breathing. Nocturnal omnivore feeding on detritus, insects, and microcrustaceans. Males build floating bubble nests triggered by seasonal rains. Hardy and easy to keep; introduced invasively in Florida.

A slender callichthyid catfish from the Amazon Basin named for the row of dark spots along its lateral line. Reaches ~10 cm and is completely peaceful — suitable for most community tanks with similarly sized fish. Groups of 3–6 are more active and exhibit interesting social behavior. Males build bubble nests for spawning. Accepts sinking pellets, bloodworm, brine shrimp, and chopped earthworms.

Similar to the porthole catfish but distinguishable by bold black-and-white striping on the caudal fin — the 'flagtail.' Reaches up to 12.5 cm and is completely peaceful. Groups of 3–6 show the most natural behavior. Males build bubble nests. Prefers slightly warmer water than the porthole catfish. Accepts the same varied diet of sinking foods and frozen invertebrates.

A venomous South Asian catfish whose pectoral spines carry a toxic secretion capable of causing severe, long-lasting pain — handle with extreme caution. Reaches 30–41 cm and uses a modified air sac to breathe atmospheric air in stagnant conditions. Gregarious; best kept in small groups. Peaceful toward similarly sized fish but will consume anything smaller. Feed meaty foods: earthworms, bloodworm, chopped prawn.
behavior: CAUTION: pectoral spines are venomous and cause severe pain. Use a wet towel or polythene bag when handling — never use a net. Obligate air-breather; must access the surface.

description: A bizarre ambush predator from Borneo, Malaysia, and Indonesia that mimics a dead leaf — mottled brown texture, flattened body, fringed margins. Reaches ~20 cm and is almost entirely immobile, waiting for prey to approach. Primarily requires live small fish and earthworms; weaning onto frozen foods is possible but difficult. Must be kept alone — will consume any fish or shrimp it can fit in its enormous mouth.
behavior: Motionless ambush predator. Wiggles maxillary barbels as a lure to attract prey. Known to produce a clicking sound.

description: One of the largest freshwater fish of Asia, the wallago or 'helicopter catfish' reaches 240 cm and is a supreme apex predator. Clearly unsuitable for any private aquarium. Found from Pakistan to Vietnam and Indonesia, lurking in deep pools and large rivers where it ambushes large fish. Even public aquaria rarely house adults. Adapts to whole fish, trout, and large prawns in captivity; large specimens feed once or twice weekly.

A large, robust loach from the Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, also sold as the redtail botia; adults develop a blue-grey body with an orange-red tail. Reaches 20–28 cm and must be kept in groups of 5 or more — solitary specimens become highly aggressive and destructive. Even in groups it may nip the fins of slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. A heavy, secure lid is essential as it is an adept jumper.

A medium-sized Mekong/Chao Phraya loach with an olive body and reddish fins, also called the silver loach. Reaches 12–17 cm and requires a group of at least 5; individuals kept in small groups direct aggression outward toward tankmates. Active, gregarious, and social within a proper group. Avoid very small or long-finned tankmates. Provide good flow, smooth pebbles, and hiding spots.

A small, peaceful loach from the Mekong basin with bold black-and-white striping, reaching 7–9 cm. Mild-mannered compared to most botia-type loaches; can be kept in community setups with active fish of similar temperament. Avoid slow-moving or long-finned species. Groups of 5 or more are essential — this is a social species that shows stress when kept in small numbers. Cooler water tolerance (down to 20°C) suits subtropical setups.

A large, impressively patterned loach from Sumatra, Borneo, and the Malay Peninsula, with greenish-gold body crossed by bold dark tiger stripes. Reaches up to 24 cm. Belligerent toward smaller fish and must be kept in groups of 5+ to diffuse intraspecific aggression; solitary specimens terrorize tankmates. Prefers warm, soft, slightly acidic water reflecting its blackwater/peat-swamp origins.

A large, striking loach from India, Myanmar, and central Thailand with bold tiger-stripe patterning and a vivid red tail. Reaches 17–25 cm and is one of the larger Syncrossus species. Belligerent; must be kept in groups of 5+ to reduce aggression toward tankmates. Suitable only for large tanks with robust, similarly sized inhabitants. Avid fin-nipper — avoid slow or long-finned species.

A vividly patterned loach from northern India and Bangladesh with alternating dark and pale vertical bands on an orange-gold body. Reaches 12–15 cm and must be kept in groups of 5 or more; solitary specimens become withdrawn or redirect aggression toward similarly-shaped fish. An active fin-nipper — avoid slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. Prefers clean, well-oxygenated water with moderate flow.

A striking loach from Myanmar and northeastern India with golden-yellow and dark wavy-stripe patterning that varies between river populations. Reaches 11–13 cm and requires groups of at least 5; individually kept fish become withdrawn or aggressive toward similar-shaped species. Not especially aggressive in a proper group but may nip fins of slow-moving or long-finned tankmates. Appreciates clean, well-oxygenated water with smooth pebbles.

A loach from the Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages with distinctive ladder-like dark saddle markings on a pale body. Typical aquarium specimens reach ~13 cm but wild fish can approach 25 cm; plan tank size accordingly. Requires groups of 5 or more — solitary fish become withdrawn or aggressive toward similar species. A cool-water tolerance down to 19°C makes it suitable for subtropical setups.

A uniformly dark, eel-like loach from the blackwater swamps and forest streams of Java, Sumatra, Borneo, and the Malay Peninsula, reaching 7–9 cm. Fully peaceful and suitable for community tanks with non-nippy fish. Groups of at least 5 are essential — solitary specimens spend virtually all their time hidden. Soft, acidic water with fine sandy substrate and leaf-litter cover best replicates its natural environment.

The largest of the kuhli loaches, this eel-like species from the Mekong basin reaches 9–12 cm. Fully peaceful and thrives in groups of 5 or more — solitary specimens remain hidden indefinitely. Inhabits slow-moving blackwater forest streams and swamps; soft, acidic water with fine substrate and ample cover replicates its natural environment. Can be kept alongside other peaceful community fish.

An eel-like loach from the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo with dark banding and pale undersides, reaching 7–9 cm. Fully peaceful and thrives in groups of 5 or more — solitary specimens hide indefinitely. Prefers soft, acidic blackwater conditions with a fine sandy substrate, leaf litter, and gentle filtration.

An eel-like loach from the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo with alternating dark and pale half-bands along the upper body, reaching up to 12 cm. Fully peaceful and gregarious; groups of 5 or more are essential as solitary fish hide continually. Prefers soft, acidic blackwater conditions with fine sandy substrate, leaf litter, and dim lighting.

A slender, eel-like loach from the Ganges and Brahmaputra river systems of northern India, Nepal, and Bangladesh that sifts sandy substrates for invertebrates. Typical aquarium specimens reach ~9 cm; wild fish can approach 15 cm. Peaceful with all tankmates. Groups of 4 or more reduce shyness. Tolerates slightly cooler water (to 20°C), consistent with its subtropical distribution.

A beautifully marked loach from the river systems of South and Southeast Asia, with intricate dark-spotted patterning on a pale body; typical aquarium specimens reach ~10 cm but wild fish can approach 15 cm. Peaceful and non-aggressive; mixes well with other substrate-dwellers. Groups of 6 or more reduce timidity. Prefers clean, well-oxygenated water with rocky or sandy substrate and moderate current.

A small, stout loach from the Tenasserim river system of Myanmar and western Thailand with bold dark banding on a pale body, reaching 6–8 cm. Less aggressive than many Schistura species but still territorial; groups need ample rocky hiding spots and broken sight lines to reduce skirmishes. Not suited to general community tanks — best kept with robust, similarly sized bottom-dwellers.

A stunning hillstream loach from fast-flowing streams in Vietnam and Laos with an intricate reticulated pattern of pale lines on a dark body. Reaches 5–7 cm. Territorial toward its own kind and similarly-shaped species; a group of 6 or more helps establish a hierarchy and display natural behavior. Requires high flow, excellent oxygenation, and smooth boulders to graze; standard aquarium conditions will not suffice.

A small hillstream loach from the fast-flowing rocky streams of Borneo with a pale body covered in dark spots. Reaches 6–8 cm. Peaceful in groups of 5 or more and safe with most non-territorial community fish. Requires very high current, strong oxygenation, smooth rocks for grazing, and regular access to biofilm; unsuitable for standard still-water setups.

A flattened, butterfly-shaped hillstream loach from fast-flowing rivers in southern China, reaching ~6 cm. Peaceful and suitable for species-specific hillstream setups or community tanks with similarly-sized, current-loving fish. Prefers slightly harder, neutral to mildly alkaline water reflecting its Chinese river origins. Requires very strong flow, excellent oxygenation, and biofilm-covered rocks to graze.

A striking hillstream loach endemic to headwater streams in Guangxi, China, with bold black-and-white panda-like patterning. Reaches 5–7 cm. Relatively peaceful but somewhat territorial within its own kind; groups of 4 or more show more natural behavior. Juveniles display vivid coloration that may fade with age. Requires very strong flow, cool water (20–24°C), excellent oxygenation, and biofilm-covered surfaces.

A large hillstream loach endemic to fast-flowing streams in Borneo with bold saddle-like dark markings on a pale body. Typical aquarium specimens reach ~10 cm; wild fish can approach 14 cm. Peaceful; bolder in groups of 6 or more. Requires very strong current, high oxygen levels, biofilm-covered rocks, and supplemental live or frozen invertebrates — a demanding species that rewards dedicated hillstream setups.

A colourful Australian gudgeon with a pale body peppered with purple-blue and orange spots. Typical specimens reach ~7 cm, but wild fish can grow to 14 cm; plan for the larger adult. Will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow — restrict companions to robust, similarly-sized fish such as rainbowfish. Can be kept singly or as a pair. Currently considered endangered in the wild, most trade specimens are captive-bred.

A spectacularly coloured Australian gudgeon from northern and eastern river systems with electric-blue, orange, and red markings on males during breeding. Reaches 10–13 cm. Highly territorial between males; best kept singly or as a pair in a well-decorated tank with broken sight lines. Adapts to an unusually wide range of water conditions (pH 5–9, 20–30°C). Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved as larvae require a marine developmental stage.

A spectacular, jewel-coloured goby endemic to hill streams in Sumatra, where males display brilliant neon iridescence. Reaches 6–8 cm. Males are loosely territorial but several can coexist with adequate space. A dedicated biofilm grazer; the tank must be well-established with abundant algae on smooth rocks and require strong flow. Not suited to standard community setups or newly established aquariums.

A small, brilliantly coloured goby from clear hill streams across Indonesia, the Philippines, and the Solomon Islands. Males display vivid red-orange and blue iridescence; females are drab. Reaches 4–6 cm. Requires a mature tank with abundant biofilm on rocks and strong current. Males are territorial toward each other; keep as one male with two or more females. Breeding in captivity requires marine conditions for larval development.

A tiny, stunning goby from clear hill streams in Japan, Taiwan, the Philippines, and southern China, with males displaying intense blue-purple iridescence. Reaches 4–6 cm. A specialist biofilm grazer requiring a mature, well-established tank with abundant algae on smooth rocks and strong flow. Keep as one male with two or more females. Multiple males can coexist in larger, well-structured setups.

A small, stocky goby from cool, fast-flowing streams in southern China and Vietnam with mottled brown-and-white patterning and distinctive pale cheeks. Reaches ~7 cm. Males are territorial toward each other but peaceful toward dissimilar-shaped tankmates. Prefers cool, well-oxygenated, neutral to slightly hard water; suitable for unheated setups alongside temperate fish like danios and white cloud minnows.

A minute, brilliantly coloured goby endemic to a handful of streams near Lianhua Mountain in Guangdong Province, China; males display striking scarlet-orange markings. Reaches only ~4 cm. IUCN Endangered in the wild — ensure any specimens are captive-bred. Males are territorial but tolerate each other with adequate cover; keep at least two males plus females. Requires cool, clear, well-oxygenated water with a varied live/frozen diet.

A beautiful non-annual killifish from coastal forest streams of Equatorial Guinea and Gabon with a yellow-green body bearing rows of vivid red dots and a lyre-shaped tail. Reaches 5–6 cm. Peaceful and relatively undemanding; a pair or trio (one male, two females) can be kept in a small, heavily planted tank. An accomplished jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water.

A compact, vividly coloured killifish from coastal forest streams in Togo, Benin, and southern Nigeria with two bold dark lateral stripes and iridescent blue-green flanks on males. Reaches ~5 cm. Peaceful and easy to breed; a pair or small harem does well in a dedicated species tank with floating plants and leaf litter. Sensitive to water quality — keep nitrates low with frequent small water changes. A tight lid is essential.

A striking non-annual killifish from the middle and lower Congo basin with a greenish body densely spotted with vivid red dots and an elaborately patterned tail. Reaches 4–5 cm. Peaceful and beginner-friendly for a killifish; a pair or harem thrives in a well-planted 40 L tank. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water and a diet of small live or frozen invertebrates. A tight-fitting lid is essential to prevent escapes.

A compact, colourful non-annual killifish from swamps and forest streams in Benin, Nigeria, and Cameroon. Males are brilliantly marked with blue-green flanks, red spotting, and a banner-like dorsal fin. Reaches ~5 cm. Peaceful; keep as a pair or trio in a species tank with floating plants and leaf litter. Prefers slightly acidic, soft water. A tight lid is mandatory — this species is an exceptional jumper.

A spectacularly patterned non-annual killifish from forest streams in southeastern Nigeria, Cameroon, Equatorial Guinea, and northwestern Gabon. Males display an astonishing mosaic of red, blue, and green markings. Reaches 5–6 cm. Shy and easily intimidated; best kept in a species tank. Pair spawning on fine-leaved plants is reliable. A tight-fitting lid is essential as the species jumps readily.

A vivid non-annual killifish from coastal swamps and forest brooks of Gabon with males displaying rich blue-green iridescence and red spotting. Reaches ~5 cm. Peaceful and suitable for a small species tank with floating plants, peat substrate, and leaf litter. Prefers slightly acidic, soft water with moderate temperatures. A tight-fitting lid is essential — killifish of this genus are accomplished jumpers.

A cool-water non-annual killifish from inland plateau streams of Gabon and the Republic of Congo with males bearing bold broken-line red patterning on blue-green flanks. Reaches 4–5 cm. Notably cooler temperature requirements (18–22°C) than most Aphyosemion. Peaceful and best maintained as a pair or trio in a species tank with acidic, very soft water and peat filtration. A secure lid is essential.

A peaceful, shy killifish from forest streams of the lower Cross River system in southeastern Nigeria and Cameroon. Males are brilliantly coloured with blue, red, and yellow markings and an elaborate lyre-shaped tail. Reaches ~6 cm. Best kept in a species or species-heavy tank away from competitive feeders. Accepts high-quality flake but thrives on live or frozen invertebrates. Secure the tank — this species jumps.

One of the larger Fundulopanchax killifish, endemic to the lower Sanaga basin in western Cameroon. Males are spectacularly marked with vivid blue, red, and orange iridescence on an olive-brown body. Reaches ~7 cm. Can be kept as a pair or trio; males may quarrel so provide ample hiding spots. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water. A non-annual species that is considered vulnerable in the wild; most available specimens are captive-bred.

A delicate, feather-finned killifish from coastal swamps and brooks in Togo, Benin, and southwestern Nigeria. Males carry elongated, filamentous fin extensions that give the species its common name. Reaches 5–6 cm. Peaceful and easy to breed; a pair or trio thrives in a species tank with floating plants, leaf litter, and peat filtration. Keep temperatures cool (21–23°C) and a tight lid fitted at all times.

A colourful but aggressive killifish from forest streams in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana. Males display intricate blue, red, and yellow markings. Reaches 6–7 cm. Despite its modest size, it is predatory toward any fish that fits in its mouth and quite aggressive for a killifish; best kept in a species-only setup as a pair or trio. Prefers cooler water (20–23°C) — excess heat reduces lifespan significantly. A secure lid is essential.

A tiny annual killifish endemic to the seasonal pools of Unguja Island (Zanzibar), Tanzania. Males are brilliantly coloured with vivid red, orange, and blue markings. Reaches 3–4 cm. An annual species with a lifespan of less than a year in the wild; in captivity specimens can live 1–2 years with stable conditions. Males quarrel — keep one male with two or more females. Eggs must be air-dried in peat for ~3 months to hatch.

An annual killifish from the seasonal floodplains of the lower Rufiji River delta in Tanzania. Males are richly coloured in red and blue. Reaches 5–6 cm. Peaceful and manageable in captivity; a pair can be kept in a 40 L tank with peat substrate for egg collection. As an annual species its lifespan is naturally short (1–2 years); eggs require dry storage for several months before hatching. Prefers hard, alkaline water reflecting its East African origins.

An annual killifish endemic to the Mafia Islands off the coast of Tanzania. Males display vivid yellow-orange and red patterning with intricate spotting. Reaches ~5 cm. An annual species with a natural lifespan under one year; eggs require dry peat storage for 2–4 months before hatching. Males compete — keep as one male with multiple females. Notably prefers unusually acidic water (pH 5.8–6.4) compared to most East African killifish.

A vivid annual killifish from coastal seasonal pools in Kenya, Somalia, and Ethiopia. Males display brilliant blue and red coloration. Reaches ~5 cm. An annual species requiring egg diapause in dry peat for 2–3 months; eggs should be checked and incubated at high humidity. Males fight — one male to several females is the recommended setup. Prefers slightly acidic to neutral water with peat filtration.

An annual killifish from the temporary pools and swamps of central Mozambique. Males are brilliantly coloured in red, orange, and blue. Reaches 4–5 cm. Inhabits pools that can reach extreme temperatures in the dry season — in captivity, maintain at 24–28°C to replicate warm tropical conditions. An annual species requiring egg diapause; eggs are stored in moist peat for several months before hatching. Males compete aggressively.

An annual killifish from the river drainages of eastern Tanzania (Ruvu, Mpiji, and lower Rufuji). Males reach ~6 cm with vivid coloration; females are smaller (~4 cm) and cryptically coloured. IUCN Vulnerable in the wild. A relatively straightforward Nothobranchius to keep; a pair does well at 20–24°C with peat substrate. Eggs incubate dry for 2–4 months. Males compete — keep one male with two or more females.

An annual killifish from the coastal plains of Kenya and Tanzania with males bearing red-tipped scales on a blue-green body. Reaches ~5 cm. One of the cooler-water Nothobranchius (18–22°C); suitable for an unheated room-temperature setup in temperate climates. An annual species with a very short natural lifespan; eggs require dry peat storage for several months before re-hydration. Males are aggressive toward each other.

An annual killifish from the Zanzibar Archipelago and adjacent coastal mainland rivers of Tanzania and Kenya. Males display black-spotted iridescent patterning. Reaches ~6 cm. An annual species; eggs require dry peat storage for 2–3 months before hatching. Males compete — keep one male with two or more females. Slightly wider temperature tolerance (22–28°C) than some Nothobranchius. A secure lid is essential.

A popular surface-dwelling killifish from coastal streams in Liberia, Côte d'Ivoire, and Ghana. Males have a vivid red chin; bodies are olive with fine horizontal dotted striping. Reaches ~6 cm. Will eat any fish small enough to swallow — avoid small nano fish as tankmates. Otherwise peaceful and easy to keep. Prefers slightly cool water (21–23°C) and a well-planted tank with floating cover. A tight lid is mandatory.

One of the larger Epiplatys panchax, with a greenish-gold body bearing six bold dark vertical bars. Wild fish approach 12 cm; typical aquarium specimens are 8 cm. Groups of 6 or more show more natural behavior. Will predate very small fish and invertebrates but otherwise peaceful in a community with similar-sized inhabitants. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water and a well-planted setup with floating plants. A secure lid is essential.

A widespread surface-hunting killifish found from Pakistan and India through Southeast Asia and into Sri Lanka. The typical aquarium form is ~5 cm but wild fish can reach 9 cm. A voracious surface predator that will eat any fish, shrimp, or fry that fits in its mouth — mix only with fish too large to be preyed upon. Hardy and adaptable across a wide pH range. A tight lid is essential; this species jumps readily.

A large, brilliantly coloured surface-hunting killifish endemic to Sri Lanka. Males display striking iridescent green-blue scales; females are more cryptically coloured. Reaches up to 10 cm. IUCN Endangered in the wild — ensure specimens are captive-bred. Predatory; will eat any fish or invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth. Best kept with fish of comparable size. Requires a heavily planted tank and a secure, tight-fitting lid.

A small, attractively patterned catfish from the blackwater rivers and peat swamps of Sumatra, recognizable by a dark spot on the shoulder and a second on the caudal peduncle. Peaceful with fish of all sizes. Requires warm, very soft, and acidic water that mirrors its natural peat-swamp habitat.
behavior: Primarily nocturnal and prefers dense cover or driftwood. Peaceful toward all other species and unlikely to bother even very small tankmates.

description: A popular West African killifish prized for the vivid iridescent blue-green gleam around its eyes that gave it the 'lampeye' name. Hardy and peaceful, it suits planted nano tanks and thrives in groups of six or more. Readily accepts Artemia, Daphnia, and small frozen or flake foods at the surface.
behavior: Active shoaling species that spends most of its time near the surface. Best kept in groups of at least 6 to feel secure and display its best colors.

description: A spectacular lampeye killifish endemic to just two river systems in southwestern Cameroon. Males reach 6 cm and display striking iridescent eyes and vivid fins; females are considerably smaller at around 4 cm. Shy and easily outcompeted, it requires pristine, cool, soft-to-neutral water and a diet of live or frozen invertebrates. Rarely encountered in the trade.
dimorphism: Males grow to 6 cm and are more colorful with iridescent finnage; females are much smaller at around 4 cm.

description: A stunning annual killifish from seasonal coastal pools in southeastern Brazil, critically endangered in the wild. Males display rows of vivid pearlescent spots and striking red-blue fins. In captivity, eggs are collected and stored through a dry diapause period before re-wetting triggers hatching. Best kept as a pair or small trio and not mixed with aggressive species.
behavior: Annual species that completes its lifecycle in seasonal pools that dry periodically. Males are aggressive toward each other and should not be housed together.
reproduction: Egg-burying annual killifish; eggs require a dry diapause of several months before re-wetting triggers hatching.
dimorphism: Males are brightly colored with pearlescent spots and vivid fins; females are more cryptically patterned and slightly smaller.

description: A robust killifish endemic to the granitic Seychelles islands and the only freshwater killifish native to those islands. Males display golden-yellow scales with red-trimmed fins. A surface-hunting predator that will consume fish small enough to swallow — keep only with species of similar or greater size. Hardy and tolerant of a wide pH range, but a secure lid is essential as it jumps readily.
behavior: Active surface swimmer. Males are territorial with each other; best kept as a single male with females or one specimen per tank.
reproduction: Egg scatterer that deposits eggs among floating plants. Eggs hatch in around two weeks.
dimorphism: Males are more colorful with vivid golden-yellow flanks and red fin margins; females are smaller and more cryptically colored.

description: A vibrant rainbowfish endemic to a single highland lake in Papua New Guinea and listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN. Peaceful and best kept in schools of 6 or more. Wild specimens can reach 15 cm but aquarium-grown fish typically stay closer to 9 cm. Requires stable, alkaline water and ample open swimming space alongside dense plantings.
behavior: Active mid-water schooler. Males display vivid colors and spar harmlessly during courtship, but no injury results.

description: A small, active rainbowfish from northeastern Australia and Papua New Guinea, rarely exceeding 7 cm. Males develop striking iridescent coloration when mature and in prime condition. Best kept in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted aquarium with gentle flow. Water chemistry varies by population, so match parameters to the collection locality.

A hardy, adaptable rainbowfish native to the Murray-Darling basin of southeastern Australia. Males display silvery-blue and yellow coloration and can reach 11 cm; females are smaller and duller. Unusually tolerant of cooler temperatures. Active and fast-moving — avoid housing with very small or slow-moving tankmates. Keep in shoals of 6 or more.

A large, active rainbowfish from New Guinea rivers including the Markham, Ramu, and Sepik systems. Multiple geographic variants with different coloration are available in the hobby. Peaceful but boisterous — best suited to spacious tanks with other robust fish. Requires good filtration and open swimming space alongside planted areas.

A large, seldom-seen rainbowfish from swamps, backwaters, and rivers of southern New Guinea. Males develop impressive coloration with age. Requires warm, neutral to slightly alkaline water and a long tank with open swimming space. Peaceful with similarly sized fish; keep in groups of 6 or more.

A medium-sized rainbowfish from tropical northern Australia, found in rainforest streams, lily lagoons, and slow-flowing coastal waterways. Males show dark banding along the body and vivid fin coloration. Prefers softer, slightly acidic water. Keep in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted tank; popular in Australian aquarium circles.

A robust rainbowfish from the Kimberley and Pilbara regions of northwestern Australia, tolerating a wide range of habitats from rivers and creeks to swamps and reservoirs. Males develop vivid coloration that takes over 12 months to fully mature. Active and boisterous; keep in groups of 6 or more alongside similarly sized fish. Requires a spacious, well-filtered tank.

A critically endangered rainbowfish endemic to Lake Wanam in Papua New Guinea, now virtually extinct in the wild due to invasive tilapia. Captive populations are maintained by enthusiasts and are the only source for the hobby. Males develop stunning emerald-green iridescence. Requires warm, alkaline water; will not thrive in soft or acidic conditions. A conservation priority species.

An endangered rainbowfish endemic to Lake Holmes in West Papua, Indonesia. Males are impressively large and colorful, developing vivid fin extensions with age. Requires neutral to alkaline, moderately hard water. A specialist species seldom seen outside dedicated rainbowfish collections; keep in groups of 6 or more in a large, well-maintained aquarium.

A critically endangered micro blue-eye endemic to a few rainforest streams near Kiunga in Papua New Guinea's upper Fly River system. Males display ornate elongated fin rays and vivid markings. Extremely rare in the hobby; best kept as a species-only tank with live micro-foods. Requires stable, cool, neutral water and densely planted conditions.

An endangered rainbowfish endemic to the Mananjary River basin of eastern Madagascar, often confused in the trade with the related Bedotia madagascariensis. Males develop a characteristic red tail. Prefers cool, clean, alkaline water with moderate hardness; sensitive to pollution. Best kept in groups of 6 or more in a well-planted aquarium with good filtration.

An elegant rainbowfish from a small area near Maros, Sulawesi, Indonesia, distinguished by elongated dorsal and anal fin rays in males. Vulnerable in the wild. Requires neutral to alkaline, moderately hard water — will not thrive in acidic conditions. Very sensitive to water quality swings. Best kept as a shoal of 6+ with a 2-3:1 female-to-male ratio.

A delicate rainbowfish from coastal wallum wetlands of southeastern Queensland and northern New South Wales, Australia. Males are notably larger and more colorful than females. Inhabits tannin-stained, acidic, slow-moving water with abundant vegetation — replicating these conditions in captivity is essential. Rarely seen outside Australian native fish enthusiast circles.

An endangered rainbowfish endemic to a small number of fast-flowing rainforest streams in the Wet Tropics of northeastern Queensland, Australia. Monotypic — the sole species in its genus. Requires cool, well-oxygenated, neutral to slightly alkaline water with strong flow. Rarely available in the hobby; best suited to experienced keepers with appropriate setups.

A large, popular livebearer from coastal marshes and estuaries of the southeastern US and Mexico. Males develop an impressive dorsal fin used in courtship displays. Requires hard, alkaline water with vegetable matter in the diet; will decline in soft or acidic conditions. Males pursue females relentlessly — keep more females than males. Tolerates brackish water.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 10–140 fry every 3–6 weeks. Males relentlessly pursue females — maintain at least 2 females per male.

description: The largest of the mollies, native to the Yucatan Peninsula. Males develop an enormous sail-like dorsal fin and reach 15 cm. More demanding than the common sailfin molly — requires warm, hard, alkaline water and a large tank. Provide plenty of algae and vegetable matter. Does not require salt but tolerates brackish conditions.
reproduction: Livebearer; females store sperm and can produce multiple broods from a single mating.

description: A hardy, widespread livebearer from rivers, springs, and estuaries of Mexico and Central America — the wild ancestor of many hybrid molly varieties sold in the hobby. Feeds primarily on detritus, algae, and plant matter. Peaceful and adaptable; tolerates a range of water conditions including brackish. Unique cave-adapted populations also exist.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 30–80 fry approximately 28 days after fertilization.

description: A biologically unique all-female livebearer from southern Texas and northeastern Mexico that reproduces through gynogenesis — females require sperm from a related male molly species to trigger reproduction, but incorporate none of the male's DNA. Rarely sold commercially; mostly of interest to researchers and specialists. Peaceful and undemanding to keep.
reproduction: All-female species; reproduces gynogenetically using sperm from males of related Poecilia species as a developmental trigger without male genetic contribution.

description: A tiny, jewel-like blue-eye from Australia, southern New Guinea, and the Aru Islands, rarely exceeding 4 cm. Males have elaborate elongated fins tipped with vivid markings and display constantly to one another. Too easily outcompeted for most community tanks; best in a species-only or carefully chosen micro-tank setup. Requires live or very small foods and densely planted conditions.

A variable blue-eye endemic to eastern Australia, ranging from small southern forms of around 3 cm to large northern males reaching 7 cm or more. Adapts readily to captivity and breeds freely in planted aquaria. Males can be intolerant of each other when spawning. Prefers live or frozen foods but accepts quality dried foods. Best kept in groups of 8 or more.

An endangered blue-eye from coastal wallum heathland habitats of southern Queensland, Australia, found in tannin-stained, acidic slow-moving waters. Males develop a warm golden-amber tone with decorated fins. Requires soft, acidic water; will struggle in hard or alkaline tanks. A conservation priority species best suited to a carefully managed nano or species tank.

A small, peaceful blue-eye from sunlit swamps, billabongs, and slow streams of northern Australia and New Guinea. Males have elegantly elongated fin rays and display vivid coloration. Prefers warm water with dense surface vegetation. Best kept as a species tank or with other small, non-competitive fish. Feed live or frozen micro-foods for best results.

A pike-shaped, highly predatory livebearer from weedy canals, lagoons, and marshes from Mexico to Nicaragua. Females are much larger than males and will eat fish approaching their own size. Must be kept with tankmates too large to swallow. Tolerates a wide range of temperatures and even brackish water. Not for community tanks with smaller fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to small broods of 10–30 large fry.

description: A hardy, colorful platy from warm springs and slow-moving waters of northeastern Mexico, noted for highly variable color patterns that have driven extensive selective breeding. Tolerates cooler temperatures than most other livebearers. Peaceful and undemanding; thrives in hard, alkaline water with a planted setup. An excellent beginner fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 10–50 fry every 4–6 weeks.

description: A distinctive wild swordtail from northeastern Mexico, notable for its horizontally-extended sword (unlike the downward-angled sword of most swordtails) and metallic green coloration. Primarily kept by livebearer enthusiasts rather than the mainstream hobby. Requires hard, alkaline water. Males can harass females — maintain more females than males.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to small broods of live fry.

description: The smallest swordtail species, endemic to the Rio Axtla in northeastern Mexico. Lacks the typical sword of larger swordtails. Requires cool, hard water with excellent filtration and frequent water changes — more delicate than common swordtails. Feeds on algae and plant material among dense vegetation. Primarily available through specialist livebearer breeders.
reproduction: Livebearer; females produce small broods of rarely more than 10 fry.

description: A tiny, rarely seen livebearer endemic to the Soto La Marina River system in Tamaulipas, Mexico, inhabiting springs, streams, and slow-moving ponds. Named for the spike-like extension of the tail fin in males. Peaceful and suitable for a small, carefully managed tank. Prefers cool, hard water and dense aquatic vegetation. Mainly found with specialist breeders.
reproduction: Livebearer; gestation is 24–35 days with 20–50 fry per brood.

description: A small, hardy livebearer endemic to fast-flowing, hard-water streams in Jamaica. Males display attractive dark barring and are notably less cannibalistic toward fry than many livebearers. Peaceful and easy to keep; does best in hard, alkaline water with dense planting. An excellent choice for a small livebearer species tank or Caribbean biotope.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to 20–80 fry approximately every 4 weeks.

description: A relatively large limia endemic to southeastern Hispaniola, tolerant of an unusually wide range of salinities from clear springs to hypersaline coastal lagoons. Males develop vivid blue and yellow coloration and establish social hierarchies with competitive displays. Better suited to experienced livebearer keepers than absolute beginners; requires hard or brackish water and a well-structured tank.
reproduction: Livebearer; produces numerous fry with no parental care.

description: The largest of the commonly kept limias, endemic to Cuba where it inhabits a wide variety of habitats including streams, lakes, estuaries, and mangrove swamps. Females are substantially larger than males. Tolerates cooler temperatures than most tropical livebearers; thrives in hard, alkaline or slightly brackish water. Peaceful and compatible with other robust hard-water fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; females give birth to live fry in regular broods.

description: A small, attractive Cuban livebearer with a distinctive black stripe running along the male's underside. Females are notably larger than the slender males. Prefers medium-hard, slightly alkaline water and appreciates a well-planted tank with floating cover. Omnivorous; requires vegetable matter in the diet. Best kept as a species tank or with other hard-water tolerant small fish.
reproduction: Livebearer; gestation is approximately 24 days producing 15–30 fry.

description: One of the world's smallest fish and the smallest North American livebearer, endemic to heavily vegetated, slow-moving waters of the southeastern United States. Uses superfoetation — continuously birthing single fry rather than discrete broods. Requires very gentle filtration, dense planting, and tankmates of similar size. Easy to keep but not suitable for typical community tanks due to its tiny size.
reproduction: Livebearer with superfoetation; births 1–5 fry continuously over 10–14 days per cycle.

description: A hardy, peaceful livebearer from Atlantic drainages of Guatemala through Panama, inhabiting swampy, vegetation-rich creeks and slow streams. Females are notably larger than the small, slender males. Feeds primarily on detritus, diatoms, and fine organic matter. Easy to keep and breed; a good alternative to the common guppy for soft-substrate, planted setups.
reproduction: Livebearer; females bear 10–80 fry after approximately 4 weeks gestation.

description: An insectivorous livebearer from Pacific-slope streams of Costa Rica and Panama, known for attractive coloration and feisty behavior. Adults are highly cannibalistic toward their own fry, making breeding challenging without separation. Best kept as a species tank or with robust, similarly sized fish. Prefers live or frozen invertebrate foods alongside dry diets.
reproduction: Livebearer; adults must be separated from fry immediately due to cannibalism.

description: A striking goodeid livebearer endemic to the Ameca River drainage in Jalisco, Mexico, now critically endangered in the wild. Males display a vivid yellow-tipped tail. Voracious omnivore that will consume plant material, so use robust vegetation. Active and competitive; may nip fins of slower tankmates. An important species for conservation-minded aquarists, with captive populations preserving its survival.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished via trophotaenia and born fully formed in broods of 10–30.

description: A boldly colored goodeid livebearer from the Grande de Santiago River basin in western Mexico, listed as Endangered in the wild. Males develop a vivid orange-red caudal peduncle. Tolerates a wide temperature range and is relatively undemanding, but may occasionally nip fins — this is reduced when kept in groups. Requires vegetable matter in the diet; an important species for goodeid conservation programs.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished internally and born fully developed.

description: The most widespread goodeid, distributed across nine Mexican states from highland lakes and rivers. Larger and more robust than most goodeids, with males displaying dark fin margins. Prefers cool, hard, alkaline water — unsuitable for typical tropical setups. Feeds on algae, plant matter, and invertebrates. A hardy, long-lived goodeid with Least Concern conservation status, unlike many of its relatives.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished via trophotaenia and born in moderate-sized broods.

description: A tiny goodeid livebearer from the Río Teuchitlán in Jalisco, Mexico, now Extinct in the Wild since 1996 due to competition from introduced platies. Survival depends entirely on captive breeding programs by dedicated goodeid enthusiasts. Grazes on benthic diatoms and algae; prefers a planted tank.
