Keeping this species is a genuine conservation act.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished internally via trophotaenia.

description: A critically endangered goodeid from shallow springs and marshes in Durango and Coahuila, Mexico. Males display attractive multi-colored flanks. Primarily herbivorous; feeds on algae and aquatic vegetation in heavily planted tanks. Threatened in the wild by invasive species and habitat loss — captive populations kept by specialist breeders are vital to the species' survival.
reproduction: Goodeid livebearer; fry are nourished internally and born fully formed.

description: A surface-dwelling livebearer from the fast-flowing mountain streams of South Sulawesi, featuring the characteristic elongated lower jaw of halfbeaks. Feeds primarily on flying insects at the surface; accepts floating dried foods but thrives on live or frozen invertebrates. Males may be aggressive toward each other; keep as a trio or more females than males. Requires a tightly covered tank as it jumps readily.
reproduction: Livebearer; females produce small broods of 5–20 large, well-developed fry.

description: A slender, surface-cruising halfbeak from lowland forest streams, rivers, and swamps of southern Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. Feeds primarily on invertebrates at or above the water surface. Prefers soft, slightly acidic to neutral water with abundant floating cover. Males may spar; keep with ample space. A tight-fitting lid is essential as this species jumps.
reproduction: Livebearer; produces small broods of large fry after extended gestation.

description: A remarkably tough, adaptable livebearer native to the central and eastern United States, widely and controversially introduced worldwide for mosquito control. Highly aggressive toward other fish — notorious for fin-nipping and harassing tankmates far larger than itself. Not recommended for community tanks. Hardy across an exceptional temperature range. Considered an invasive pest in many countries outside its native range.
reproduction: Livebearer; females bear 50–200 fry multiple times per season after a single mating.

description: A delicate, slow-moving beauty from blackwater peat swamps of Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo. Requires very soft, highly acidic water — arguably the most demanding of the commonly sold gouramis. Feeds primarily on small live invertebrates; initially reluctant to accept prepared foods. Unusual among labyrinth fish as a maternal mouthbrooder. Keep in a species tank or with other blackwater specialists; avoid boisterous tankmates.
reproduction: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 30–40 eggs for 2 weeks.

description: A small chocolate gourami from the blackwater peat swamps of South Kalimantan, Borneo. Distinguished from Sphaerichthys osphromenoides by bold, regularly spaced crossbands. Like its relatives, demands very soft, acidic blackwater conditions and live or frozen invertebrate foods. A paternal mouthbrooder. Best maintained as a species tank or with other blackwater specialists.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood.

description: A cryptic chocolate gourami from small, shaded forest creeks with abundant leaf litter and wood debris in Kalimantan, Borneo. Mimics a dead leaf as camouflage. Like all Sphaerichthys, it demands highly acidic blackwater with negligible hardness and feeds exclusively on small live invertebrates in the wild. Mouthbrooding species; rarely encountered in the hobby.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder; incubation by one parent for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A tiny, jewel-like gourami endemic to the blackwater peat swamps of Bangka Island, now Endangered due to habitat loss. Males display spectacular nuptial colors — iridescent blue and red fin margins — during courtship. Requires extremely soft, acidic water with near-zero hardness and very gentle flow. Feeds only on tiny live invertebrates. A specialist species for the dedicated blackwater aquarist.
reproduction: Cave spawner; male guards the bubble nest and mouthbroods the fry.

description: One of the smallest and most rarely kept licorice gouramis, from blackwater streams of West Kalimantan, Borneo. Tolerates slightly cooler temperatures than most Parosphromenus species. Like all licorice gouramis, demands extremely soft, acidic water and lives only on tiny live foods such as Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, and micro worms. A conservation-priority blackwater specialist.
reproduction: Cave spawner; male mouthbroods or guards the fry in a bubble nest.

description: A tiny licorice gourami from blackwater peat swamp streams in Kalimantan, Borneo. Males display three colour forms — silver, brownish, or red-spotted — and perform elaborate courtship displays. Considered slightly more forgiving than some congeners but still demands very soft, acidic water and a diet of small live invertebrates. Best kept as a species tank or with tiny, non-competitive blackwater cyprinids.
reproduction: Cave spawner; male guards or mouthbroods the small brood.

description: One of the most gentle and easy-going gouramis, endemic to Myanmar. Distinguished by its characteristically thick lips used to pick invertebrates from surface vegetation. Shy and slow-moving; does best in a heavily planted tank with calm tankmates and minimal water flow. Accepts most prepared and frozen foods. A great community gourami for beginners who want something other than the common blue or dwarf gourami.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards nest and fry.

description: A colorful, moderately sized gourami found across the Indian subcontinent and Myanmar. Males display vivid blue and orange banding and are more assertive than the closely related thick-lipped gourami, with potential inter-male aggression in smaller tanks. Unfussy eater; thrives in planted tanks with floating vegetation and gentle filtration. A rewarding and underappreciated species in the hobby.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest and guards fry.

description: A large, hardy gourami widely distributed across Southeast Asia and commonly farmed as a food fish. Bears attractive snakeskin-patterned scaling. Primarily herbivorous, grazing heavily on algae, plant material, and phytoplankton. Generally peaceful but its size means it will consume very small tankmates. Tolerant of a wide range of water conditions; one of the most adaptable labyrinth fish.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs a large floating nest.

description: A rarely kept, elegant gourami from slow, heavily vegetated waters of India and Bangladesh. Notable for tolerating unusually cool temperatures. Often arrives in poor condition from import; susceptible to ectoparasites. Despite a peaceful disposition overall, adults can be openly hostile toward conspecifics — best kept singly or in a single pair in a well-planted, species-appropriate tank. Feeds on small live invertebrates.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder; male broods the eggs and fry.

description: A remarkable ambush predator from blackwater peat swamps with one of the most protrusible jaws of any fish, capable of extending its mouth a third of its body length in a lightning lunge. Primarily eats live fish; very difficult to wean onto dead prey. Requires highly acidic, very soft blackwater with minimal flow. For specialist aquarists only — not compatible with any fish it can swallow.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder in some populations; incubation details vary by locality.

description: A large, aggressive combtail endemic to the rivers and streams of Sri Lanka, recognizable by its spiky, comb-like caudal fin extensions. Highly aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly shaped fish — not suitable for community tanks. Can coexist with robust, schooling cyprinids in a spacious setup. Feeds on invertebrates and molluscs; accepts earthworms and larger frozen foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest aggressively.

description: A large, territorial anabantoid from peat swamps and slow-moving waters of the Malay Peninsula and Greater Sunda Islands. The largest combtail species; requires substantial space and robust tankmates that can defend themselves. Predatory and aggressive — will eat smaller fish and fight with conspecifics. Warmer water than the Sri Lankan combtail; tolerates a wider pH range.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards eggs and fry.

description: A small, strikingly banded African anabantoid from slow forest streams of the Congo Basin, including the Chiloango River. Very shy and easily intimidated; benefits from small dither fish and dense planting to encourage activity. Feeds on worms and aquatic invertebrates; requires live or frozen foods. Spawns nocturnally using a floating bubble nest like its relatives.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; spawns nocturnally among floating plants.

description: A large, predatory African climbing perch from coastal rivers spanning Senegal to the Congo, inhabiting both slow and fast-moving waters. Will consume any fish or invertebrate that fits in its mouth. Patterned body provides excellent camouflage. Relatively hardy and tolerant of varied water parameters for an anabantoid. Best kept as a centrepiece predator with large, robust tankmates.
reproduction: Egg scatterer; pelagic eggs hatch in 24–36 hours with no parental care.

description: A cold-tolerant paradisefish from rice paddies, ponds, and slow streams of China and Korea. Exceptionally hardy — can survive near-freezing temperatures and even remain active when shallow water bodies freeze over. Lacks the elongated tail extensions of the common paradise fish; has a rounded caudal fin. Males are territorial with each other but manageable in a spacious planted tank with robust tankmates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest and guards eggs and fry.

description: A dark, attractively marked paradisefish endemic to Vietnam, closely related to the common paradise fish but with deeper, more uniform dark pigmentation. Hardy and adaptable; tolerates a wide temperature range. Males are territorial toward each other but similar in behavior to Macropodus opercularis. Does best in a planted tank with plenty of cover and similarly-sized tankmates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A rarely encountered paradisefish endemic to hill streams of the Quang Tri River basin in central Vietnam, inhabiting areas with submerged roots and overhanging vegetation. Distinguished by its striking red dorsal and caudal coloration. Care requirements are similar to other Macropodus species — tolerates a range of temperatures, prefers a planted tank, and males are territorial with one another.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A small croaking gourami from rice paddies, marshes, and swamps of the Mekong basin, closely related to but larger than the dwarf croaking gourami. Like its relatives, produces audible croaking sounds during courtship and territorial encounters using its pectoral fins and swimbladder. Peaceful and undemanding; suitable for smaller planted community tanks. Feeds on small invertebrates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fan the eggs.

description: A massive, endangered giant gourami endemic to the Kinabatangan and Segama river basins of Sabah, Borneo. Juveniles display vivid red fin coloration that develops fully with age. Like the common giant gourami, it requires a truly enormous tank and generates heavy bioload. Rarely caught even in the wild. An impressive, long-lived species for public aquaria or dedicated specialists with the space to house it properly.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; large nest constructed from plant material.

description: A peaceful, subtly attractive gourami with an elongated spike-like central tail ray, found across India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, and the Malay Peninsula. Shy and best kept in a quiet, dimly lit, planted tank; prefers live or frozen micro-foods over dried preparations. Can be kept as a pair or small group with calm, small cyprinids. A cave or coconut shell is essential for spawning.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder in a cave or crevice; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A close relative of the spiketail paradisefish, endemic to ditches, swamps, and slow streams in Kerala, southern India. Slightly larger and prefers slightly warmer water than P. cupanus. Peaceful and shy; requires a heavily planted setup with gentle filtration and a cave for breeding. Both parents tend the bubble nest. Infrequently available in the hobby.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; both parents guard the nest and fry.

description: A tiny, exquisitely patterned anabantoid restricted to a small area of southwest Sri Lanka — one of the most geographically restricted labyrinth fish. Requires unusually cool temperatures for a tropical gourami. Only suitable for a dedicated species tank given its rarity and conservation value. Extremely shy; feeds exclusively on small live invertebrates. Captive breeding is a conservation priority.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder in a cave or crevice; pair spawns in a small clutch.

description: A wild betta from stagnant pools, ditches, and paddy fields of Thailand and the Malay Peninsula. Despite the species epithet ('peaceful'), males do fight — though less intensively than the common betta. Males display iridescent blue scaling and a distinctive red crescent on the tail fin. Best kept in a single-species tank or with very small, non-confrontational fish. Prefers live or frozen invertebrate foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest and guards the fry.

description: A bubble-nesting wild betta from sluggish ditches, rice paddies, and swamps of eastern Thailand and central Laos. Males display brilliant iridescent green scaling on the opercles and a barred caudal fin. Similar care to B. imbellis; males are territorial toward each other but generally manageable. Thrives in a heavily planted, low-flow tank. Prefers live or frozen small invertebrates alongside dried foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A distinctively colored wild betta endemic to Samut Sakhon Province, Thailand, inhabiting brackish nipa palm swamps where it uses water-filled leaf axils as breeding sites. Uniquely among bubble-nesting bettas, it tolerates and benefits from slightly hard, alkaline to slightly brackish water. Males display iridescent blue-green scaling. Best kept in a species tank with dense cover.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male constructs the nest in floating vegetation or plant structures.

description: A small wild betta from shallow marshes, grass fields, and paddy fields in eastern Thailand and Cambodia. Very closely related to B. smaragdina and B. imbellis; often confused with the latter in the hobby. Males display iridescent emerald-green coloration. Peaceful relative to most bettas; males can sometimes coexist with sufficient cover. Prefers live or frozen micro-foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny paternal mouthbrooding betta from shallow, leaf-littered forest streams in East Kalimantan. Named for the distinctive white margins on the fins of both sexes. One of the more peaceful and community-tolerant wild bettas; can be kept in small groups if the tank is well-planted. Requires acidic, soft water and small live or frozen invertebrate foods.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 10–21 days.

description: A small, striking paternal mouthbrooding betta from blackwater forest streams in the Mahakam River basin of East Kalimantan. Named for its somewhat snakehead-like head profile. Males display brilliant red-orange coloration. One of the easiest wild mouthbrooding bettas to keep; tolerates groups. Requires highly acidic, very soft water and small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia and Artemia.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for approximately 2 weeks.

description: One of the largest and most spectacular wild bettas, endemic to Brunei and Sarawak on Borneo and Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. Males display intense red coloration with iridescent red opercular patches. Requires cool, highly acidic, soft blackwater and a diet of high-quality live or frozen invertebrate foods. Males fight constantly — keep only as a bonded pair. A prestigious, demanding species for the specialist.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for up to 30 days.

description: One of the largest betta species, reaching over 12 cm, from forest streams in the Kayan and Howong drainages of East Kalimantan. A paternal mouthbrooder like other members of the unimaculata complex. More peaceful than bubble-nesting bettas toward other species but males spar with each other. Prefers cooler, clear water and a diet of meaty live or frozen foods. Needs a tightly covered tank as it is a strong jumper.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–4 weeks.

description: A large paternal mouthbrooding betta restricted to southern East Kalimantan, Borneo. Part of the unimaculata group of large mouthbrooders. Requires slightly acidic, soft water and a carnivore diet of live or frozen invertebrates and small fish. Males are territorial with conspecifics; best kept as a single pair or individually. A secure lid is essential.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–4 weeks.

description: A medium-sized paternal mouthbrooding betta from slow ravines and isolated pools surrounded by leaf litter in northeastern Borneo. Feeds primarily on terrestrial insects in the wild; accepts live or frozen invertebrates in captivity. A gifted jumper — cover the tank securely. Males compete but overall less aggressive than bubble-nesting bettas toward other species.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for 2–3 weeks.

description: A widespread paternal mouthbrooding betta from forest streams and hill rivers of the Malay Peninsula, Penang, and Sumatra. Inhabits areas with overhanging vegetation, submerged roots, and leaf litter rather than dense aquatic vegetation. Less aggressive than most bettas; pairs can be kept together without constant fighting. Feeds on insects and invertebrates. Requires a tightly fitted lid.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–4 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta endemic to acidic peat swamps of Johor, Malaysia, among the most extreme blackwater specialists of its genus. Requires exceptionally acidic, very soft water with negligible hardness. Males display a vivid blue-green iridescence. Feeds on small live invertebrates; reluctant to accept frozen or dried foods. For the dedicated blackwater specialist only.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from streams and standing waters of Java, Indonesia. One of the less aggressive wild bettas; pairs and small groups can coexist with adequate cover. Males display spotted patterning that gives the species its name. Requires soft, acidic to neutral water and small live or frozen invertebrates for best condition. A tightly fitted lid is essential.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from fast-flowing hill streams of Borneo, found amid submerged leaf litter, tree roots, and overhanging bank vegetation. Subtly attractive with fine horizontal banding. Pairs and small groups can be maintained together if sufficient cover is provided. Prefers small live or frozen invertebrates. Requires a tight-fitting lid and leaf-litter décor for natural behavior.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A diminutive, deep wine-red betta from blackwater peat swamps of Sumatra and Malaysia. Males display vivid crimson bodies with iridescent green fin margins. Bubble-nesting species that requires very soft, acidic blackwater with minimal hardness. Aggressive toward conspecifics — best kept as a solitary individual or as a single pair with dense cover. Prone to obesity; feed sparingly with live or frozen micro-foods.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: One of the smallest members of the coccina complex, from highly acidic peat swamp forests in Sarawak and adjacent Malaysia. Brown base coloration with a striking iridescent blue-green spot on the flank. Demands extreme blackwater conditions with very low hardness and negligible dissolved minerals. Bubble-nesting species; best kept as a solitary individual or single pair. Feed tiny live or frozen invertebrates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny, Endangered bubble-nesting betta endemic to a very small area of peat swamp forest in Selangor, Malaysia. Among the most specialized blackwater bettas — requires extremely acidic water with near-zero hardness in a well-shaded, densely planted tank. Closely related to B. coccina and B. brownorum. Captive breeding populations are vital for conservation. Not a fish for the average aquarist.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny coccina-group bubble-nesting betta from extremely acidic peat swamps south of Pekan, Pahang, Malaysia. Among the more temperature-sensitive wild bettas, preferring cooler conditions than most tropical species. Demands near-zero hardness blackwater with very low pH. Males display reddish coloration typical of the coccina group. Best kept alone or as a single pair in a dedicated blackwater setup.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A diminutive coccina-group betta from blackwater peat swamp forests with very little sunlight in West Kalimantan. Males display vivid red finnage against a darker body. Requires extreme blackwater conditions with negligible hardness and highly acidic pH. Keeps well only in a dedicated species tank or blackwater setup. Feeds on tiny live or frozen invertebrates such as Daphnia and Artemia nauplii.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A tiny Endangered betta from the blackwater peat swamps of Johor, Malaysia, threatened by oil palm plantation expansion. Males are largely blue-black with a striking dark, jewel-like appearance. Like all coccina-group bettas, demands highly acidic, extremely soft blackwater with minimal flow. A bubble-nesting species. Captive breeding programs are helping preserve this rare and exquisite fish.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A Critically Endangered coccina-group betta described in 2013, from shallow peat swamps of the Sebangau drainage in Central Kalimantan. Inhabits water just 5–50 cm deep among dense aquatic vegetation. Requires highly acidic blackwater with near-zero hardness. Only available through specialist breeders. Like all coccina-group bettas, best kept in a single-species blackwater setup.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: One of the smallest coccina-group bettas, endemic to peat swamp and secondary forest habitats on Bangka Island, Indonesia. Named for its wine-burgundy coloration. Requires extremely acidic, soft blackwater with virtually no dissolved minerals. Rarely available except through specialist breeders. Best kept in a tiny, dedicated blackwater species tank with dim lighting and dense floating vegetation.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: A Vulnerable paternal mouthbrooding betta from swamp forests and forest streams in West Kalimantan, Borneo. One of the smaller mouthbrooding bettas. Requires soft, acidic water and live or frozen invertebrate foods. Can be kept as a pair or small group with adequate shelter and a heavily planted blackwater setup. Rarely seen in the hobby outside specialist circles.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–3 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from forest swamp streams with dense vegetation in Central and West Kalimantan, Indonesian Borneo. Males display orange-red opercular bars and extended finnage. Requires soft, acidic blackwater conditions and a diet of live or frozen invertebrates. Best kept as a single pair or alone; males can be aggressive with each other. A firm favourite among Betta enthusiasts.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from the peat swamp forests of South Kalimantan, Borneo, closely related to B. foerschi. Males display attractive reddish-brown coloration with elongated finnage. Requires acidic, soft blackwater and a carnivore diet of live or frozen invertebrates. Best kept as a single pair in a dedicated blackwater species tank. Infrequently available in the hobby.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates for approximately 2 weeks.

description: An Endangered paternal mouthbrooding betta endemic to peat swamps of northern Sumatra near Lake Toba. Despite its common name, not closely related to B. splendens — males do not build bubble nests. Requires highly acidic, soft blackwater typical of Sumatran peat swamps. Males display vivid coloration. A sought-after species among wild Betta enthusiasts; only available through specialist breeders.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from limestone hill streams and sluggish marshes of Krabi Province, southern Thailand. Unusual among wild bettas for its preference for neutral to slightly alkaline water — reflecting its limestone karst habitat. Less aggressive than most bettas and can be kept as a pair or small group. Feeds on small aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for 2–3 weeks.

description: A paternal mouthbrooding betta from swamps in South Kalimantan, Indonesian Borneo. One of the larger mouthbrooding bettas; requires a longer tank footprint than smaller species. Subdued but attractive coloration. Feeds on small live or frozen invertebrates; reluctant with dried food alone. Best kept alone or as a single pair with plenty of cover. A tightly fitted lid is essential.
reproduction: Paternal mouthbrooder; male incubates the brood for approximately 2 weeks.

description: A large, slender bubble-nesting betta from peat swamp streams of Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula, primarily hunting insects — especially damselfly larvae — at the water surface. One of the less commonly kept large bettas; requires a spacious, covered tank with acidic, soft water. Aggressive with conspecifics; best kept individually or as a single pair. Accepts dried food but does best on live or frozen invertebrates.
reproduction: Bubble nest builder; male guards the nest and fry.

description: One of the most gentle cichlids available, from small, clear forest creeks of the Orinoco delta and coastal rivers of Guyana. Named for the keyhole-shaped marking on its flank. Shy and easily stressed; does best in a planted tank with leaf litter, driftwood, and small, non-aggressive tankmates. Susceptible to poor water quality — requires mature, well-filtered water. Excellent choice for soft-water community tanks.
reproduction: Open spawner on flat surfaces; both parents guard eggs and fry.

description: A colorful, hardy Central American cichlid from muddy lake and swamp habitats with dense vegetation. Males develop dazzling yellow and orange breeding colors. Tolerates an unusually wide temperature and pH range. Territorial with other males but manageable in a spacious setup. Both parents defend fry actively. A relatively easy cichlid well suited to community tanks with similarly sized fish.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents actively defend eggs and fry.

description: A Vulnerable cichlid endemic to Pacific-slope streams and lakes of Costa Rica. Named for the T-shaped bar pattern on the flank. Both sexes are attractively colored. Males can be highly aggressive toward females not in spawning condition — provide ample space and separate if necessary. Strong biparental care instincts; excellent parents. Omnivorous and undemanding in feeding.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry aggressively.

description: A moderately sized Central American cichlid from Atlantic-slope rivers of Honduras and Guatemala. Males are larger and display more vivid coloration than females. Territorial during spawning but manageable in a spacious tank with robust tankmates. Biparental care; both parents defend eggs and fry. An undemanding feeder that accepts most prepared foods alongside live and frozen items.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry.

description: A compact, colorful cichlid from Atlantic-slope streams and lakes of Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Honduras. Females display a distinctive bright orange abdominal spot; males bear bold black stripes. Forms strong monogamous pair bonds. Territorial during breeding; males can injure females outside spawning condition — ensure adequate space and escape routes. Excellent biparental care.
reproduction: Open spawner; monogamous pair provides biparental care.

description: A handsome cichlid from Atlantic-slope rivers and lakes from Belize to Nicaragua, named for its striking blue eyes. Blue body with black vertical stripes; females develop vivid orange bellies during spawning. Tolerates a wide temperature range. Territorial during breeding but moderately manageable for a Central American cichlid. Both parents provide care for eggs and fry.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A large, spectacular cichlid from Lake Nicaragua and the San Juan River drainage, displaying stunning mosaic coloration with orange and green flanks. Relatively peaceful for its size among Central American cichlids. Prefers hard, alkaline water; will not thrive in acidic conditions. Digs enthusiastically — avoid delicate plants. Omnivorous; accepts pellets, frozen foods, and vegetables.
reproduction: Open spawner or cave spawner; strong biparental care.

description: A Lake Nicaragua cichlid named for its resemblance to the African Tropheus in behavior and diet. Grazes on aufwuchs — algae and organic material covering rocks and submerged wood. Colorful and active; prefers rocky décor with algae growth. Diet must include significant vegetable matter or spirulina-based foods. Territorial with its own kind; provide ample space and structure.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry.

description: A large, iridescent cichlid from Atlantic-slope rivers of northeastern Mexico, covered in brilliant pearl-green spotting. Often confused with the Texas cichlid (H. cyanoguttatus). Highly territorial, especially during breeding — treats smaller fish as prey. Digs enthusiastically. Despite its aggression, an undemanding feeder that accepts pellets, frozen food, and vegetables. Best with similarly aggressive, large tankmates.
reproduction: Open spawner; strong biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: An Endangered cichlid endemic to the springs and karst systems of the upper Río Verde and La Media Luna in San Luis Potosí, Mexico. Adults display distinctive black-and-white to grey polymorphic coloration that deepens during breeding. Prefers hard, alkaline spring water. Feeds on fish, aquatic insects, algae, and invertebrates. Population has declined dramatically since the 1950s; captive stocks assist conservation.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A large cichlid from the warm karstic spring of La Media Luna and the Panuco River system in Mexico. Named for its distinctive thick lips adapted for scraping aufwuchs and extracting molluscs. Yellow color morph most common in the hobby. Requires hard, alkaline water and a large tank. Moderately territorial; manageable with large, robust tankmates. An impressive species for the large cichlid enthusiast.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A close relative of the common firemouth cichlid from the Papaloapán River basin in Mexico. Displays the classic fire-throat threat display when intimidated, spreading its gill covers to reveal brilliant red coloration. Moderately territorial; typically more peaceful than larger Central American cichlids. Omnivorous and undemanding. Provides attentive biparental care; pairs defend eggs and fry vigorously.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A firemouth relative from lakes and warm lagoons of Belize and the Motagua River basin in Honduras. Distinguished from the common firemouth by more intense blue iridescence and a slightly different flank pattern. Performs the same impressive gill-flare threat display. Prefers warm, hard, alkaline water typical of Central American lowland lakes. Relatively peaceful except during spawning; forms strong pair bonds.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A firemouth cichlid from the Usumacinta River drainage of Mexico and Guatemala. Distinguishable from the common firemouth by a dark gullet coloration visible during the gill-flare display. Prefers harder, more alkaline water than most firemouth relatives. Territorial during breeding. Both parents provide strong biparental care. Omnivorous and accepts most prepared and frozen cichlid foods.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A massive cichlid from the Usumacinta lowlands, male developing a spectacular bright-red head and nuchal hump with age. Primarily herbivorous/detritivorous; diet should include plant matter, algae wafers, and cichlid pellets supplemented with protein. Highly aggressive in captivity — only for very large setups with similarly sized robust tankmates. Strong parental care instincts.
reproduction: Open spawner; aggressive biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A large, elegant cichlid from the Usumacinta River drainage of Mexico, named for its silvery-white base coloration. Related to the redhead cichlid but smaller and less frequently encountered in the hobby. Moderately aggressive; manageable with large, similarly sized tankmates in a spacious tank. Omnivorous and accepts most prepared cichlid foods. Provides strong parental care during breeding.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A large herbivorous cichlid from Atlantic-slope rivers and lake valleys of southeastern Mexico and northern Guatemala, including brackish-tolerant coastal habitats. Feeds predominantly on aquatic and terrestrial plant matter. Requires a diet rich in vegetable matter — algae wafers, blanched vegetables, and spirulina-based pellets. Moderately aggressive; best with large robust tankmates in a spacious, plant-tolerant setup.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A very large, aggressive cichlid from slow-flowing lower river valleys of the Pacific slope of Central America. Rarely available in the hobby; possibly contributed to hybrid flowerhorn development. Feeds primarily on small fish and invertebrates. Only suitable for massive, dedicated tanks with carefully selected large tankmates. Both parents guard eggs fiercely. Requires significant space and robust filtration.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry aggressively.

description: A massive ambush predator from slow-moving Central American rivers and lakes with one of the most extensible jaws of any cichlid — capable of extending to over a quarter of its body length to engulf prey. Coloration ranges from pink and red to gold. A pure piscivore that requires a very large tank and generates enormous bioload. Only for dedicated large-predator specialists with the facilities to match.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry.

description: A large, highly predatory cichlid from Atlantic-slope rivers of Central America, closely related to the guapote. Distinguished by a spotted yellow pattern — the 'yellow jacket.' Feeds primarily on live fish; a major piscivore. Aggressive and territorial; only suitable for very large tanks with no smaller tankmates. Both parents produce large clutches of 500–700 eggs and guard them intensively.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental guarding of large egg clutches.

description: A large, predatory guapote-type cichlid from the Motagua River drainage of Guatemala and Honduras, with some populations on the Pacific slope. Sometimes called the false yellow jacket for its resemblance to P. friedrichsthalii. Hunts fish, aquatic insects, and terrestrial insects in moderate to fast-flowing river sections. Highly aggressive; requires a very large dedicated tank.
reproduction: Open spawner; strong biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A large herbivorous cichlid from the Coatzacoalcos River basin in Mexico, grazing on aufwuchs, vegetable bottom debris, and algae. Not a commonly available species outside specialist cichlid circles. Requires a diet dominated by plant matter and a relatively narrow temperature range. Moderately territorial during spawning; manageable with robust tankmates in a spacious tank with rocky décor.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A large cichlid endemic to Hispaniola, feeding primarily on aquatic plants, algae, and turret snails. One of only two cichlid species native to Hispaniola. Prefers hard, alkaline water reflecting its Caribbean island habitat. Aggressive toward conspecifics; needs a spacious tank with robust, similarly-sized tankmates. Not frequently kept outside cichlid enthusiast circles. Provide algae and plant matter in the diet.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry.

description: The only member of its genus, this striking green and blue cichlid is a demanding blackwater specialist from the upper Rio Negro and Orinoco. The name comes from its high-domed forehead and rounded profile. Requires very soft, acidic, warm water with near-zero hardness — tolerates almost no deviation. Despite its size, relatively peaceful with similarly-sized species. Feed a varied diet of meaty foods.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: A large, disc-shaped cichlid from the Amazon basin, named for the prominent dark triangular blotch on its flank that becomes more intense during spawning. Surprisingly peaceful for its size — can be kept with smaller fish including tetras. Primarily herbivorous; will consume aquarium plants so provide vegetable matter in the diet. Best kept as a pair or small group with plenty of open swimming space.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard eggs and fry on flat surfaces.

description: A rare blackwater uaru from the Atabapo River in Venezuela, classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN due to gold mining threatening its habitat. Distinguished from the common uaru by a panda-like black-and-white pattern. Requires very warm temperatures and extremely soft, acidic blackwater. Peaceful and social; best kept in small groups. Rarely available in the hobby and commands high prices when offered.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental guarding of eggs and fry.

origin: Upper Orinoco and upper Negro River basins
description: The true spotted severum, only formally described in 2015 and rarely distinguished in the trade from the more common Heros efasciatus. Identifiable by a distinctive partial seventh body band. An omnivore consuming plant material and benthic invertebrates in the wild; prefers soft, slightly acidic water from its blackwater origins. Semi-aggressive during spawning but otherwise a manageable cichlid for an intermediate keeper.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

description: The largest cichlid in the Americas and one of the largest in the world, reaching nearly 1 metre in the wild. A powerful ambush predator that eats any fish it can swallow whole. Requires a public-aquarium-scale tank — a minimum 10-foot footprint for a single adult. Only realistically kept by professional facilities or extremely dedicated specialists with the infrastructure to match. Not a home aquarium fish.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental guarding of eggs and fry on flat surfaces.

origin: Upper Fly River, Papua New Guinea
description: A small, active rainbowfish endemic to the upper Fly River in Papua New Guinea, listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to its extremely restricted range. Males display striking horizontal striping along the flanks. Peaceful schooling species well suited to planted tanks with neutral to slightly alkaline water. Captive-bred specimens are the conservation-responsible choice; wild collection should be avoided.
reproduction: Egg scatterer; spawns among fine-leaved plants.

origin: Amazon River basin, Brazil and Guyana
description: A medium-large ambush predator from the Amazon and its tributaries including the Madeira and Branco drainages. Lurks among marginal vegetation waiting to engulf prey. Similar in biology to the better-known C. myersi but with a more southerly range in the Amazon basin proper. Not community-compatible with smaller fish; will consume anything that fits in its large mouth. Both parents guard eggs and fry aggressively.
reproduction: Open spawner; strong biparental care of large egg clutches.

origin: Putumayo and Napo River basins, upper Amazon
description: A large-mouthed ambush predator from the Putumayo and Napo rivers of the upper Amazon, sharing the family's characteristic ability to rapidly expand its mouth to engulf prey. Accepts a wide temperature range in captivity. Highly piscivorous; not safe with fish smaller than itself. Both parents deposit and guard over 1,000 eggs on open substrates. Rarely seen in the trade outside specialist cichlid circles.
reproduction: Open spawner; both parents guard large clutches of eggs and fry.

origin: Negro, Tapajós and upper Orinoco basins
description: A blackwater chocolate cichlid from the Negro, Tapajós, and upper Orinoco rivers, related to Hypselecara temporalis but preferring more acidic conditions. Develops a rich purple-violet sheen over the chocolate base coloration. Requires soft, acidic blackwater in captivity. More peaceful than its size suggests; compatible with similarly-sized robust tankmates. Rarely encountered in the trade and commands premium prices.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental care of eggs and fry.

origin: Orinoco basin and upper Negro, Colombia, Venezuela, Brazil
description: A flag cichlid relative from the Orinoco and upper Negro drainages, closely related to but distinct from the more common Mesonauta festivus. Tolerates a very wide pH range and is generally peaceful, making it one of the more beginner-friendly medium cichlids. The characteristic diagonal dark stripe from mouth to dorsal fin is present in all Mesonauta species. Pairs form stable bonds and share parental duties.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental guarding of eggs and fry.

description: A graceful substrate-sifting eartheater from the Amazon basin, best kept in groups of 5–8 where it establishes stable dominance hierarchies and poses little threat to fish larger than a few millimetres. Named for its noticeably high forehead. Requires a deep sandy substrate and pristine water quality; extremely sensitive to dissolved organics. Ovophilic mouthbrooder — females incubate eggs in the mouth.
reproduction: Ovophilic mouthbrooder; female incubates eggs and broods fry in the mouth.

description: A mid-sized eartheater restricted to the Tocantins drainage of central Brazil. Like all Geophagus eartheaters, it sifts fine substrate through its gill rakers to extract invertebrates and detritus. Group-living and generally peaceful outside spawning; lone specimens become stressed and may be bullied. Requires excellent water quality with a sand substrate and regular large water changes.
reproduction: Ovophilic mouthbrooder; female holds eggs and fry in the buccal cavity.

origin: Lower Casiquiare and upper Negro, southern Venezuela
description: A blackwater eartheater from the Casiquiare canal region and the upper Negro in Venezuela, inhabiting some of the most ion-poor waters on earth. Tolerates a wide pH range but thrives in soft, slightly acidic conditions. Group-living and peaceful; requires groups of at least 5 to form stable hierarchies and reduce intra-group aggression. Extremely sensitive to dissolved organics — pristine water quality is essential.
reproduction: Ovophilic mouthbrooder; female incubates eggs and fry orally.

description: A large Geophagus eartheater from Venezuela, largely unstudied in the hobby. Shares the characteristic substrate-sifting feeding behavior of the genus, sifting mouthfuls of sand to filter out invertebrates. Requires groups of 5–8 individuals, fine sand substrate, and exceptional water quality with regular large water changes. Rarely available outside specialist importers.
reproduction: Ovophilic mouthbrooder; female incubates eggs and fry in the buccal cavity.

origin: Coastal drainages of eastern and southern Brazil and Uruguay
description: One of the most cold-tolerant cichlids, native to coastal Brazilian and Uruguayan drainages where water temperatures can dip to near 18°C. Males can reach 28cm and become territorial when breeding; females and juveniles are more subdued. Substrate sifter but also consumes plant matter, invertebrates, and fish fry. More forgiving of harder water and wider pH than most South American cichlids.
reproduction: Open spawner; biparental guarding of eggs and fry on substrate.

origin: Amazon River basin, Peru to eastern Brazil and French Guiana
description: One of the most widely distributed and commonly kept Satanoperca eartheaters, found throughout much of the Amazon basin. Peaceful and gregarious; must be kept in groups of at least 5 to prevent aggression toward the weakest individuals. A dedicated substrate sifter that requires fine sand and pristine water quality. Tolerates a wide pH range but is extremely sensitive to dissolved organics — large weekly water changes are non-negotiable.
reproduction: Delayed mouthbrooder; eggs spawned on substrate then picked up for incubation.

origin: Essequibo River, Guyana and Nickerie River, Suriname
description: A smaller Satanoperca eartheater from Guyana and Suriname, covered in pale iridescent spots that give rise to its common name. Like all Satanoperca, it sifts mouthfuls of substrate for invertebrates and organic matter. Peaceful and social; requires groups to thrive and fine sandy substrate for normal feeding behaviour. Susceptible to deteriorating water quality — powerful filtration and weekly large water changes are essential.
reproduction: Delayed mouthbrooder; eggs are picked up and incubated in the mouth.

origin: Upper Negro and Orinoco basins, Colombia, Venezuela, Brazil
description: A large, demanding blackwater eartheater from the upper Negro and Orinoco, distinguished by three spots arranged diagonally along the flank. Requires very soft, highly acidic water — pH above 6.0 is stressful long-term. One of the more challenging Satanoperca species, as it cannot tolerate any compromise in water chemistry. Groups of 5–8 establish stable hierarchies; solo specimens suffer and decline.
reproduction: Delayed mouthbrooder; eggs laid on substrate then incubated buccally.

origin: Solimões-Amazon and Negro River basins, Brazil
description: A large, beautiful eartheater from the central Amazon and Negro basins, tolerating a wider pH range than some of its genus-mates. Distinguished from the demon eartheater by subtle scaling and colour differences. Peaceful and gregarious; best maintained in groups with a sand substrate and excellent water quality. Like all Satanoperca, extremely vulnerable to deteriorating water conditions — nitrate must be kept very low.
reproduction: Delayed mouthbrooder; eggs transferred to mouth shortly after spawning.

origin: Paraguay, Paraná and lower Uruguay drainages
description: A cool-water South American eartheater from the subtropical Paraná-Paraguay system. Unlike most cichlids, it thrives at lower temperatures and requires a cooler dormancy period of several months (16–18°C) each year to remain healthy long-term. Males develop a pronounced nuchal hump with age and become aggressive toward other males. Best kept as a harem of one male with multiple females. Substrate sifter; requires sand and vegetable matter in the diet.
reproduction: Delayed mouthbrooder; female incubates eggs and fry in the buccal cavity.

description: A subtropical eartheater from the Uruguay River and Laguna dos Patos drainages, streaked with iridescent blue along the flanks. Hardy and easy to feed, but it is a cool-water fish: it tolerates unheated tanks and does best with a cooler winter period rather than constant tropical temperatures. More boisterous than most eartheaters — keep one male with several females over fine sand it can sift.
behavior: Sifts mouthfuls of sand for food morsels all day. Groups form a dominance hierarchy; males get scrappy with each other during spawning.
reproduction: Easily bred in captivity; a seasonal drop in temperature helps trigger spawning.

description: A thick-lipped, mouthbrooding eartheater from the Laguna dos Patos and Lagoa Mirim drainages, with red and blue tones in mature males. Peaceful and plant-tolerant for a cichlid, but a true subtropical species: an annual cool period around 16–18 °C is considered essential for its long-term health. Needs a deep bed of soft sand to sift and clean, well-filtered water.
behavior: A constant sand-sifter that is mild-mannered toward tankmates; sensitive to deteriorating water quality.
reproduction: Mouthbrooder; spawns at 23–25 °C, and an annual three-month winter cool-down keeps breeding condition.
dimorphism: Males grow noticeably larger than females (about 17 cm vs 13 cm) and develop more intense head coloration.

description: A small, surprisingly peaceful eartheater relative from the Amazon basin, marked with an eye-spot below a dark head bar. Loosely gregarious — keep a group of five or more over fine sand so its constant sifting and dominance displays stay sociable rather than spiteful. Sensitive to old water; pair it with calm tankmates that enjoy soft, warm conditions.
behavior: Sifts sand for food and forms a clear pecking order within the group; aggression only sharpens around breeding pairs.
dimorphism: Males tend to be larger and deeper-bodied with longer dorsal and caudal fin extensions.

description: The blackwater counterpart to the cupid cichlid, collected from the acidic, tannin-stained waters of the Rio Negro and Orinoco. Peaceful and group-living, but demanding: it wants very soft, acidic, scrupulously clean water and a fine sand bed to sift. Best reserved for a dedicated blackwater community with other gentle, soft-water species.
behavior: Loosely gregarious sand-sifter that forms a dominance hierarchy; wilts quickly in hard or poorly maintained water.

description: A striking Amazonian cichlid whose dorsal fin rays trail into long filaments, over flanks dusted with iridescent scales. Mostly placid for its size but prone to sudden bursts of aggression toward its own kind, and it remodels the substrate constantly — anchor plants well or skip them. Give it sand, open floor space and strong filtration with big weekly water changes.
behavior: An incessant digger that rearranges the tank floor; aggression eases in larger tanks and groups.
reproduction: A hole nester — pairs excavate tunnels and chambers beneath the substrate for spawning.
dimorphism: Mature females are noticeably thicker-bodied; males carry the longer trailing fin filaments.

description: A stocky bandit-style cichlid from the river systems of the Guianas, wearing a dark vertical band through the eye and a blue sheen across the flanks. Generally peaceful for a cichlid but firmly territorial when breeding, and a determined digger — furnish with rocks, roots and line-of-sight breaks rather than delicate plants.
behavior: Digs constantly and claims a cave or rock pile as its territory; squabbles are mostly display rather than damage.
reproduction: A substrate and cave spawner that breeds several times a year; pairs guard their fry for weeks after hatching.

description: A Suriname bandit cichlid with a wedge-shaped flank bar; the sought-after 'red spot' form adds crimson scale flecks. Intraspecific bickering is loud but rarely harmful, and it leaves dissimilar tankmates alone — a good cichlid for a roomy community of upper-water species. Wants sand to dig and rockwork to claim.
reproduction: A cave spawner: after cleaning a site, the pair attaches eggs to a vertical surface and defends the cave.
dimorphism: Females stay around two-thirds of male size; old males develop a large, blocky head.

description: The 'true green terror' of Peru's Pacific-slope rivers, distinguished from the familiar gold saum by silvery-white fin edging. A hard-water specialist that appreciates alkaline conditions unusual among South American cichlids. Highly territorial — keep it singly or as a proven pair with robust, similar-sized tankmates and excellent filtration.
behavior: Territorial and increasingly pugnacious with age; sifts mouthfuls of fine substrate while feeding.
dimorphism: Males grow larger and develop more extended fins and a nuchal hump with age.

description: A hardy acara from Panama and southern Costa Rica, freckled with pale blue spangles over an earthy body. Young fish are relatively docile, but adults grow territorial and will swallow anything bite-sized, so choose medium tankmates like larger tetras and armored catfish. Easy to feed and forgiving of varied water — a solid first medium cichlid.
behavior: Mellow when young, more territorial with age; best kept singly or as a bonded pair unless the tank is very large.

description: One of the largest acaras, named for the dark saddle blotch on its flank and spread across nearly the whole Amazon and Orinoco. Peaceful as a juvenile but transforms into a territorial, predatory adult that will eat small fish outright. Plan for a big, well-filtered tank with open floor, sturdy hardscape and tankmates too large to swallow.
behavior: Burrows and rearranges the substrate; aggression climbs sharply as it matures, so choose tankmates for its adult self.

description: A calm, regal-looking acara from clear- and blackwater streams of the upper Negro and Orinoco. Assertive enough to hold its ground but far from a thug — it fits mature communities of larger tetras, rainbowfish and moderate cichlids. Males don't tolerate each other, so keep one, or a bonded pair, among caves and driftwood.
behavior: Territorial mainly when breeding; otherwise an easy-going mid-size cichlid that appreciates cover and open swimming room.

description: A peaceful golden-hued acara from Colombia's Meta River, with a dark lateral stripe and warm yellow fins. One of the gentler mid-size cichlids — only breeding pairs get pushy — though it will still swallow truly tiny tankmates. Pairs nicely with firemouths, severums, bigger tetras and peaceful barbs in a sheltered, planted layout.
behavior: Mild for a cichlid; claims a small territory around its spawning site but otherwise cohabits well with similar-sized fish.

description: A dwarf 'smiling acara' whose cheeks and belly flush deep crimson in breeding dress. Genuinely peaceful — happy alongside tetras, pencilfish, hatchetfish and corys — and at home in cooler, soft, well-planted tanks. Can be a picky eater at first; tempt it with small live and frozen foods before weaning onto quality dried.
behavior: Quiet and a little shy outside of spawning, when pairs guard a small territory; sensitive to declining water quality.
reproduction: Pairs spawn on a cleaned flat surface and tend the eggs and fry together.

description: An old-school aquarium classic from the cool coastal lagoons of southern Brazil. Very peaceful outside of breeding, undemanding about food, and comfortable at room temperature — one of the few cichlids that suits an unheated indoor tank. Give it sand to dig in and a quiet community of similar easy-going fish.
behavior: Digs gently through the substrate and mostly keeps to itself; pairs guard their spawn but rarely cause real trouble.

description: The 'little pig' of subtropical South America — a barred, mood-ring cichlid whose colors shift in seconds. Famously cold-tolerant, it thrives in unheated indoor tanks, but it earns its Central-American-style reputation: it digs, uproots plants and turns fiercely territorial when breeding. Keep with robust fish of similar size and attitude.
behavior: An active digger that rearranges its tank and defends a large breeding territory; color patterns flicker with mood.

description: A subtly handsome acara from the Paraná-Paraguay system with one of South America's most charming breeding strategies: both parents take turns mouthbrooding. Mild-mannered, easy to feed and undemanding about water, it suits a calm community of similar-sized fish. Provide sand, leaf litter and a few flat surfaces for spawning.
behavior: Calm and unobtrusive outside of breeding, when pairs guard a small territory around their chosen spawning leaf.
reproduction: A biparental mouthbrooder: eggs are laid on a loose leaf the pair can drag to safety, then brooded in both parents' mouths.

description: The giant of the peacock basses and one of the largest cichlids on Earth, approaching a metre in the wild. A blistering open-water predator that inhales anything mouth-sized; the speckled 'paca' pattern of resting fish transforms into bold bars in breeding adults. Realistically a fish for public aquaria, ponds or enormous custom tanks only.
behavior: A pursuit hunter that needs long, unobstructed swimming lanes; tankmates must be far too large to swallow.

description: A butterfly-type peacock bass marked with three big gold-ringed ocelli along each flank. Like all Cichla it is a fast-growing, open-water predator that demands huge, well-filtered tanks and swallows anything that fits in its cavernous mouth. Juveniles sold at a few centimetres become 60 cm+ fish within a couple of years.
behavior: Cruises shallow lagoon margins in the wild; in captivity it needs open length to swim and robust, oversized tankmates.

description: A strikingly cool-toned peacock bass from the Tocantins-Araguaia, washed in steel blue with five dark vertical bars. Somewhat more tank-manageable than its giant cousins but still a true monster fish: a fast, powerful predator needing a very large footprint, heavy filtration and tankmates too big to eat.
behavior: An active open-water hunter; grows fast and strikes hard at feeding time, so secure the lid and keep hands clear.

description: The most tank-manageable peacock bass, prized for the golden body and light-spangled fins of mature fish. Usually 30–45 cm in aquaria (wild fish grow larger), it is still a serious predator that needs a long six-foot-plus tank, pristine water and meaty foods. The usual entry point into Cichla keeping — plan for its adult size from day one.
behavior: Less frantic than larger Cichla but still an ambush-and-pursuit hunter; anything bite-sized is food.

description: An elongate, ambush-hunting pike cichlid from coastal drainages of the Guianas, named for the dark eyespot ringed in pale gold at the base of its tail. A serious predator that will eat any tankmate it can swallow and is often scrappy toward its own kind. Best kept singly or as a proven pair with robust, oversized companions, in a long tank with caves and driftwood.
behavior: Lurks among cover and ambushes prey; territorial and intolerant of most tankmates, especially conspecifics.

description: The gentlest of the dwarf pikes (now placed in the genus Wallaciia), a slim, characterful predator only a few centimetres long with rows of bright spots along its sides. Far more community-friendly than its larger relatives — it can even be kept in small groups given enough cover — though it will still snap up tiny fish and shrimp. A great introduction to pike cichlids.
behavior: Spends its time among caves and leaf litter; mild for a pike but territorial toward its own kind without ample hiding places.
dimorphism: Females flush red in the belly when in breeding condition; males grow a little larger.

description: A tiny, flat-headed dwarf pike from the Tocantins (now genus Wallaciia), one of the smallest Crenicichla-type cichlids in the hobby. Despite its size it can be surprisingly feisty and wants more space than its length suggests. A predator at heart — pair it with mid-water fish too big to eat, over a tank full of caves and tangled wood.
behavior: Cave-dwelling ambush feeder; can be aggressive toward similar bottom-dwellers and its own kind.
dimorphism: Males carry striped yellow markings in the fins.

description: A blackwater dwarf pike from the Rio Negro (now genus Wallaciia), marked with a row of dark blotches and a tail-base eyespot. Wants soft, acidic, tannin-stained water and the same cave-rich layout as the other dwarf pikes. A small but determined predator best kept with peaceful, soft-water tankmates that are too large to be eaten.
behavior: Secretive among roots and leaf litter; ambushes small prey and defends a chosen cave.

description: A large, vividly marked pike cichlid (now genus Lugubria) from the Rio Negro and Guiana drainages, with orange-red flushing and bold spotting in mature fish. A powerful open-water and ambush predator topping 30 cm; it eats fish wholesale and brooks little company. A specialist's fish for a big, well-filtered blackwater tank with sturdy, similar-sized tankmates.
behavior: Patrols and ambushes from cover; highly predatory and territorial, especially with other pikes.

description: A slender dwarf cichlid whose males flash electric-blue flanks and long, sail-like dorsal and ventral fins. Males are firmly territorial with each other, so keep a single pair or one male with several females over a heavily structured, planted tank. Wild fish prefer it kept simple, while tank-bred stock adapts well to gentle community life.
behavior: Females claim small territories and brood in caves; males display constantly and chase rival males.
reproduction: Cave spawner; the female tends eggs and fry while the male guards the wider territory.
dimorphism: Males are markedly larger and more colorful with extended finnage; females are smaller and yellower.

description: A popular 'blue panda' apisto with neon sky-blue males set off by a black tail blotch. Hardier and easier than many dwarfs, but it wants soft, acidic water to color up and breed. Keep as a pair or harem with quiet dither fish; males get pushy with each other and with the female around spawning time.
behavior: Forms monogamous pairs; the female becomes intensely protective of fry, sometimes turning on the male.
dimorphism: Males are larger and far bluer with an extended lower jaw; females turn bright yellow when guarding eggs.

description: The original 'panda' dwarf cichlid: blue-grey males with bold black eye, gill and tail spots, and warm orange-edged females. A blackwater specialist that needs soft, acidic water to thrive. Best as a harem of one male to three or four females, each given her own cave and visual barriers, in a densely planted tank.
behavior: Females stake out individual territories around caves; the male patrols between them and turns territorial when breeding.
reproduction: Cave spawner; females turn vivid yellow with black markings while guarding eggs and fry.
dimorphism: Males are larger and blue-grey; brooding females flush bright yellow-orange.

description: A red-cheeked Orinoco dwarf cichlid whose males develop rosy flanks and trailing fin tips, especially in line-bred 'super red' forms. Wants soft, warm, acidic water and the usual apisto layout of caves, wood and dense planting. Keep a single pair or a harem with peaceful dithers; rival males will not coexist in small tanks.
behavior: A cave-spawning harem breeder; males display intense red coloration when courting and competing.
dimorphism: Males larger, redder and longer-finned; females smaller and yellow when in breeding dress.

description: A Colombian dwarf cichlid prized for males marked with red dorsal edging and blue-green cheeks; much aquarium 'viejita' is actually the closely related macmasteri. Needs soft, acidic water and a structured, planted tank with caves. Keep as a pair or modest harem with calm dither fish, giving each female her own retreat.
behavior: Harem-forming cave spawner; the male defends the colony while females guard individual broods.
dimorphism: Males larger with red-trimmed fins and stronger color; females smaller and plainer outside breeding.

description: The 'Inka' dwarf cichlid from Peruvian blackwater, with bold black bars over a yellow body and red-and-blue fin edging. A nijsseni-complex fish that forms either pairs or harems, but it is feistier than most apistos, so give each female a generous cave-studded territory. Needs soft, acidic water to look and breed its best.
behavior: More combative than the average apisto; females hold individual territories while the male oversees the group.
dimorphism: Males are larger and longer-finned; females turn vivid black-and-yellow when guarding fry.

description: A blackwater dwarf cichlid named for the two dark lines along its body, with males growing dramatic lyre tails and color that varies between collection forms. A soft-water specialist that demands acidic, tannin-rich conditions and is unforgiving of hard water. Best as a harem with quiet dithers in a dimly lit, heavily decorated tank.
behavior: Harem breeder where females claim caves and the male patrols; needs very soft, acidic water to spawn.
dimorphism: Males are larger with extended lyre-shaped tails and stronger color; females are small and yellow when brooding.

description: An elegant Rio Uaupés dwarf cichlid whose males carry sweeping lyretails and filamentous dorsal extensions. A true blackwater fish needing very soft, strongly acidic water — one of the more demanding apistos to keep and breed. Reserve it for a dedicated soft-water setup with gentle tankmates and abundant cover.
behavior: Shy and harem-forming; will only thrive and spawn in soft, acidic, immaculate water.
dimorphism: Males are larger with extended lyre tails and dorsal filaments; females stay small and plain.

description: The aptly named 'sunset' apisto, its males glowing fiery red-orange with neon-blue cheeks and black-edged fins. Strikingly beautiful but notoriously hard to pair — individuals can be aggressive and compatible matches take patience. Wants soft, acidic, well-shaded water and small, peaceful dither fish to coax this shy fish into the open.
behavior: Can be quarrelsome and choosy about mates; shy without cover and dither fish to lead it out.
dimorphism: Males are larger and intensely red-orange; females are smaller and yellow, blackening when guarding eggs.

description: A larger Peruvian dwarf cichlid famous for its unusual biology: females practice larvophilic mouthbrooding, carrying the fry in their mouths — rare among Apistogramma. Comes from cool, clear, fast forest streams, so it likes slightly cooler, soft, acidic water. Keep a single pair in smaller tanks or a harem in larger ones, with plenty of caves and pipework.
behavior: Territorial when breeding; a facultative mouthbrooder that may carry fry or guard them in a substrate pit.
reproduction: Larvophilic mouthbrooder — the female (sometimes both parents) holds the fry in her mouth until they are free-swimming.
dimorphism: Males grow noticeably larger with extended fins; females are smaller and plainer.

description: A French Guiana dwarf cichlid whose dominant males develop a glowing gold head and a net-like pattern of dark scales over green flanks. Larger and longer than the familiar golden dwarf, but just as peaceful outside breeding. Wants soft, acidic water and a planted tank with caves; keep a single pair or one male with a couple of females.
behavior: Peaceful for a cichlid; the female becomes a fierce fry-guard, turning checkered and driving off intruders.
dimorphism: Males are much larger with a gold head and patterned body; females are small, plain, and checkered when brooding.

description: A spectacular blackwater dwarf cichlid (now placed in Nannacara) marked with bold black diagonal bars and a coppery face. Demanding and contradictory: it can be aggressive even toward its own mate, yet is shy and easily bullied, so it needs a long footprint, soft acidic water and no boisterous tankmates. A connoisseur's fish for a dedicated blackwater tank.
behavior: Pair aggression runs both ways, so a large footprint with sightline breaks is needed even for a single pair; shy in bright, open tanks.
dimorphism: Males grow larger with stronger barring and a brighter face; females are smaller.

description: A delicate checkerboard cichlid named for the spade-shaped tail of mature males, patterned with a double row of dark blotches and a blue-sheened head. Very peaceful toward other species but a true soft-water specialist, needing extremely acidic, tannin-stained water to do well. Best in a quiet blackwater tank with small dithers and fine leaf litter.
behavior: Gentle and slightly timid; thrives only in very soft, acidic water and dislikes brisk tankmates.
dimorphism: Males have the spade-shaped tail and blue head sheen; females are plainer and similar in size or slightly larger.

description: A slim, torpedo-shaped 'black stripe' dwarf cichlid with a bold lateral line and males that flare spade-shaped, neon-edged tails. Shy yet sharply territorial between males, so keep a single pair or a one-male harem with lots of cover and small, peaceful dithers. Soft, acidic, spotless water is essential for this fragile blackwater fish.
behavior: Males are intensely territorial with each other but the fish is otherwise shy; needs dense planting and hiding spots.
dimorphism: Males are larger with elongated, colorful spade tails; females stay small and yellowish.

description: A jewel-like blackwater dwarf cichlid, slender and silvery with iridescent gill-plate spangles, rarely seen outside specialist circles. Fragile and exacting: it needs very soft, warm, acidic water, a fine sand bed and deep leaf litter to feel secure. Largely non-territorial but easily intimidated, so house it only with tiny, gentle tankmates.
behavior: Picks tiny invertebrates from sand and leaf litter; peaceful but delicate and quick to stress in unstable water.
